```mermaid
    code --> step2["Step 2 Second digit 0 = Generic SAE code"]
    code --> step3["Step 3 Third digit 3 = Ignition system"]
    code --> step4["Step 4 Fourth digit 0 = Generic subgroup"]
    code --> step5["Step 5 Fifth digit 01 = Cylinder 1 misfire"]

    step1 --> answer[Diagnosis Misfire detected in Cylinder 1]
    step2 --> answer
    step3 --> answer
    step4 --> answer
    step5 --> answer
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks)
**`[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model Paper Set B]`**
**Q:** Define On-Board Diagnostics (OBD-II). List any **two** standardized features that differentiate OBD-II from OBD-I.

**Model Answer:**

> **Definition:** OBD-II is a standardized automotive electronic system that continuously monitors engine and emissions-related components, stores Diagnostic Trouble Codes (DTCs), and triggers the Malfunction Indicator Lamp (MIL) when a fault is detected.

**Two Standardized Features:**

1. **Universal 16-pin SAE J1962 connector** located within 610 mm of the steering wheel — replacing manufacturer-specific connectors.
2. **Standardized 5-character DTC format** (e.g., P0301) per SAE J2012 — replacing proprietary codes.
3. **Standardized communication protocols** (J1850, ISO 9141-2, KWP2000, CAN).
4. **Mandatory Freeze Frame & Readiness Flags.**

*(Any two well-explained points = full 3 marks)*

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks)
**`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`**
**Q:** Explain the function of the **Malfunction Indicator Lamp (MIL)** in an OBD-II system. Why is it governed by a **two-trip logic** for most emissions-related faults?

**Model Answer:**

**Function of MIL:**
The MIL, also called the "Check Engine" light, is the **driver-visible indicator** that one or more on-board monitors have detected a malfunction in the engine, fuel, ignition, or emissions system. It is driven by the ECU via a bulb-driver circuit in the instrument cluster.

**Two-Trip Logic Rationale:**
To prevent false alarms from transient sensor noise (e.g., momentary fuel splash on the O$_2$ sensor during cornering), the MIL is illuminated only after the same fault is detected on **two consecutive driving cycles**. The fault counter is reset to zero if the fault is not seen on the next cycle. This de-bouncing logic ensures only persistent, reproducible faults light the MIL.

*(3 marks: 1.5 for function + 1.5 for two-trip rationale)*

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Do NOT** write OBD-I pin assignments — the question is about **OBD-II** standardized pins. Mixing them costs **2 marks**.
> 2. Always state **pin numbers AND function** together when describing J1962 — writing only "CAN High" without "Pin 6" loses **1 mark**.
> 3. The DTC format MUST be written as **5 characters** in the order `X-Y-DD-FF` (e.g., P-0-30-1). Omitting the second digit loses **1 mark**.
> 4. The two-trip logic must mention **"two CONSECUTIVE driving cycles"** — missing the word "consecutive" loses **1 mark**.
> 5. Always end with a neat **labeled diagram** or **table** — examiners reward visual clarity with **1–2 bonus marks** even when content is average.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) — Choice Option A
**`[KTU University Exam - May 2023, Module 1, Set A]`**

**Q:** With a neat block diagram, explain the **architecture of the OBD-II system**. Discuss in detail:
**(a)** The SAE J1962 connector, its pinout, and the role of each pin. **(7 marks)**
**(b)** The SAE J1979 diagnostic modes of operation with examples of services provided. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) SAE J1962 Connector — Architecture & Pinout (7 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):**
The SAE J1962 Data Link Connector (DLC) is the standard 16-pin trapezoidal connector mandated for all OBD-II compliant vehicles. It must be located within **610 mm (24 inches)** of the steering wheel and accessible without tools.

**Pinout Table (4 marks):**

| Pin | Assignment | Protocol/Function |
|:---:|:-----------|:------------------|
| 2 | J1850 Bus+ | VPW / PWM |
| 4 | **Chassis Ground** | Mandatory |
| 5 | **Signal Ground** | Mandatory |
| 6 | CAN-High | ISO 15765-4 |
| 7 | K-Line | ISO 9141-2 / KWP2000 |
| 10 | J1850 Bus− | PWM only |
| 14 | CAN-Low | ISO 15765-4 |
| 15 | L-Line | ISO 9141-2 / KWP2000 |
| 16 | **+12 V Battery** | Mandatory, unswitched |

Pins 1, 3, 8, 9, 11, 12, 13 are **manufacturer reserved**.

**Role of Pins (2 marks):**
- Pins **4, 5, 16** form the **power & ground triad** (mandatory on every vehicle).
- Pin **2 (and 10)** carry the J1850 differential/VPW signal.
- Pins **6 & 14** form the **twisted-pair CAN bus** for ISO 15765 communication.
- Pins **7 & 15** are the **K and L lines** for ISO 9141 and KWP2000 protocols.
- The trapezoidal mechanical keying prevents reversed insertion.

---

**(b) SAE J1979 Modes of Operation (7 marks):**

**Introduction (1 mark):**
SAE J1979 (equivalent ISO 15031-5) defines **10 standardized diagnostic services** (Modes 0x01 through 0x0A) that any universal OBD-II scan tool can invoke across all compliant vehicles.

**Mode Table with examples (6 marks — 0.5 per mode, 1 mark for best examples):**

| Mode (Hex) | Service | Example Use |
|:----------:|:--------|:-----------:|
| 01 | Read Live Powertrain Data | Engine RPM, MAF, HO2S voltages |
| 02 | Read Freeze Frame Data | Snapshot at moment of fault |
| 03 | Read Stored DTCs | P0301, P0420 |
| 04 | Clear DTCs & MIL | Service reset |
| 05 | O$_2$ Sensor Test Results | Pre-CAN Mode 5 results |
| 06 | On-Board Monitor Test Results | Catalyst, EVAP live test |
| 07 | Read Pending DTCs | Single-trip pending codes |
| 08 | Bidirectional Control | Actuate EVAP purge valve |
| 09 | Read Vehicle Information | VIN, calibration ID, CVN |
| 0A | Read Permanent DTCs | Codes that cannot be cleared |

**Conclusion:**
The standardization of J1979 services ensures that a **single scan tool** can diagnose any OBD-II compliant vehicle regardless of make or model — a key commercial and regulatory triumph.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Choice Option B
**`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022, Module 1, Set B]`**

**Q:** Explain in detail the **Diagnostic Trouble Code (DTC) structure** as per SAE J2012. With the help of a suitable example, demonstrate the decoding process. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Describe the **OBD-II communication protocols** in detail with their respective standards, signal lines, and bit-rates. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) DTC Structure as per SAE J2012 (7 marks):**

**Standard (1 mark):**
SAE J2012 defines a **5-character alphanumeric code** of the form `XYDDFF`, where every character carries a precise meaning.

**Breakdown of Each Position (3 marks):**

| Position | Possible Values | Meaning |
|:--------:|:----------------|:--------|
| **X** (1st) | P / C / B / U | System: Powertrain, Chassis, Body, Network |
| **Y** (2nd) | 0, 1, 2, 3 | Code Type: Generic SAE / Manufacturer-specific |
| **DD** (3rd–4th) | 00–99 | Subsystem (e.g., 01 = Fuel/Air; 30 = Ignition; 42 = Catalyst) |
| **FF** (5th) | 00–99 | Specific fault index |

**Worked Example — Decoding P0420 (3 marks):**

| Char | Value | Decoded Meaning |
|:----:|:-----:|:----------------|
| P | System | Powertrain (engine + transmission) |
| 0 | Code Type | Generic (SAE) — same on every car |
| 4 | Subsystem (digit 1) | Auxiliary Emissions Controls |
| 2 | Subsystem (digit 2) | Catalyst |
| 0 | Fault Index | Below Threshold (Bank 1) |

**Final Diagnosis:** *"Catalyst System Efficiency Below Threshold (Bank 1)."* Mechanic would inspect HO2S-2 voltage swings, exhaust back-pressure, and catalytic converter integrity.

---

**(b) OBD-II Communication Protocols (7 marks):**

**Introduction (1 mark):**
SAE and ISO bodies standardized **five protocols** for OBD-II communication, ensuring any universal scan tool can interface with any compliant vehicle via the J1962 DLC.

**Protocol Details (6 marks — 1.2 marks each):**

| # | Protocol | Standard | Signal Pins | Bus Type | Bit-Rate |
|:-:|:---------|:--------:|:-----------:|:--------:|:--------:|
| 1 | SAE J1850 VPW | SAE J1850 | 2, 4, 5, 16 | Single-wire | **10.4 kbps** |
| 2 | SAE J1850 PWM | SAE J1850 | 2, 4, 5, 10, 16 | Two-wire diff | **41.6 kbps** |
| 3 | ISO 9141-2 | ISO | 4, 5, 7, 15, 16 | K + L line | **10.4 kbps** |
| 4 | ISO 14230-4 (KWP2000) | ISO | 4, 5, 7, 15, 16 | K + L line | **10.4 kbps** |
| 5 | ISO 15765-4 (CAN) | ISO 15765 | 4, 5, 6, 14, 16 | Twisted-pair diff | **500 kbps** |

**Conclusion:**
Since **2008 in the US** and **2007 in the EU**, all light-duty vehicles must use **CAN (ISO 15765-4)** as the OBD-II protocol. CAN provides high bandwidth (500 kbps), robust differential signaling immune to EMI, and built-in error detection (CRC, ACK) — making it the de-facto modern OBD-II language.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Pin this checklist to your study wall the night before the exam.**

- [x] **OBD-II = On-Board Diagnostics Generation 2**, mandated in **US 1996**, **EU 2001/2004**, **India 2020 (BS-VI)**.
- [x] **Connector**: **16-pin trapezoidal SAE J1962**, accessible within **610 mm** of steering wheel.
- [x] **Mandatory Pins**: **4 (Chassis GND), 5 (Signal GND), 16 (+12 V)**.
- [x] **DTC Format**: `P0301` style — **5 chars** = System + Type + Subsystem + Fault.
- [x] **First char** = P (Powertrain) / C (Chassis) / B (Body) / U (Network).
- [x] **Second char** = 0 (Generic SAE) or 1 (Manufacturer-specific).
- [x] **MIL logic = 2 consecutive trips** (40 warm-up cycles retained in KAM after fault clears).
- [x] **Protocols**: J1850 VPW (10.4 kbps, GM/Chrysler), J1850 PWM (41.6 kbps, Ford), ISO 9141-2 (10.4 kbps, EU/Asia), KWP2000 (10.4 kbps), CAN (500 kbps, all 2008+).
- [x] **CAN signal pins**: Pin **6 (CAN-High)** and Pin **14 (CAN-Low)**.
- [x] **KWP2000 / ISO 9141**: Pin **7 (K-Line)** and Pin **15 (L-Line)**.
- [x] **J1979 Modes**: **0x01–0x0A** — Live Data, Freeze Frame, Stored DTCs, Clear, O$_2$ test, Monitor results, Pending, Bidirectional, Vehicle Info, Permanent DTCs.
- [x] **Catalyst efficiency** $\eta_{cat} = 1 - (A_2 / A_1)$; threshold = **0.5** → P0420.
- [x] **Freeze Frame**: Engine parameter snapshot at the exact moment the fault was flagged.
- [x] **Readiness Flags**: Show whether each non-continuous monitor has completed its test cycle.
- [x] **Evolution**: OBD-I (proprietary) → OBD-II (universal) → OBD-III (telematics, proposed).
- [x] **OBD-II voltage thresholds**: Logic High ≥ 4.0 V, Logic Low ≤ 1.0 V; CAN recessive ≈ 2.5 V, dominant differential = 2.0 V.
- [x] **BS-VI in India** made OBD-II mandatory for all new vehicles from **1 April 2020**.
- [x] **P0420** = Catalyst Efficiency Below Threshold (most frequently tested OBD-II code in KTU papers).
- [x] **P0301** = Cylinder 1 Misfire (most common ignition code in practical diagnostics).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Vehicle Motion Control
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEICT721 - AUTOMOBILE INSTRUMENTATION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Vehicle Motion Control

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

**Vehicle Motion Control (VMC)** refers to the integrated set of electromechanical, electronic, and embedded control systems that actively regulate the longitudinal, lateral, and vertical dynamics of an automobile in order to enhance **safety, stability, comfort, fuel economy, and drivability**. It is a sub-domain of automotive mechatronics in which physical vehicle subsystems (throttle, brake, steering, suspension, drivetrain) are closed-loop regulated using real-time sensor feedback and deterministic control algorithms.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** Vehicle Motion Control is the centralized or distributed control of vehicle dynamics axes — *longitudinal motion (speed/acceleration)*, *lateral motion (steering/yaw)*, and *vertical motion (ride/load)* — implemented through **by-wire technologies** and real-time sensor-actuator feedback loops.

### 1.2 The Three Axes of Vehicle Motion

| Axis of Motion | Physical Quantity | Subsystem Involved | Primary Sensor(s) |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Longitudinal** | Forward speed, acceleration | Throttle, Brake, Drivetrain | Wheel speed, Accelerator pedal position |
| **Lateral** | Yaw, side-slip, lateral G | Steering, Differential brake | Yaw rate, Steering angle, Lateral accelerometer |
| **Vertical** | Body bounce, pitch, roll | Suspension, Damper | Ride-height, Accelerometer (vertical) |

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Horse and Reins" Model

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy for Intuition:** Think of a horse (the vehicle) and its rider (the driver). Without a rider, the horse runs uncontrolled — fast, slow, drifting, or stumbling. The rider uses the **reins** (steering), **legs** (brake), and **voice** (throttle) to command the horse, while feeling the **pull on the reins** and the **horse's body movement** as feedback.
> 
> In modern vehicles, the *rider* is replaced by an **Electronic Control Unit (ECU)**, the *reins* are replaced by **electronic signals over a CAN/LIN bus**, and the *feel of the horse* is replaced by a network of **sensors (wheel speed, yaw, lateral G)**. The whole feedback loop is what we call Vehicle Motion Control.

### 1.4 Why is Vehicle Motion Control Important?

- **Safety**: Prevents skidding, rollover, and collision through ABS, ESC, AEB.
- **Comfort**: Active suspension cancels road disturbances.
- **Efficiency**: Adaptive cruise control optimizes fuel consumption.
- **Emissions**: Coordinated throttle/drivetrain control reduces exhaust pollutants.
- **Driver Assistance**: Enables semi-autonomous and autonomous driving features (SAE Level 2+).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight for KTU Exams:** Always frame the answer around the *control loop*: *Sensor → ECU (Controller) → Actuator → Vehicle Body → New State → Sensor again*. Any VMC system is a closed-loop mechatronic system.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Architectural Layers of a VMC System

A Vehicle Motion Control system is structured in **four hierarchical layers**:

1. **Sensor Layer** — Acquires physical quantities:
   - Wheel speed sensors (Hall-effect / MR / inductive)
   - Steering angle sensor (potentiometric / optical encoder)
   - Yaw rate sensor (MEMS gyroscope)
   - Lateral & longitudinal accelerometers (MEMS capacitive)
   - Throttle pedal position sensor (TPS, dual redundant)

2. **Controller / ECU Layer** — Executes control law:
   - Dedicated microcontrollers (e.g., Infineon AURIX, NXP S32K)
   - Runs control algorithms (PID, State-Space, Sliding Mode, MPC)
   - Communication via **CAN (Controller Area Network) bus** at 500 kbps typical.

3. **Actuator Layer** — Converts electrical command into mechanical action:
   - Electronic Throttle Body (ETB) with DC motor + gear
   - Hydraulic / Electro-hydraulic brake modulator
   - Electric Power Steering (EPS) motor
   - Variable-geometry suspension damper

4. **Vehicle Body (Plant)** — The physical plant with non-linear dynamics.

### 2.2 Control Strategy Classes

- **Open-loop control:** No feedback (rare in VMC; used only in simple pre-pressurized brake systems).
- **Closed-loop feedback control:** The dominant paradigm. The ECU continuously measures *e(t) = r(t) − y(t)*, where *r(t)* is the desired state and *y(t)* is the measured state.
- **Feed-forward + Feedback:** Combines a predictive open-loop term (e.g., expected grade resistance) with a feedback correction (e.g., PID).
- **Multi-variable / MIMO control:** ESC and integrated chassis control couple longitudinal and lateral states.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> All equations below are **mandatory for KTU 2024 ESE** (End Semester Exam). Memorize the variable meanings and units.

| # | Formula / Law | Expression | Meaning | Units |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Newton's 2nd Law (Longitudinal) | $F_{\text{net}} = m \cdot a$ | Net tractive force produces linear acceleration | N, kg, m/s² |
| 2 | Longitudinal Force Balance | $F_t - F_{\text{roll}} - F_{\text{aero}} - F_{\text{grade}} = m \cdot a$ | Sum of tractive, rolling, aerodynamic, and grade forces | N |
| 3 | Rolling Resistance | $F_{\text{roll}} = \mu_r \cdot m \cdot g \cdot \cos\theta$ | Friction-style loss; $\mu_r \approx 0.012$ for car tire | N |
| 4 | Aerodynamic Drag | $F_{\text{aero}} = \tfrac{1}{2} \cdot \rho \cdot C_d \cdot A \cdot v^2$ | Drag force proportional to $v^2$ | N |
| 5 | Grade Resistance | $F_{\text{grade}} = m \cdot g \cdot \sin\theta$ | Component of weight along slope | N |
| 6 | Slip Ratio (ABS/TCS) | $\lambda = \dfrac{\vert v_{\text{wheel}} - v_{\text{vehicle}} \vert}{\max(v_{\text{wheel}}, v_{\text{vehicle}})}$ | Tire-road grip measure, $0 \le \lambda \le 1$ | dimensionless |
| 7 | Slip Ratio (Braking) | $\lambda_b = \dfrac{v_{\text{vehicle}} - v_{\text{wheel}}}{v_{\text{vehicle}}}$ | Positive during braking | dimensionless |
| 8 | Slip Ratio (Traction) | $\lambda_t = \dfrac{v_{\text{wheel}} - v_{\text{vehicle}}}{v_{\text{wheel}}}$ | Positive during acceleration spin | dimensionless |
| 9 | Yaw Rate (Bicycle Model) | $r = \dfrac{v \cdot \delta_f}{L}$ | Geometric yaw rate, $L$ = wheelbase, $\delta_f$ = front steer | rad/s |
| 10 | PID Control Law | $u(t) = K_p \, e(t) + K_i \int_0^t e(\tau)\,d\tau + K_d \,\dfrac{de(t)}{dt}$ | Cruise / throttle / brake controller output | V or % |
| 11 | Closed-Loop Transfer Function | $\dfrac{Y(s)}{R(s)} = \dfrac{G(s)\,C(s)}{1 + G(s)\,C(s)}$ | Standard unity negative feedback | dimensionless |
| 12 | Sensor Sampling Frequency | $f_s \ge 2 f_{\text{max}}$ (Nyquist) | Minimum sampling to avoid aliasing | Hz |
| 13 | CAN Bus Throughput | $T = \dfrac{8 \cdot N_{\text{bytes}}}{L_{\text{bit}}}$ | Frame latency, typical $L_{\text{bit}} = 2 \mu s$ @ 500 kbps | s |
| 14 | Wheel Deceleration Threshold (ABS) | $a_{\text{th}} = -1.5 g$ to $-2.0 g$ | Hard-lock trigger reference | m/s² |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL KTU TABLE RULE:** In the slip ratio formula, the absolute-value bars are written as $\vert v_{\text{wheel}} - v_{\text{vehicle}} \vert$ using the LaTeX `\vert` command. This avoids corrupting the markdown table column separator.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **ABS / ESC systems** are mandatory under EU GSR (General Safety Regulation) and equivalent Indian AIS-052 norms from **2022 onwards** for new passenger cars.
- Vehicle Motion Control is the **foundational enabler of ADAS** (Advanced Driver Assistance Systems): adaptive cruise control (ACC), lane keeping assist (LKA), automatic emergency braking (AEB), and ultimately autonomous driving (SAE Levels 3–5).
- In **Formula-1 and motorsport**, VMC is taken to its limits through Traction Control, Drag Reduction System (DRS), and active suspension (now banned in F1 since 2022 cost-cap era constraints).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation — Longitudinal Vehicle Dynamics Equation

We start from a free-body diagram of a vehicle moving up an inclined road of angle $\theta$ with velocity $v$ and acceleration $a$.

**Forces acting on the vehicle along the longitudinal axis:**

1. **Tractive force** at the tire-road contact patch: $F_t$ (N), produced by engine torque through the drivetrain.
2. **Rolling resistance force**: $F_{\text{roll}}$, opposing motion.
3. **Aerodynamic drag force**: $F_{\text{aero}}$, opposing motion.
4. **Grade resistance force**: $F_{\text{grade}}$, the gravitational component along the slope.
5. **Inertial force**: the mass times acceleration, $m \cdot a$ (by D'Alembert).

**Step 1 — Apply Newton's 2nd Law in the longitudinal direction:**

$$
\sum F_x = m \cdot a
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the four forces with their proper sign convention (resistive forces taken negative, tractive force positive):**

$$
F_t \; - \; F_{\text{roll}} \; - \; F_{\text{aero}} \; - \; F_{\text{grade}} \; = \; m \cdot a
$$

**Step 3 — Expand each term using its empirical form:**

$$
F_t \; - \; \mu_r \, m \, g \cos\theta \; - \; \tfrac{1}{2} \rho \, C_d \, A \, v^2 \; - \; m \, g \sin\theta \; = \; m \, a
$$

**Step 4 — Re-arrange to express acceleration as a function of velocity and grade:**

$$
a(v,\theta) \; = \; \frac{F_t}{m} \; - \; \mu_r \, g \cos\theta \; - \; \frac{\rho \, C_d \, A}{2 m} \, v^2 \; - \; g \sin\theta
$$

**Step 5 — Interpretation of each term:**

- $\dfrac{F_t}{m}$ → **commanded** acceleration (the VMC system's *control input*).
- $\mu_r \, g \cos\theta$ → **rolling loss deceleration** (typically 0.10 – 0.15 m/s² for a passenger car).
- $\dfrac{\rho C_d A}{2m} \, v^2$ → **aero loss deceleration** (dominates above 80 km/h; doubles for every $\sqrt{2}$× speed).
- $g \sin\theta$ → **grade deceleration** (positive $\theta$ = uphill penalty, negative $\theta$ = downhill assist).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** A typical 14-mark derivation question will ask: *"Derive the longitudinal vehicle dynamics equation for a vehicle climbing a gradient of angle $\theta$."* Always include the free-body step (Step 1) explicitly — it carries 2 marks on its own.

### 3.2 Derivation — Slip Ratio Under Braking (ABS Application)

**Step 1 — Define wheel linear velocity:**
The wheel's ground-contact linear velocity (assuming no slip) is:
$$
v_{\text{wheel}} \; = \; \omega \cdot r_{\text{eff}}
$$
where $\omega$ is the wheel angular velocity (rad/s) and $r_{\text{eff}}$ is the effective rolling radius (m).

**Step 2 — Define vehicle body velocity:**
$v_{\text{vehicle}}$ is measured by the chassis-mounted longitudinal accelerometer integrated over time, or estimated via a non-driven (free) wheel.

**Step 3 — Express the slip ratio under braking:**
When the brake pedal is applied harder than the tire-road friction can sustain, $v_{\text{wheel}} < v_{\text{vehicle}}$, and slip is defined as:

$$
\lambda_b \; = \; \frac{v_{\text{vehicle}} - v_{\text{wheel}}}{v_{\text{vehicle}}}
$$

**Step 4 — Boundary cases:**

- $\lambda_b = 0$ → free rolling, no slip.
- $0 < \lambda_b < 0.15$ → stable region, longitudinal force rises linearly.
- $0.15 \le \lambda_b \le 0.30$ → **optimal slip range** for maximum braking force.
- $\lambda_b \to 1.0$ → wheel lock, lateral grip collapses, vehicle skids.

**Step 5 — ABS Control Objective:**
The ABS ECU aims to keep $\lambda_b$ in the $0.08$ to $0.30$ range by rapidly modulating brake pressure (typically 15–25 cycles/second).

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — VMC Subsystems at a Glance

| Subsystem | Axis Controlled | Primary Sensor | Primary Actuator | Controller Frequency | Key Industry Name |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Cruise Control (CC)** | Longitudinal | Vehicle speed | Electronic Throttle | 10–50 Hz | Bosch, Continental |
| **Adaptive Cruise Control (ACC)** | Longitudinal | Radar + Camera + Speed | Throttle + Brake | 20–100 Hz | Mobileye, Bosch |
| **ABS** | Longitudinal | Wheel speed (4×) | Hydraulic modulator | 25–50 Hz | Bosch ABS 9 |
| **TCS / ASR** | Longitudinal | Wheel speed + Throttle | Engine + Brake | 20–50 Hz | Continental MBD |
| **ESC / ESP / VSC** | Lateral + Longitudinal | Yaw + Steering + Lateral G | Selective wheel brake | 50–100 Hz | Bosch ESP 10 |
| **EPS** | Lateral (assist) | Torque + Steering angle | Electric motor | 500 Hz | JTEKT, Nexteer |
| **Active Suspension** | Vertical | Body + Wheel accelerometer | Variable damper | 100–200 Hz | Bilstein, ZF |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 High-Level Block Diagram of a Generic Vehicle Motion Control Loop

The following Mermaid diagram illustrates the **closed-loop architecture** common to all VMC systems: a desired set-point is compared with a measured state, the error drives a controller, the controller commands an actuator, and the actuator acts on the vehicle body (the plant).

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Driver Input OR ADAS Setpoint] --> B[Reference Signal r of t]
    B --> C[Comparator Summing Junction]
    D[Sensor Cluster on Vehicle Body] --> E[Measured State y of t]
    E --> C
    C --> F[Error e of t equals r minus y]
    F --> G[ECU Controller PID or MPC]
    G --> H[Actuator Command u of t]
    H --> I[By Wire Actuator Throttle Brake Steer]
    I --> J[Vehicle Body Plant Non Linear Dynamics]
    J --> K[New Vehicle State Longitudinal Lateral Vertical]
    K --> D
    style A fill:#f9e79f,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style G fill:#aed6f1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style I fill:#f5b7b1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style J fill:#d5f5e3,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

### 4.2 Cruise Control — Detailed Functional Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Set Speed Switch ON] --> B[Setpoint Speed Vref kmph]
    B --> C[Comparator]
    D[Wheel Speed Sensor] --> E[Vehicle Speed Estimator]
    E --> F[Measured Speed Vactual kmph]
    F --> C
    C --> G[Error ek equals Vref minus Vactual]
    G --> H[PID Controller with Anti Windup]
    H --> I[Throttle Actuator Command Percent]
    I --> J[Electronic Throttle Body DC Motor Drive]
    J --> K[Throttle Plate Angle Opening]
    K --> L[Engine Torque Output]
    L --> M[Drivetrain and Wheels]
    M --> N[Vehicle Acceleration and Speed]
    N --> D
    style H fill:#aed6f1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style J fill:#f5b7b1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style L fill:#d5f5e3,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

### 4.3 Antilock Braking System (ABS) — Block-Level Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Brake Pedal Force] --> B[Master Cylinder Pressure]
    B --> C[ABS Hydraulic Modulator 4 Channels]
    D[Wheel Speed Sensor Front Left] --> E[Wheel Speed Sensor Front Right]
    E --> F[Wheel Speed Sensor Rear Left]
    F --> G[Wheel Speed Sensor Rear Right]
    G --> H[Slip Ratio Calculator]
    H --> I[ABS ECU Reference Slip equals 0.15 to 0.20]
    I --> J[Pressure Increase Hold Decrease Logic]
    J --> K[Solenoid Valves in Modulator]
    K --> L[Wheel Cylinder Pressure]
    L --> M[Brake Torque and Deceleration]
    M --> N[Wheel Deceleration Feedback]
    N --> H
    style I fill:#aed6f1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style C fill:#f5b7b1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style L fill:#d5f5e3,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Block Diagrams Matter in KTU:** Almost every 14-mark question in OEICT721 Module 1 includes an *"Explain with block diagram"* sub-part. Memorize the canonical loop: *Reference → Comparator → Controller → Actuator → Plant → Sensor → Comparator*. You can re-draw this skeleton for CC, ABS, TCS, ESC, and EPS with only minor component substitutions.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Define Vehicle Motion Control. List any two sensors and two actuators used in modern VMC systems.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **(1 mark)** Vehicle Motion Control is the closed-loop electronic regulation of a vehicle's longitudinal, lateral, and vertical dynamics using sensors, ECUs, and actuators to ensure safety, stability, and comfort.
- **(1 mark)** Sensors: wheel speed sensor, yaw rate sensor (or steering angle sensor, lateral accelerometer).
- **(1 mark)** Actuators: electronic throttle body, hydraulic brake modulator (or EPS motor, active damper).

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*What is the slip ratio in ABS? State the optimal slip ratio range for maximum braking force.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **(1 mark)** Slip ratio under braking is defined as $\lambda_b = \dfrac{v_{\text{vehicle}} - v_{\text{wheel}}}{v_{\text{vehicle}}}$.
- **(1 mark)** It quantifies the relative motion between the tire contact patch and the road surface.
- **(1 mark)** The optimal slip ratio for maximum longitudinal braking force is **0.15 ≤ λ_b ≤ 0.30** (peaking near λ = 0.20 on dry asphalt).

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU ESE Pattern:** Each Part-B question carries 14 marks, divided into sub-parts (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks. You must attempt **one full question** out of the internal choice pair.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**Q.A.(a) [7 Marks] — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, adapted]**

*With a neat block diagram, explain the operation of an Electronic Cruise Control System. List the major components and state the role of the PID controller in maintaining set speed.*

**Model Solution:**

1. **(2 marks) Block Diagram:** Draw the closed-loop architecture — *Set Switch → Setpoint → Comparator → PID ECU → Throttle Actuator → Engine → Vehicle → Wheel Speed Sensor → Comparator*. (Refer to Section 4.2 of these notes.)
2. **(1 mark)** Components: set/resume switches, ECU, electronic throttle body (DC motor + position sensor), wheel speed sensor, brake switch (override), clutch switch (manual transmission).
3. **(1 mark)** Operation: when the driver activates cruise, the current vehicle speed is latched as $V_{\text{ref}}$. The ECU measures actual speed $V_{\text{act}}$ via wheel-speed sensors.
4. **(1 mark)** Error signal $e(t) = V_{\text{ref}} - V_{\text{act}}$ is fed to the PID controller.
5. **(1 mark)** The PID controller computes throttle opening command using:
$$
u(t) = K_p \, e(t) + K_i \int_0^t e(\tau)\,d\tau + K_d \frac{de(t)}{dt}
$$
   - $K_p$ → proportional response (immediate correction).
   - $K_i$ → integral term (eliminates steady-state error due to grade/wind).
   - $K_d$ → derivative term (damps overshoot during downhill/uphill transients).
6. **(1 mark)** Brake-pedal depression or clutch depression immediately disengages cruise for safety (hardware override).

**Q.A.(b) [7 Marks] — [KTU University Exam – May 2024, adapted]**

*Derive the longitudinal vehicle dynamics equation for a car of mass $m$ climbing a gradient $\theta$, including tractive force, rolling resistance, aerodynamic drag, and grade resistance. Identify which term dominates at (i) 30 km/h and (ii) 120 km/h.*

**Model Solution:**

1. **(2 marks)** Free-body diagram step: $\sum F_x = m \cdot a$, with four forces — $F_t$ (forward), $F_{\text{roll}}$, $F_{\text{aero}}$, $F_{\text{grade}}$ (all backward).
2. **(2 marks)** Empirical substitution of each force as shown in Section 3.1, arriving at:
$$
a = \frac{F_t}{m} - \mu_r g \cos\theta - \frac{\rho C_d A}{2m} v^2 - g \sin\theta
$$
3. **(1 mark)** Units verification: each term has units of m/s².
4. **(1 mark)** At **30 km/h (≈ 8.33 m/s)**:
   - $F_{\text{roll}} = 0.012 \times 1500 \times 9.81 \times 1 = 176 \text{ N} \Rightarrow 0.12 \text{ m/s}^2$ loss
   - $F_{\text{aero}} = 0.5 \times 1.2 \times 0.3 \times 2.2 \times 69.4 = 27.5 \text{ N} \Rightarrow 0.018 \text{ m/s}^2$ loss
   - **Dominant term → Rolling resistance** ($F_{\text{roll}}$).
5. **(1 mark)** At **120 km/h (≈ 33.33 m/s)**:
   - $F_{\text{roll}} = 0.012 \times 1500 \times 9.81 \times 1 = 176 \text{ N} \Rightarrow 0.12 \text{ m/s}^2$ loss
   - $F_{\text{aero}} = 0.5 \times 1.2 \times 0.3 \times 2.2 \times 1111 = 440 \text{ N} \Rightarrow 0.29 \text{ m/s}^2$ loss
   - **Dominant term → Aerodynamic drag** ($F_{\text{aero}}$), since it scales as $v^2$.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**Q.B.(a) [7 Marks] — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**

*Explain the working principle of an Antilock Braking System (ABS) with a block diagram. Define slip ratio and explain how the ECU maintains the optimal slip range.*

**Model Solution:**

1. **(2 marks)** Block diagram: 4 wheel speed sensors → slip ratio calculator → comparator (with reference 0.15–0.20) → ECU → solenoid valves → hydraulic modulator → wheel cylinders. (Refer to Section 4.3.)
2. **(1 mark)** Slip ratio definition (braking): $\lambda_b = (v_{\text{vehicle}} - v_{\text{wheel}}) / v_{\text{vehicle}}$.
3. **(1 mark)** Optimal range: $0.15 \le \lambda_b \le 0.30$ — point of peak friction coefficient $\mu$.
4. **(1 mark)** The ECU runs a **cycle of increase–hold–decrease** on brake pressure at 15–25 Hz, using PWM-controlled solenoid valves.
5. **(1 mark)** If $\lambda_b < 0.08$ → increase pressure (no lock risk). If $0.08 \le \lambda_b \le 0.25$ → hold. If $\lambda_b > 0.25$ → release pressure to allow wheel re-spin.
6. **(1 mark)** Net effect: vehicle retains steering control (lateral grip intact) while achieving ~80–90% of maximum possible deceleration, especially on low-μ surfaces (wet, snow).

**Q.B.(b) [7 Marks] — [KTU University Exam – May 2023, adapted]**

*Compare Traction Control System (TCS) and Electronic Stability Control (ESC). State the sensors and actuators unique to each system and explain the role of the yaw-rate sensor in ESC.*

**Model Solution:**

1. **(2 marks) Comparison Table:**

| Aspect | TCS | ESC |
|---|---|---|
| Axis controlled | Longitudinal (acceleration spin) | Lateral + longitudinal (yaw instability) |
| Trigger condition | Drive wheel slip during acceleration | Yaw rate deviates from steering demand |
| Primary action | Reduce engine torque / apply brake to spinning wheel | Brake individual wheels + reduce torque |
| Sensors | Wheel speed, throttle position | Yaw rate, steering angle, lateral G, wheel speed |
| Typical use | Launch on slippery road, hill start | Sharp turn, evasive lane change, slippery corner |

2. **(1 mark)** TCS-specific: throttle position sensor, engine torque request signal.
3. **(1 mark)** ESC-specific: yaw rate sensor (MEMS gyroscope, ±100 °/s range, ~0.1 °/s resolution), steering angle sensor (optical encoder, ±720°, 0.1° resolution), lateral accelerometer (±1.5–2 g).
4. **(1 mark)** Yaw rate sensor role: measures actual rotational velocity of vehicle about its vertical axis. Compared with the *desired* yaw rate computed from steering angle and vehicle speed (using bicycle model $r_{\text{desired}} = v \cdot \delta_f / L$).
5. **(1 mark)** If $r_{\text{actual}} > r_{\text{desired}}$ → oversteer (rear sliding out) → brake the *outer front* wheel.
6. **(1 mark)** If $r_{\text{actual}} < r_{\text{desired}}$ → understeer (plowing wide) → brake the *inner rear* wheel. This selective braking generates a counter-yaw moment that restores stability within 100–200 ms.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks**
> 
> 1. **Forgetting the feedback loop in the block diagram** (–2 marks). A block diagram without an arrow returning from the sensor to the comparator is marked *open-loop*, which is structurally wrong for VMC.
> 2. **Mixing up slip ratio sign conventions** (–1 mark). Under *braking*, $v_{\text{wheel}} < v_{\text{vehicle}}$, so $\lambda_b$ is **positive** when defined as $(v_{\text{vehicle}} - v_{\text{wheel}})/v_{\text{vehicle}}$.
> 3. **Confusing ABS, TCS, ESC** (–1 to –2 marks). ABS prevents *wheel lock*; TCS prevents *wheel spin*; ESC prevents *yaw instability*. These are three distinct systems — never interchange their objectives.
> 4. **Skipping the free-body step in derivations** (–2 marks). Always start with $\sum F = ma$ before substituting empirical formulas.
> 5. **Forgetting units** in numerical sub-parts (–1 mark). Always state N, m/s², rad/s explicitly.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **VMC = closed-loop mechatronic regulation** of longitudinal, lateral, and vertical vehicle dynamics.
- The **canonical control loop** is: *Setpoint → Comparator → ECU → Actuator → Plant → Sensor → Comparator*. Draw this for any VMC sub-question.
- **Newton's 2nd Law** is the foundation of all vehicle dynamics: $F_t - F_{\text{roll}} - F_{\text{aero}} - F_{\text{grade}} = m \cdot a$.
- **Rolling resistance** dominates at low speeds; **aerodynamic drag** (∝ $v^2$) dominates at high speeds.
- **Slip ratio** quantifies tire-road grip loss; **optimal range is 0.15–0.30** for braking.
- **ABS** = prevents wheel lock (longitudinal, brake-only).
- **TCS** = prevents wheel spin (longitudinal, engine + brake).
- **ESC** = prevents yaw instability (lateral + longitudinal, selective wheel brake).
- **Sensors to memorize**: wheel speed, steering angle, yaw rate, lateral accelerometer, throttle pedal position, brake pressure.
- **Actuators to memorize**: electronic throttle body, hydraulic brake modulator, EPS motor, active damper.
- **Communication bus**: CAN (500 kbps typical), with sub-buses like LIN for low-speed sensor clusters.
- **Key Industry Names**: Bosch ESP 10, Continental MBD, ZF Active Suspension, Mobileye ACC.
- **For derivations**: always show free-body, then $\sum F = ma$, then empirical substitution, then final acceleration form.
- **For block diagrams**: never use markdown inside Mermaid node labels; use plain uppercase text only.
- **For numerical questions**: 1500 kg, $C_d A = 0.66$ m², $\mu_r = 0.012$, $\rho = 1.2$ kg/m³ are typical default values to assume unless stated.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Digital Cruise Control
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEICT721 - AUTOMOBILE INSTRUMENTATION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Digital Cruise Control

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Digital Cruise Control (DCC)** — also termed *Automatic Cruise Control (ACC-base)* or *Electronic Speed Control (ESC)* — is a **closed-loop, feedback-based electronic driver-assistance system** that automatically regulates the longitudinal velocity of a road vehicle to a driver-prescribed *set-point speed*, by modulating the throttle valve actuation angle (and selectively the brake actuator in advanced variants) without continuous manual pedal input from the driver.

Mathematically, it is a real-time implementation of a **SISO (Single-Input Single-Output) negative feedback controller**, where:

$$e(t) = v_{set} - v_{actual}(t)$$

is the speed-error signal continuously fed to an embedded **Electronic Control Unit (ECU)** running a digital control algorithm (commonly PID, State-Space, or Fuzzy Logic).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight — Distinction to remember:**
> - **Cruise Control (CC)** → maintains *driver-set* speed only.
> - **Adaptive Cruise Control (ACC)** → CC + radar/lidar-based *headway* (distance) maintenance.
> - **Predictive Cruise Control (PCC)** → CC + GPS/topographic *look-ahead* fuel optimization.

---

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine you are riding a bicycle on a flat road and want to maintain exactly **20 km/h**. You don't have a speedometer, but you can *feel* the wind. To keep speed constant:

1. You set a *mental target* (20 km/h).
2. You continuously sense (feel + look) your current speed.
3. If you feel you're going *slower* than target → you **push the pedals harder** (increase throttle).
4. If you feel you're going *faster* → you **stop pedaling** (decrease throttle).
5. This loop happens *many times per second*.

**Digital Cruise Control does exactly this — but for your car — using a microcontroller as the "brain," a wheel-speed sensor as the "feeling," and an electronic throttle as the "pedal muscle."**

> [!NOTE]
> The word **"Digital"** here emphasizes that the control algorithm executes in a *microprocessor/microcontroller domain* (sampled, discrete-time signals), as opposed to early *analog* (op-amp-based) vacuum-operated systems used in 1958–1970s Chrysler, Lincoln, and Mercedes vehicles.

---

## 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Engineering Metrics

| Parameter | Symbol | Standard Value / Unit |
|---|---|---|
| Standard highway cruise speed (typical) | $v_{set}$ | **80 – 120 km/h** |
| Set-point resolution | $\Delta v$ | **± 1 km/h** |
| Vehicle mass (mid-size sedan) | $m$ | **1200 – 1800 kg** |
| Air density (sea level, 25 °C) | $\rho$ | **1.225 kg/m³** |
| Gravitational acceleration | $g$ | **9.81 m/s²** |
| Sampling period of digital ECU | $T_s$ | **10 – 50 ms** |
| Aerodynamic drag coefficient (typical car) | $C_d$ | **0.25 – 0.40** |

---

## 1.4 Historical Context (For 2-Mark Sub-Part Questions)

| Year | Milestone |
|---|---|
| **1948** | First mechanical "Speedostat" by Ralph Teetor (blind inventor) |
| **1958** | Chrysler *Imperial* — first production *analog* cruise control |
| **1973** | Electronic (analog op-amp based) CC introduced |
| **1980s** | Digital ECU-based CC becomes industry standard |
| **1995** | First *Adaptive* Cruise Control (ACC) — Mitsubishi Diamante (radar-based) |
| **2010s+** | Integration into ADAS suites (Tesla Autopilot, Toyota Safety Sense) |

> [!TIP]
> In KTU viva/exams, mentioning **Ralph Teetor** as the inventor of cruise control often earns a quick ½ mark appreciation point from examiners.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 System Architecture — Functional Decomposition

A Digital Cruise Control system is built from **six (6) primary functional blocks**:

1. **Driver Interface Module (HMI)**
   - Set (SET) button
   - Resume (RES) button
   - Coast / Decelerate button
   - Cancel / Brake-override switch
   - Accelerate button
   - Dashboard indicator lamp

2. **Speed / Velocity Sensing Subsystem**
   - **Wheel Speed Sensors** (Hall-effect or magneto-resistive) at non-driven wheels
   - Transmission Output Shaft Speed (OSS) sensor
   - GPS-based cross-validation (in modern variants)
   - Signal conditioning → frequency-to-voltage conversion → digital pulses to ECU

3. **Electronic Control Unit (ECU) — The "Brain"**
   - 16-bit / 32-bit microcontroller (e.g., Infineon TriCore, NXP S32K, Renesas RH850)
   - Runs the control algorithm (PID, Fuzzy, or Model Predictive)
   - Sampling period $T_s$ typically 10–50 ms
   - Failure detection (OBD-II compliant)

4. **Actuation Subsystem — Electronic Throttle Body (ETB)**
   - **Drive-by-wire** (DBW) throttle — replaces mechanical cable
   - Two DC motors (for redundancy) driving a gear-train to rotate throttle plate
   - Two Throttle Position Sensors (TPS) for closed-loop angle verification
   - Fail-safe spring returns throttle to idle position on ECU failure

5. **Brake-Override Subsystem (Modern DCC)**
   - Deactivates CC immediately upon brake pedal actuation
   - Uses brake light switch + master-cylinder pressure sensor

6. **Display & Telemetry Subsystem**
   - Digital instrument cluster (set speed indication, "CRUISE" icon)
   - CAN/LIN bus communication between ECU and HMI

---

## 2.2 Principle of Operation — Step-by-Step Logic

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 — Most Frequently Asked 7-Mark Question:** "Explain the working principle of a Digital Cruise Control System with a neat block diagram."

**Step 1 — Initialization:**
- Driver accelerates manually to desired speed using accelerator pedal.
- Driver presses the **SET** button → ECU samples current vehicle speed $v_{actual}$ and latches it as $v_{set}$ in non-volatile RAM.

**Step 2 — Error Generation:**
- At every sampling instant $t = kT_s$, ECU computes the **speed error**:
  $$e(k) = v_{set} - v_{actual}(k)$$

**Step 3 — Control Law Computation (Digital PID):**
$$u(k) = K_p e(k) + K_i \sum_{j=0}^{k} e(j) T_s + K_d \frac{e(k) - e(k-1)}{T_s}$$

where:
- $K_p$ = Proportional gain (instantaneous response)
- $K_i$ = Integral gain (eliminates steady-state error)
- $K_d$ = Derivative gain (dampens oscillation)
- $u(k)$ = commanded throttle position (% of full opening)

**Step 4 — Actuation:**
- ECU drives the ETB motor via PWM signals to achieve the commanded throttle angle.

**Step 5 — Vehicle Response:**
- Throttle opening → engine torque → tractive force → vehicle acceleration
- Vehicle dynamics: $m \frac{dv}{dt} = F_{trac} - F_{drag} - F_{roll} - F_{grade}$

**Step 6 — Feedback:**
- New wheel speed is sensed, loop repeats at frequency $1/T_s$.

**Step 7 — Override / Safety:**
- Brake pedal press → ECU zeroes $u(k)$ immediately
- Cancel button → exits CC mode but retains $v_{set}$ in memory
- Resume button → restores previous $v_{set}$ and re-engages control

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> Memorize this table verbatim — it covers 80% of numerical problems asked in KTU ESE on this topic.

| # | Equation / Formula | Physical Meaning | Typical Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $e(t) = v_{set} - v_{actual}(t)$ | Speed error signal | m/s or km/h |
| 2 | $m \dfrac{dv}{dt} = F_{trac} - F_{drag} - F_{roll} - F_{grade}$ | Newton's 2nd Law (longitudinal) | N (after multiplying by m) |
| 3 | $F_{trac} = \dfrac{\eta_t \cdot T_{eng} \cdot i_g \cdot i_0}{r_{wheel}}$ | Tractive force at tire contact patch | N |
| 4 | $F_{drag} = \dfrac{1}{2} \rho C_d A_f v^2$ | Aerodynamic drag force | N |
| 5 | $F_{roll} = \mu_{rr} \cdot m g \cos\theta$ | Rolling resistance | N |
| 6 | $F_{grade} = m g \sin\theta$ | Grade resistance (uphill) | N |
| 7 | $u(t) = K_p e(t) + K_i \int_0^t e(\tau) d\tau + K_d \dfrac{de(t)}{dt}$ | Continuous-time PID control law | % throttle |
| 8 | $u(k) = K_p e(k) + K_i T_s \sum e(j) + K_d \dfrac{e(k)-e(k-1)}{T_s}$ | Discrete-time PID (positional) | % throttle |
| 9 | $\Delta u(k) = K_p [e(k)-e(k-1)] + K_i T_s e(k) + K_d \dfrac{e(k)-2e(k-1)+e(k-2)}{T_s}$ | Discrete-time PID (incremental / velocity form) | Δ% throttle |
| 10 | $G_{PID}(s) = K_p + \dfrac{K_i}{s} + K_d s$ | PID transfer function (Laplace) | dimensionless |
| 11 | $G_{plant}(s) = \dfrac{K_v}{\tau_v s + 1}$ | First-order vehicle velocity dynamics | m/s per % |
| 12 | $\omega_n = \sqrt{\dfrac{K_p K_v}{\tau_v}}$, $\zeta = \dfrac{\tau_v \omega_n}{2}$ | Natural frequency & damping ratio (closed-loop) | rad/s, dimensionless |
| 13 | $T_s = \dfrac{1}{f_{sample}}$ | Sampling period relationship | seconds |
| 14 | $e_{ss} = \lim_{s \to 0} s \cdot E(s)$ | Final Value Theorem (steady-state error) | m/s |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common pitfall:** In Formula 7, do NOT confuse $K_i$ (integral gain) with $K_i$ (integral coefficient). In most KTU textbooks, $K_i$ is the gain multiplier applied to the *integrated* error, i.e., $\int e\,dt$, NOT the integral time constant $T_i$. The two are related by $K_i = K_p / T_i$.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Fuel Economy:** Maintaining steady speed eliminates the fuel-wasting acceleration-deceleration cycles of human driving. Studies show **7–14% fuel savings** on highway drives.
- **Driver Comfort:** Reduces fatigue on long highway journeys (>100 km).
- **Emission Control:** Steady-state combustion → cleaner exhaust (lower HC and CO).
- **Safety Foundation:** Forms the algorithmic core of modern ACC, AEB (Autonomous Emergency Braking), and highway autopilot systems.
- **Legal Compliance:** Required for Level 2+ ADAS vehicles in EU (GSR 2, 2022+).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Vehicle Longitudinal Dynamics Equation

**Starting Point — Newton's Second Law along the longitudinal axis:**

The net longitudinal force on a vehicle equals mass times longitudinal acceleration:

$$F_{net} = m \cdot a_x = m \cdot \frac{dv}{dt}$$

**The four opposing/propelling forces on a vehicle moving on a road with gradient angle $\theta$ are:**

1. Tractive force from engine $F_{trac}$ (drives the vehicle forward)
2. Aerodynamic drag $F_{drag}$ (opposes motion)
3. Rolling resistance $F_{roll}$ (opposes motion)
4. Grade force $F_{grade}$ (opposes motion if uphill, assists if downhill)

**Applying force balance along x-axis:**

$$m \frac{dv}{dt} = F_{trac} - F_{drag} - F_{roll} \pm F_{grade}$$

**Substituting the standard constitutive equations:**

$$F_{trac} = \frac{T_{eng} \cdot \eta_t \cdot i_g \cdot i_0}{r_{wheel}}$$

where:
- $T_{eng}$ = Engine torque (N·m)
- $\eta_t$ = Drivetrain efficiency (typically 0.85 – 0.92)
- $i_g$ = Transmission gear ratio
- $i_0$ = Final drive (differential) ratio
- $r_{wheel}$ = Effective tire radius (m)

$$F_{drag} = \frac{1}{2} \rho C_d A_f v^2$$

$$F_{roll} = \mu_{rr} \cdot m g \cos\theta$$

$$F_{grade} = m g \sin\theta$$

**Combining (assuming flat road, $\theta = 0$):**

$$m \frac{dv}{dt} = \frac{T_{eng} \cdot \eta_t \cdot i_g \cdot i_0}{r_{wheel}} - \frac{1}{2} \rho C_d A_f v^2 - \mu_{rr} \cdot m g$$

> This is the **fundamental governing ODE of vehicle longitudinal motion** used in almost all DCC simulations.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Linearized Plant Transfer Function

**Assumption for control design:** Around an equilibrium cruise speed $v_0$, treat the plant as a *first-order linear system*.

At steady-state equilibrium:

$$0 = F_{trac,0} - \frac{1}{2} \rho C_d A_f v_0^2 - \mu_{rr} m g$$

Perturbing each variable by a small amount $x = x_0 + \tilde{x}$:

$$m \frac{d\tilde{v}}{dt} = \tilde{F}_{trac} - \rho C_d A_f v_0 \cdot \tilde{v}$$

(Rolling resistance is approximately constant and absorbed into the equilibrium.)

**Rearranging into standard first-order form:**

$$\frac{m}{\rho C_d A_f v_0} \frac{d\tilde{v}}{dt} + \tilde{v} = \frac{1}{\rho C_d A_f v_0} \tilde{F}_{trac}$$

**Define plant parameters:**

$$\tau_v = \frac{m}{\rho C_d A_f v_0} \quad \text{(vehicle time constant, seconds)}$$

$$K_v = \frac{1}{\rho C_d A_f v_0} \quad \text{(velocity DC gain, m/s per N)}$$

**The linearized plant transfer function (Throttle force → Velocity):**

$$G_{plant}(s) = \frac{\tilde{V}(s)}{\tilde{F}_{trac}(s)} = \frac{K_v}{\tau_v s + 1}$$

**Numerical Example for a typical sedan cruising at $v_0 = 30$ m/s (108 km/h):**

- $m = 1500$ kg
- $\rho = 1.225$ kg/m³
- $C_d = 0.30$
- $A_f = 2.2$ m²

$$\tau_v = \frac{1500}{1.225 \times 0.30 \times 2.2 \times 30} = \frac{1500}{24.255} \approx 61.85 \text{ s}$$

$$K_v = \frac{1}{24.255} \approx 0.0412 \text{ m/s per N}$$

> [!NOTE]
> The large $\tau_v$ (~62 s) confirms the vehicle is a *slow plant*. A properly designed PID must have $T_s \ll \tau_v$ (typically $T_s = 0.02$ s, satisfying Nyquist comfortably).

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Closed-Loop Characteristic Equation

The forward path of the closed-loop DCC system comprises:
- PID controller: $G_c(s) = K_p + \dfrac{K_i}{s} + K_d s$
- Plant: $G_p(s) = \dfrac{K_v}{\tau_v s + 1}$

Assuming unity feedback ($H(s) = 1$):

$$G_{open}(s) = G_c(s) \cdot G_p(s) = \left(K_p + \frac{K_i}{s} + K_d s\right) \cdot \frac{K_v}{\tau_v s + 1}$$

**Closed-loop transfer function:**

$$T(s) = \frac{G_{open}(s)}{1 + G_{open}(s)}$$

**Characteristic equation (denominator set to zero):**

$$1 + G_{open}(s) = 0$$

$$\Rightarrow s(\tau_v s + 1) + K_v(K_p s + K_i + K_d s^2) = 0$$

**Expanding:**

$$\tau_v s^2 + s + K_v K_d s^2 + K_v K_p s + K_v K_i = 0$$

**Grouping by powers of $s$:**

$$(\tau_v + K_v K_d) s^2 + (1 + K_v K_p) s + K_v K_i = 0$$

**Comparing with standard 2nd-order form $s^2 + 2\zeta\omega_n s + \omega_n^2 = 0$:**

$$\omega_n = \sqrt{\frac{K_v K_i}{\tau_v + K_v K_d}}$$

$$\zeta = \frac{1 + K_v K_p}{2 \sqrt{K_v K_i (\tau_v + K_v K_d)}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Design Rule-of-Thumb:** For comfortable automotive cruise response, target $\zeta \approx 0.7$ (slightly underdamped) and $\omega_n \approx 0.3 - 1.0$ rad/s. These yield settling times of 3–10 seconds with minimal overshoot — matching human driver expectations.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Steady-State Error Analysis (Hill Climbing)

**Scenario:** Vehicle is cruising on a flat road at $v_{set}$ using PID control. It encounters a +3% grade. Will speed drop?

**Approach:** Apply the Final Value Theorem to the error transfer function:

$$E(s) = \frac{R(s)}{1 + G_c(s) G_p(s)}$$

For a step disturbance (e.g., entering a grade), $R(s) = \dfrac{v_{set}}{s}$:

$$e_{ss} = \lim_{s \to 0} s \cdot E(s) = \lim_{s \to 0} \frac{s \cdot v_{set}/s}{1 + G_c(s) G_p(s)} = \frac{v_{set}}{1 + G_c(0) G_p(0)}$$

**With PID, $G_c(0) \to \infty$ (integrator term), so:**

$$e_{ss} = 0$$

> This is the **fundamental advantage of integral action**: a P-only controller would have a *non-zero* speed droop on hills, but a PI or PID controller **eliminates it completely in steady state**.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner trap:** Many students answer "PI control" alone is sufficient for DCC. While true for steady-state, **derivative action is added to improve transient response** (hill entry transient overshoot/oscillation). The correct full answer is *PID is the industry default*.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Summary of Key Derivations

| Derivation # | Title | Core Result | Use in DCC Design |
|---|---|---|---|
| 3.1 | Vehicle dynamics ODE | $m \dot{v} = F_{trac} - F_{drag} - F_{roll}$ | Plant model |
| 3.2 | Linearized TF | $G_p(s) = K_v / (\tau_v s + 1)$ | Controller design input |
| 3.3 | Closed-loop poles | $\omega_n, \zeta$ from K gains | Tuning guidance |
| 3.4 | Steady-state error | $e_{ss} = 0$ with integral term | Justifies PID choice |
