# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📘 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> *[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper Style]*

### **Q1. Define Digital Cruise Control. List any four driver input controls associated with it.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1.5 marks):** Digital Cruise Control is an electronic driver-assistance system that automatically maintains a vehicle at a driver-selected set speed by continuously regulating the throttle opening through a closed-loop feedback control algorithm executed by an on-board ECU, thereby eliminating the need for continuous accelerator pedal input.

**Four driver controls (1.5 marks — 0.375 each):**
1. **SET** — Latches the current speed as the reference set-point.
2. **RES (Resume)** — Restores the previously stored set-point.
3. **COAST** — Gradually decreases set speed.
4. **CANCEL** — Disengages CC without erasing the stored set-point.

> [!NOTE]
> Other acceptable controls: ACCEL (tap-up), brake-pedal override, OFF switch.

---

### **Q2. Distinguish between Cruise Control (CC), Adaptive Cruise Control (ACC), and Predictive Cruise Control (PCC).**

**Model Answer (3 marks — 1 mark per row):**

| Feature | CC | ACC | PCC |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Maintains set speed?** | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes |
| **Maintains safe distance to lead vehicle?** | ❌ No | ✅ Yes (via radar) | ✅ Yes (via radar) |
| **Uses GPS / map / topography?** | ❌ No | ❌ Usually no | ✅ Yes — anticipates hills & curves |
| **Primary goal** | Driver comfort | Safety + comfort | **Fuel economy** + safety |
| **Sensor suite** | Wheel speed only | Wheel speed + radar/lidar | All ACC sensors + GPS + map DB |

---

## 📗 PART B — Essay Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> **INTERNAL CHOICE INSTRUCTION:** Answer **either** Question A **or** Question B in full.

---

### **🅰️ Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Adapted]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the working principle of a **Digital Cruise Control system** in a modern automobile. Identify all major sub-systems and clearly indicate the feedback path. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the **vehicle longitudinal dynamics equation** starting from Newton's Second Law. Clearly state the meaning of every term and the typical units. Show how the equation is **linearized** around an equilibrium cruise speed $v_0$ to obtain a first-order plant transfer function. **(7 marks)**

---

#### ⭐ MODEL SOLUTION — Question A

**Part (a) — Working Principle (7 marks):**

**Block diagram (3 marks):**
- Draw the diagram from Section 4.1 of these notes.
- Label: Set switch → ECU RAM → Summing junction → Digital PID → Throttle actuator → Vehicle plant → Wheel sensor → Feedback to summing junction.
- Use clear arrows for signal flow; mark feedback with a minus sign at the summing junction.

**Working explanation (4 marks — 1 mark per key point):**
1. **Set phase:** Driver accelerates manually to the desired cruise speed and presses the SET switch. The ECU samples the current wheel-speed-sensor pulses, computes $v_{actual}$ via $v = 2\pi r N / (60 P)$ where $N$ = pulses per second and $P$ = pulses per revolution, and stores it as $v_{set}$ in non-volatile memory. **(1 mark)**
2. **Error generation:** At each sampling instant $t = kT_s$, the ECU computes $e(k) = v_{set} - v_{actual}(k)$. **(1 mark)**
3. **Control law:** The PID algorithm computes the required throttle opening $u(k) = K_p e(k) + K_i T_s \sum e(j) + K_d [e(k)-e(k-1)]/T_s$. **(1 mark)**
4. **Actuation & safety:** ECU drives the electronic throttle body (ETB) via a PWM-controlled DC motor. A brake-pedal switch or CAN message immediately overrides $u(k)$ to zero for safety. **(1 mark)**

---

**Part (b) — Vehicle Dynamics Derivation (7 marks):**

**Step 1 (2 marks):** State the force balance on the vehicle:

$$m \frac{dv}{dt} = F_{trac} - F_{drag} - F_{roll} - F_{grade}$$

**Step 2 (2 marks):** Substitute the four force expressions:

$$m \frac{dv}{dt} = \frac{T_{eng} \eta_t i_g i_0}{r_{wheel}} - \frac{1}{2} \rho C_d A_f v^2 - \mu_{rr} m g \cos\theta - m g \sin\theta$$

**Step 3 (1 mark):** For a flat road ($\theta = 0$) and small perturbations, take $\cos\theta \approx 1$, $\sin\theta = 0$:

$$m \frac{dv}{dt} = \frac{T_{eng} \eta_t i_g i_0}{r_{wheel}} - \frac{1}{2} \rho C_d A_f v^2 - \mu_{rr} m g$$

**Step 4 (2 marks):** Linearize around $v_0$. Let $v = v_0 + \tilde{v}$, $F = F_0 + \tilde{F}$:

$$m \frac{d\tilde{v}}{dt} = \tilde{F}_{trac} - \rho C_d A_f v_0 \tilde{v}$$

Taking Laplace transform:

$$(m s + \rho C_d A_f v_0) \tilde{V}(s) = \tilde{F}_{trac}(s)$$

$$G_p(s) = \frac{\tilde{V}(s)}{\tilde{F}_{trac}(s)} = \frac{1/(\rho C_d A_f v_0)}{[m/(\rho C_d A_f v_0)] s + 1} = \frac{K_v}{\tau_v s + 1}$$

where $\tau_v = m/(\rho C_d A_f v_0)$ and $K_v = 1/(\rho C_d A_f v_0)$. **QED.**

---

### **🅱️ Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**(a)** Explain the **Discrete-time PID control algorithm** used in Digital Cruise Control. Write the **positional form** and the **incremental (velocity) form** of the algorithm. State the role of each of the three gains $K_p$, $K_i$, $K_d$ with one practical effect each. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A vehicle of mass **1500 kg** cruises at **$v_0 = 30$ m/s** on a flat road. Air density $\rho = 1.225$ kg/m³, $C_d = 0.30$, $A_f = 2.2$ m², $\mu_{rr} = 0.012$. Compute the **plant time constant** $\tau_v$ and **DC gain** $K_v$. If a PI controller is to be designed for a desired natural frequency $\omega_n = 0.5$ rad/s and damping ratio $\zeta = 0.7$, determine the **required values of $K_p$ and $K_i$**. **(7 marks)**

---

#### ⭐ MODEL SOLUTION — Question B

**Part (a) — Discrete PID Algorithm (7 marks):**

**Positional form (2.5 marks):**
$$u(k) = K_p e(k) + K_i T_s \sum_{j=0}^{k} e(j) + K_d \frac{e(k) - e(k-1)}{T_s}$$

- $u(k)$: Throttle command at step $k$ (in %).
- $e(k)$: Speed error at step $k$.
- $K_p e(k)$: Proportional term — produces an immediate throttle correction proportional to current error.
- $K_i T_s \sum e(j)$: Integral term — accumulates past errors to eliminate steady-state error (crucial for hill climbing).
- $K_d [e(k)-e(k-1)]/T_s$: Derivative term — predicts future error trend; damps oscillations.

**Incremental form (2.5 marks):**
$$\Delta u(k) = K_p [e(k) - e(k-1)] + K_i T_s e(k) + \frac{K_d}{T_s} [e(k) - 2e(k-1) + e(k-2)]$$

$$u(k) = u(k-1) + \Delta u(k)$$

**Advantages of incremental form (1 mark):** Bumpless transfer during mode changes; anti-windup is easier; protects against actuator saturation by computing $\Delta u$ rather than absolute $u$.

**Roles of gains (1 mark — 0.33 each):**
- $K_p$ ↑ → faster response, but more overshoot and oscillation.
- $K_i$ ↑ → faster steady-state error elimination, but risk of integrator windup.
- $K_d$ ↑ → better damping and stability, but amplifies sensor noise.

---

**Part (b) — Numerical Design (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Plant parameters (2 marks):**
$$\tau_v = \frac{m}{\rho C_d A_f v_0} = \frac{1500}{1.225 \times 0.30 \times 2.2 \times 30}$$

Computing denominator: $1.225 \times 0.30 = 0.3675$; $\times 2.2 = 0.8085$; $\times 30 = 24.255$

$$\tau_v = \frac{1500}{24.255} = 61.84 \text{ s}$$

$$K_v = \frac{1}{24.255} = 0.04123 \text{ m/s per N}$$

**Step 2 — Assume $K_d = 0$ for PI design (1 mark):**

The closed-loop characteristic equation reduces to:

$$(\tau_v) s^2 + (1 + K_v K_p) s + K_v K_i = 0$$

**Step 3 — Compare with standard form (2 marks):**
$$\omega_n^2 = \frac{K_v K_i}{\tau_v} \quad \Rightarrow \quad K_i = \frac{\omega_n^2 \tau_v}{K_v}$$

$$2\zeta\omega_n = \frac{1 + K_v K_p}{\tau_v} \quad \Rightarrow \quad K_p = \frac{2\zeta\omega_n \tau_v - 1}{K_v}$$

**Step 4 — Substitute numbers (2 marks):**
$$K_i = \frac{(0.5)^2 \times 61.84}{0.04123} = \frac{0.25 \times 61.84}{0.04123} = \frac{15.46}{0.04123} \approx 375.0$$

$$K_p = \frac{2 \times 0.7 \times 0.5 \times 61.84 - 1}{0.04123} = \frac{43.288 - 1}{0.04123} = \frac{42.288}{0.04123} \approx 1025.7$$

**Final Answer:** $K_p \approx 1026$, $K_i \approx 375$, $K_d = 0$ (PI controller).

> [!WARNING]
> **⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Unit Inconsistency:** Mixing m/s and km/h without conversion (1 m/s = 3.6 km/h) — costs 0.5–1 mark.
> 2. **Forgetting $K_d$ assumption:** If asked for PI, must explicitly state $K_d = 0$. Many students leave it ambiguous.
> 3. **Skipping the Laplace step:** Writing the linearized ODE but not converting to $s$-domain loses 0.5 mark.
> 4. **Wrong Damping Ratio Formula:** Using $1/(2\zeta\omega_n)$ instead of $2\zeta\omega_n$ is a very common error.
> 5. **Ignoring Units in Final Answer:** Always append units (e.g., $K_p$ is dimensionless, $K_i$ has units of $s^{-1}$).
> 6. **Not drawing arrows on block diagram:** A block diagram without signal-flow arrows gets only 1.5/3 marks.

---

## 🎯 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID-REVISION CHECKLIST

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Read this section **on the morning of the exam** for a 5-minute high-yield review.

### ✅ Must-Remember Definitions
- **Digital Cruise Control** = Closed-loop feedback speed regulator.
- **Adaptive Cruise Control (ACC)** = CC + headway control via radar/lidar.
- **Predictive Cruise Control (PCC)** = CC + GPS/topographic look-ahead.
- **Drive-by-Wire (DBW)** = Electronic throttle replacing mechanical cable.
- **SET/RES/COAST/CANCEL** = Four primary driver commands.

### ✅ Must-Remember Equations
- Error: $e(k) = v_{set} - v_{actual}(k)$
- Longitudinal dynamics: $m\dot{v} = F_{trac} - F_{drag} - F_{roll} - F_{grade}$
- Drag: $F_{drag} = \tfrac{1}{2} \rho C_d A_f v^2$
- Plant TF: $G_p(s) = K_v/(\tau_v s + 1)$
- PID (positional): $u(k) = K_p e(k) + K_i T_s \sum e(j) + K_d [e(k)-e(k-1)]/T_s$
- Natural frequency: $\omega_n = \sqrt{K_v K_i / (\tau_v + K_v K_d)}$
- Damping ratio: $\zeta = (1 + K_v K_p)/[2\sqrt{K_v K_i(\tau_v + K_v K_d)}]$

### ✅ Must-Remember Numbers
- $g = 9.81$ m/s²
- $\rho = 1.225$ kg/m³ (sea level, 25 °C)
- Typical $C_d$ (sedan) = 0.25 – 0.40
- Typical $A_f$ = 2.0 – 2.5 m²
- Cruise set-speed range = 30 – 200 km/h
- Sampling period $T_s$ = 10 – 50 ms
- Target $\zeta \approx 0.7$ for comfortable ride

### ✅ Must-Remember Facts
- Inventor: **Ralph Teetor (1948)**.
- First production car: **1958 Chrysler Imperial** (analog).
- First production ACC: **1995 Mitsubishi Diamante**.
- Six functional blocks: HMI, Sensors, ECU, Actuator, Brake Override, Display.
- Why PID? — P gives speed, I eliminates steady-state error, D damps oscillation.
- Why electronic throttle (ETB)? — Mechanical cable cannot be modulated by ECU in real time.

### ✅ Must-Remember Safety Features
1. Brake-pedal override (immediate CC deactivation).
2. CANCEL switch.
3. Neutral gear detection.
4. Clutch pedal detection (manual transmission).
5. Watchdog timer (ECU software health check).
6. Redundant throttle position sensors (TPS1, TPS2).
7. Fail-safe spring returns throttle to idle on power loss.

### ✅ Common Exam Traps to Avoid
- ❌ Calling it "Automatic Speed Control" without saying "Closed-Loop" → loses 0.5 mark.
- ❌ Drawing CC block diagram without the feedback arrow.
- ❌ Forgetting to convert km/h to m/s in numericals.
- ❌ Confusing $K_i$ (gain) with $T_i$ (integral time).
- ❌ Writing $u = K_p e + K_i \int e$ only (missing $K_d$ part) when PID is asked.
- ❌ Not mentioning integrator anti-windup (a 2024 favorite 2-mark sub-question).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Current Trends in Modern Automobiles
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEICT721 - AUTOMOBILE INSTRUMENTATION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Current Trends in Modern Automobiles

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 1 — CURRENT TRENDS IN MODERN AUTOMOBILES

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Modern Automobile Trends** refer to the rapidly evolving technological, electronic, and environmental paradigms that define the contemporary automotive industry. These trends integrate **electrification**, **automation**, **connectivity**, and **sustainable mobility** into a unified vehicle architecture, fundamentally transforming the conventional Internal Combustion Engine (ICE) vehicle into a sophisticated, software-defined, and intelligent transportation system.

According to the **KTU 2024 Scheme OEICT721 syllabus (Module 1)**, current trends encompass the convergence of:
- **Electrification** (BEV, HEV, PHEV, FCEV)
- **Autonomy** (ADAS, Level 0–5 automation per SAE J3016)
- **Connectivity** (IoT, V2X, 5G, OTA updates)
- **Shared Mobility** (MaaS, ride-hailing, subscription models)
- **Sustainable Materials & Manufacturing** (lightweight composites, 3D printing)

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Smartphone Evolution" of Cars

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy:** Think of an automobile's journey the same way mobile phones evolved from the basic Nokia 1100 (purely mechanical ICE cars) to today's iPhone 15 Pro (software-defined, connected EVs).

| Era | Mobile Phone Analogy | Automobile Equivalent |
|---|---|---|
| **1990s** | Basic feature phones (calls/SMS only) | Pure mechanical ICE vehicles |
| **2000s** | Camera, music, basic apps | Fuel injection, ABS, basic ECU |
| **2010s** | Smartphones (apps, GPS, internet) | Connected cars, ADAS, infotainment |
| **2020s+** | AI-powered foldables with cloud sync | Autonomous EVs with OTA & V2X |

Just as a smartphone is no longer just a "phone" but a **mini-computer**, a modern automobile is no longer just a "vehicle" but a **rolling data center on wheels**.

---

### 1.3 The Four Pillars of Modern Automotive Trends (ACES Framework)

The global automotive industry uses the **"ACES"** framework to categorize current trends:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The ACES Megatrend:**
> 1. **A** — Autonomous Driving
> 2. **C** — Connected Vehicles
> 3. **E** — Electrification
> 4. **S** — Shared & Smart Mobility

---

### 1.4 Key Performance Metrics (Bolded Standards)

- **SAE J3016** — Standard for driving automation levels (Level 0 to Level 5)
- **WLTP** — Worldwide Harmonised Light Vehicles Test Procedure (range & emission measurement)
- **ISO 26262** — Functional safety standard for automotive electronic systems
- **UNECE R155 / R156** — Cyber security and software update regulations
- **Euro 7 / BS-VII** — Latest emission norms (target: **< 0.1 g/km NOx**)
- **Bharat Stage VI (BS6)** — India emission standard equivalent to Euro 6
- **0–100 km/h in under 2 seconds** — modern EV hypercar benchmark
- **Battery specific energy** target: **> 400 Wh/kg** (solid-state battery goal)

---

### 1.5 Why This Topic Matters in KTU Examinations

> [!NOTE]
> This is a **high-frequency introductory question** in KTU ESE (End Semester Examination) for OEICT721. Examiners typically award 3–7 marks for direct conceptual questions and 14 marks for descriptive comparative analyses (e.g., "Compare EV vs ICE vehicles"). A solid grasp of trends, terminologies, and basic comparative metrics is essential.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Trend 1 — Electrification of Vehicles

Electrification refers to replacing the conventional gasoline/diesel powertrain with electric or hybrid powertrains. The four main categories are:

### 2.1.1 Battery Electric Vehicle (BEV)
- **Definition:** A vehicle powered solely by an electric motor fed from an on-board rechargeable battery.
- **Key Components:** Battery pack (Li-ion / Solid-state), Inverter, Electric motor (PMSM / Induction), On-board charger (OBC), DC-DC converter, Thermal Management System.
- **No Tailpipe Emissions.**
- **Examples:** Tesla Model 3, Tata Nexon EV, BYD Atto 3.

### 2.1.2 Hybrid Electric Vehicle (HEV)
- **Definition:** Combines an ICE engine with an electric motor, but **cannot be externally plugged in**. Battery is charged via regenerative braking and the engine itself.
- **Types:** Micro-hybrid (start-stop only), Mild hybrid (48V), Full hybrid (e.g., Toyota Prius).
- **Fuel Savings:** Typically **20–35%** over conventional ICE.

### 2.1.3 Plug-in Hybrid Electric Vehicle (PHEV)
- **Definition:** Larger battery (typically **8–20 kWh**) that can be charged from an external source. Offers **30–80 km** of pure electric range.
- **Examples:** Toyota Prius Prime, Volvo XC90 T8.

### 2.1.4 Fuel Cell Electric Vehicle (FCEV)
- **Definition:** Generates electricity on-board via a **Proton Exchange Membrane (PEM) fuel cell** using **compressed hydrogen (H₂)**.
- **By-product:** Only **water vapor** and warm air.
- **Refuelling time:** ~**5 minutes** (vs. 30–60 min for BEV fast charging).
- **Examples:** Toyota Mirai, Hyundai Nexo.

---

## 2.2 Trend 2 — Autonomous Driving (Self-Driving Cars)

Based on **SAE J3016** standard, automation is classified into **6 levels (0 to 5)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **SAE J3016 Automation Levels (High-Yield for KTU):**
> - **Level 0:** No automation. Driver does everything. (e.g., old Maruti 800)
> - **Level 1:** Driver Assistance — Adaptive Cruise Control OR Lane Keeping (single function). (e.g., Honda City)
> - **Level 2:** Partial Automation — ACC + Lane Centering simultaneously. (e.g., Tesla Autopilot, MG Astor)
> - **Level 3:** Conditional Automation — Car drives itself in specific conditions; driver must be ready to intervene. (e.g., Audi A8 Traffic Jam Pilot)
> - **Level 4:** High Automation — Fully autonomous in geo-fenced areas. (e.g., Waymo robotaxis in Phoenix, USA)
> - **Level 5:** Full Automation — No steering wheel, no human intervention needed. (Still in R&D)

---

## 2.3 Trend 3 — Connected Vehicles (V2X, IoT, 5G)

Connected vehicle technology enables communication between the car and its surroundings:

| Acronym | Full Form | Communicates With |
|---|---|---|
| **V2V** | Vehicle-to-Vehicle | Other cars |
| **V2I** | Vehicle-to-Infrastructure | Traffic lights, road signs |
| **V2P** | Vehicle-to-Pedestrian | Smartphones of pedestrians |
| **V2N** | Vehicle-to-Network | Cloud servers (5G) |
| **V2G** | Vehicle-to-Grid | Power grid (bidirectional EV charging) |
| **V2X** | Vehicle-to-Everything | All of the above |

> [!NOTE]
> **5G-C-V2X (Cellular V2X)** uses the **5.9 GHz** band (in most countries) with latency under **5 ms**, enabling real-time collision avoidance.

---

## 2.4 Trend 4 — Advanced Driver Assistance Systems (ADAS)

ADAS is the suite of electronic features that assist the driver. **It is the stepping stone to full autonomy.**

| ADAS Feature | Function |
|---|---|
| **AEB** — Automatic Emergency Braking | Applies brakes to prevent collision |
| **ACC** — Adaptive Cruise Control | Maintains distance from vehicle ahead |
| **LKA** — Lane Keeping Assist | Steers back into lane |
| **BSD** — Blind Spot Detection | Warns of vehicles in blind spot |
| **FCW** — Forward Collision Warning | Alerts driver of imminent crash |
| **LDW** — Lane Departure Warning | Audible warning on lane drift |
| **TSR** — Traffic Sign Recognition | Reads speed limit signs via camera |
| **NV** — Night Vision | Infrared camera detects pedestrians/animals |
| **PA** — Park Assist | Auto-parallel/perpendicular parking |

---

## 2.5 Trend 5 — Shared & Smart Mobility (MaaS)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mobility as a Service (MaaS)** integrates various transport services (taxi, bus, metro, EV rental) into a single on-demand digital platform, accessible via a smartphone app. Examples: **Ola, Uber, BluSmart, Namma Yatri**.

- **Ride-hailing** — On-demand driver service (Uber, Ola).
- **Car-sharing** — Short-term self-drive rental (Zoomcar, Drivezy).
- **Subscription model** — Monthly flat fee for a car (Hyundai Subscribe, Revv).
- **Robotaxi** — Fully autonomous taxi (Waymo, Cruise, Tesla Cybercab).

---

## 2.6 Trend 6 — Lightweight & Sustainable Materials

- **High-Strength Steel (HSS)** — Up to **25%** mass reduction.
- **Aluminium Alloys (Al-Si)** — Used in engine blocks, body panels.
- **Carbon Fibre Reinforced Polymer (CFRP)** — Used in BMW i3, i8 chassis.
- **Magnesium alloys** — **36% lighter** than aluminium; used in steering wheels, seat frames.
- **Natural Fibre Composites** — Hemp, flax, jute (eco-friendly interior trims).
- **Recycled plastics** — PET bottles into seat fabrics.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why lightweight?** Reducing mass by **100 kg** improves fuel economy by approximately **0.4 L/100 km** in ICE vehicles and extends EV range by **~6–10 km**.

---

## 2.7 Trend 7 — 3D Printing (Additive Manufacturing) in Automobiles

- Used for **prototyping**, **custom jigs**, and increasingly for **end-parts**.
- **Examples:** Bugatti Chiron brake caliper (3D printed titanium), Local Motors Strata (first 3D-printed car).
- **Material:** Nylon (PA12), ABS, TPU, photopolymer resins, metal powders.

---

## 2.8 Trend 8 — Alternative Fuels (Beyond Petrol/Diesel)

| Fuel | Formula | CO₂ Saving vs Petrol |
|---|---|---|
| **CNG** | CH₄ (compressed) | ~20–25% |
| **LNG** | CH₄ (liquefied, -162°C) | ~15–20% |
| **Ethanol (E85, E100)** | C₂H₅OH | 30–70% (depending on blend) |
| **Biodiesel (B100)** | FAME from vegetable oil | ~50–80% |
| **Hydrogen (H₂)** | H₂ (PEM fuel cell) | ~100% tailpipe |
| **Synthetic e-fuels** | Made from captured CO₂ + H₂O | Up to 85% |

---

## 2.9 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memorize this table — it covers ~80% of numericals asked in KTU ESE for this topic.**

| S.No | Concept | Formula / Equation | Variables & Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Battery Energy Stored | $E = V_{nom} \times Q$ | V in Volts, Q in Ah, E in Wh | $1 \text{ kWh} = 1000 \text{ Wh}$ |
| 2 | Battery Energy (alt.) | $E = V \times I \times t$ | V, I (A), t (h) | Used when discharge time known |
| 3 | Specific Energy | $E_{sp} = E / m_{bat}$ | Wh/kg | Higher = lighter battery |
| 4 | Energy Density (volumetric) | $E_{vol} = E / V_{bat}$ | Wh/L | Important for packaging |
| 5 | EV Driving Range | $R = (E_{bat} \times \eta_{sys}) / C_{100}$ | km | $C_{100}$ = consumption in kWh/100 km |
| 6 | Vehicle Power Required | $P = F \times v$ | P in W, F in N, v in m/s | Includes drag + rolling + climbing |
| 7 | Rolling Resistance Force | $F_{r} = \mu_{r} \times m \times g$ | N | $\mu_r$ ≈ 0.008–0.015 for car tyres |
| 8 | Aerodynamic Drag Force | $F_{d} = 0.5 \times \rho \times C_{d} \times A \times v^{2}$ | N | ρ = 1.225 kg/m³ (air density) |
| 9 | Climbing Force | $F_{c} = m \times g \times \sin(\theta)$ | N | θ = road slope angle |
| 10 | Total Traction Force | $F_{t} = F_{r} + F_{d} + F_{c}$ | N | Sum of resistive forces |
| 11 | Motor Power Output | $P_{motor} = T \times \omega$ | T (Nm), ω (rad/s) | $P(kW) = T(Nm) \times N(rpm) / 9550$ |
| 12 | State of Charge (SoC) | $SoC = (Q_{rem} / Q_{full}) \times 100$ | % | $Q_{rem}$ = remaining Ah |
| 13 | State of Health (SoH) | $SoH = (E_{max,current} / E_{max,new}) \times 100$ | % | Battery degradation metric |
| 14 | Charging Time | $t = (C \times SoC_{req}) / (I_{charge} \times 1000)$ | hours | C in Ah, I in A |
| 15 | Cost of Ownership/km | $CoC = (Fuel/Elec Cost) / Range$ | ₹/km | Critical for consumer analysis |
| 16 | CO₂ Emission (ICE) | $m_{CO_2} = m_{fuel} \times 2.31$ | kg | 2.31 = stoichiometric ratio for petrol |
| 17 | Regenerative Braking Efficiency | $\eta_{regen} = E_{recov} / E_{kinetic}$ | % | Typically 60–80% |
| 18 | Kinetic Energy of Vehicle | $E_{k} = 0.5 \times m \times v^{2}$ | Joules | Used for regen calculations |

> [!WARNING]
> **CRITICAL MARKDOWN RULE:** Never use the vertical pipe `\|` inside formula expressions in tables. Use `\vert` or `\mid` instead. Example: write $a \mid b$ not $a \vert b$ in plain text. This prevents the markdown parser from breaking your table layout.

---

## 2.10 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **OEM Design Engineers** use these formulas for powertrain sizing.
- **Battery Management System (BMS)** firmware uses SoC & SoH equations.
- **ADAS algorithms** compute $F_t$ and braking distance in real time.
- **Fleet operators** (Ola, Uber, BluSmart) optimize routes using EV range equations.
- **Emission regulators** apply CO₂ formulas to certify BS-VI / Euro 7 vehicles.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation 1 — EV Driving Range from Battery Parameters

### Problem Statement
> A Tata Nexon EV Long Range has a **40.5 kWh** battery pack. The vehicle consumes an average of **14.5 kWh per 100 km** under WLTP test conditions. The system efficiency (inverter + motor + transmission) is **88%**. Calculate the **effective driving range** in km.

---

### Step 1: Identify the Given Parameters

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{bat} &= 40.5 \text{ kWh} \\
C_{100} &= 14.5 \text{ kWh} / 100 \text{ km} \\
\eta_{sys} &= 0.88
\end{aligned}
$$

**Logic:** The total chemical energy in the battery is $E_{bat}$, but only a fraction of it (governed by $\eta_{sys}$) reaches the wheels as mechanical work. Hence, the *usable* energy is reduced.

---

### Step 2: Apply the Usable Energy Formula

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{usable} &= E_{bat} \times \eta_{sys} \\
E_{usable} &= 40.5 \times 0.88 \\
E_{usable} &= 35.64 \text{ kWh}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Logic:** Multiply to find how many kWh of mechanical energy are actually delivered to the tyres. The remaining $4.86 \text{ kWh}$ is lost as heat in cables, inverter switching losses, motor copper losses, and gearbox friction.

---

### Step 3: Apply the Driving Range Formula

$$
\begin{aligned}
R &= \frac{E_{usable}}{C_{100}} \times 100 \\
R &= \frac{35.64}{14.5} \times 100 \\
R &= 2.4579 \times 100 \\
R &\approx 245.79 \text{ km}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

### Step 4: Final Answer with Units

$$
\boxed{R \approx 246 \text{ km}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> This matches the **WLTP-claimed range of 437 km** for the Nexon EV Long Range when *not* accounting for the 88% system efficiency drop. The discrepancy shows why KTU examiners love this type of question — it tests whether students understand that *battery capacity ≠ usable range*.

---

## 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation 2 — Total Traction Force on a Climbing Vehicle

### Problem Statement
> A **1,500 kg** electric SUV is travelling at **60 km/h** up a **6°** gradient. Given: $\mu_r = 0.012$, $C_d = 0.30$, $A = 2.4 \text{ m}^2$, $\rho_{air} = 1.225 \text{ kg/m}^3$, $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$. Calculate the **total traction force** required.

---

### Step 1: Convert Speed to SI Units

$$
\begin{aligned}
v &= 60 \text{ km/h} \\
v &= 60 \times \frac{1000}{3600} \\
v &= 16.667 \text{ m/s}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Logic:** All formulas are in SI units, so we must convert km/h → m/s by the factor $\frac{1000}{3600} = \frac{5}{18}$.

---

### Step 2: Calculate the Rolling Resistance Force $F_r$

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_r &= \mu_r \times m \times g \\
F_r &= 0.012 \times 1500 \times 9.81 \\
F_r &= 176.58 \text{ N}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Logic:** Rolling resistance is the friction between tyre and road. The coefficient $\mu_r$ is multiplied by the normal force ($m \cdot g$) to get the resistive force.

---

### Step 3: Calculate the Aerodynamic Drag Force $F_d$

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_d &= 0.5 \times \rho \times C_d \times A \times v^2 \\
F_d &= 0.5 \times 1.225 \times 0.30 \times 2.4 \times (16.667)^2 \\
F_d &= 0.4410 \times 277.78 \\
F_d &\approx 122.50 \text{ N}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Logic:** Drag grows with the **square of speed**, so a vehicle at 120 km/h experiences 4× the drag of one at 60 km/h. This is why high-speed cruising drains EV batteries quickly.

---

### Step 4: Calculate the Climbing Force $F_c$

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_c &= m \times g \times \sin(\theta) \\
F_c &= 1500 \times 9.81 \times \sin(6^\circ) \\
F_c &= 14715 \times 0.10453 \\
F_c &\approx 1538.16 \text{ N}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Logic:** On an incline, the component of gravity acting along the slope opposes motion. The trigonometric identity $\sin(\theta)$ extracts this component.

---

### Step 5: Sum All Three Forces to Get $F_t$

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_t &= F_r + F_d + F_c \\
F_t &= 176.58 + 122.50 + 1538.16 \\
F_t &\approx 1837.24 \text{ N}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

### Step 6: Compute Required Motor Power

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{req} &= F_t \times v \\
P_{req} &= 1837.24 \times 16.667 \\
P_{req} &\approx 30,621 \text{ W} \\
P_{req} &\approx 30.6 \text{ kW}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

### Step 7: Final Answer

$$
\boxed{F_t \approx 1.84 \text{ kN} \quad ; \quad P_{req} \approx 30.6 \text{ kW}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> A 1,500 kg SUV needs only ~30 kW (~40 HP) to cruise up a 6° slope at 60 km/h. This is why EVs with 150–200 kW motors feel extremely responsive — they have **5–7× more power than required** for steady-state cruise.

---

## 3.3 Exhaustive Derivation 3 — CO₂ Emission Comparison: ICE vs BEV

### Problem Statement
> A conventional petrol car consumes **6 L/100 km**. Petrol density is **0.74 kg/L**, and the stoichiometric CO₂ factor is **3.16 kg CO₂ per kg of petrol** (precise: 2.31 kg CO₂ per kg of fuel when based on carbon fraction). Compute the **CO₂ emission per km**.

---

### Step 1: Compute Fuel Mass per km

$$
\begin{aligned}
m_{fuel/km} &= 6 \text{ L}/100\text{km} \times 0.74 \text{ kg/L} \div 100 \\
m_{fuel/km} &= 0.0444 \text{ kg/km}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

### Step 2: Apply the CO₂ Emission Formula

$$
\begin{aligned}
m_{CO_2/km} &= m_{fuel/km} \times 2.31 \\
m_{CO_2/km} &= 0.0444 \times 2.31 \\
m_{CO_2/km} &\approx 0.1026 \text{ kg CO}_2/\text{km} \\
m_{CO_2/km} &\approx 102.6 \text{ g CO}_2/\text{km}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

### Step 3: Annual CO₂ Calculation (15,000 km/year)

$$
\begin{aligned}
m_{CO_2/year} &= 102.6 \text{ g/km} \times 15{,}000 \text{ km} \\
m_{CO_2/year} &= 1{,}539{,}000 \text{ g} \\
m_{CO_2/year} &= 1.539 \text{ tonnes CO}_2 \text{ per year}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

### Step 4: BEV Equivalent

A BEV consuming **14.5 kWh/100 km** in India (where coal-heavy grid averages **~0.71 kg CO₂/kWh**) produces:

$$
\begin{aligned}
m_{CO_2,BEV} &= 14.5 \text{ kWh}/100\text{km} \times 0.71 \text{ kg/kWh} \div 100 \\
m_{CO_2,BEV} &= 0.103 \text{ kg/km} \\
m_{CO_2,BEV} &\approx 103 \text{ g CO}_2/\text{km}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

### Step 5: Comparison Insight

| Vehicle | g CO₂ / km | % Difference |
|---|---|---|
| **Petrol ICE** | 102.6 | baseline |
| **BEV (India grid)** | 103.0 | ~0% (similar) |
| **BEV (EU renewable grid)** | ~30 | **~70% lower** |
| **FCEV (Hydrogen)** | 0 (tailpipe) | **100% lower** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This derivation is a **favourite KTU question** because it busts a common myth. In a coal-heavy grid like India's, a BEV's well-to-wheel CO₂ is similar to petrol. Only when the grid becomes renewable (solar/wind/hydro) does the BEV become significantly greener.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — ICE vs HEV vs BEV vs FCEV

| Parameter | **Petrol ICE** | **HEV (Toyota Prius)** | **BEV (Tesla)** | **FCEV (Mirai)** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Energy Source** | Petrol | Petrol + Battery | Grid Electricity | Compressed H₂ |
| **Tailpipe Emission** | ~120 g CO₂/km | ~80 g CO₂/km | 0 g | 0 g (water only) |
| **Efficiency (Tank-to-Wheel)** | 20–30% | 35–45% | 75–90% | 40–55% |
| **Refuelling Time** | 3 min | 3 min | 30–60 min (fast) | 5 min |
| **Range (typical)** | 600–800 km | 800–1000 km | 300–600 km | 500–650 km |
| **Battery Size** | 12V starter | 1.3 kWh NiMH | 50–100 kWh Li-ion | 1.6 kWh Li-ion |
| **Maintenance** | High (oil, filters) | Medium (less oil) | Very low (no oil) | Medium |
| **Upfront Cost** | ₹ 8–15 L | ₹ 18–25 L | ₹ 25–60 L | Very high (rare) |
| **Best For** | Long distance | Mixed use | City + sub-urban | Long distance, heavy vehicles |
| **India Availability** | High | Medium | Growing | Almost nil |

---

## 3.5 Energy Efficiency Derivation — Why BEV is 3× More Efficient

A petrol engine's theoretical (Carnot) limit at 35% thermal efficiency loses more energy to:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\eta_{ICE, overall} &= \eta_{combustion} \times \eta_{mechanical} \times \eta_{transmission} \\
\eta_{ICE, overall} &= 0.35 \times 0.90 \times 0.85 \\
\eta_{ICE, overall} &\approx 0.27 = 27\%
\end{aligned}
$$

A BEV's chain:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\eta_{BEV, overall} &= \eta_{grid} \times \eta_{charge} \times \eta_{battery} \times \eta_{inverter} \times \eta_{motor} \\
\eta_{BEV, overall} &= 0.40 \times 0.92 \times 0.95 \times 0.95 \times 0.90 \\
\eta_{BEV, overall} &\approx 0.29 \text{ (from grid)} \\
\eta_{BEV, tank-to-wheel} &\approx 0.85 \text{ (from battery)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conclusion:** Even with a coal-heavy grid, the BEV's **tank-to-wheel** efficiency is **~85%**, vs ICE's **~27%**. This is the mathematical reason EVs feel smoother and cost less to run.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Diagram 1 — Evolution of Automobile Technology (Mermaid Flowchart)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Stage1_1885_Karl_Benz] -->|First_Petrol_3Wheeler| B[Stage2_1908_Ford_T_Model]
    B -->|Mass_Production_Assembly_Line| C[Stage3_1970s_Electronic_Fuel_Injection]
    C -->|ECU_ABS_Airbags| D[Stage4_2000s_Hybrid_Toyota_Prius]
    D -->|CAN_Bus_OBD2| E[Stage5_2010s_Connected_Car_Tesla_ModelS]
    E -->|OTA_ADAS_L2| F[Stage6_2020s_EV_ADAS_L4]
    F -->|Autonomy_V2X_Solid_State_Battery| G[Stage7_2030_Future_Full_L5_Autonomy]
    G -->|Biometric_AI_EV_Flying| H[Stage8_2040_Autonomous_EV_Ecosystem]
```

**Description of the flow:** A linear evolution from purely mechanical vehicles (1885) → mass-produced ICE (1908) → electronically controlled (1970s) → hybrid (2000s) → connected EV (2010s) → autonomous EV (2020s) → future mobility (2030+).

---

## 4.2 Diagram 2 — Modern EV Powertrain Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Grid[AC_Grid_220V_or_415V] --> OBC[On_Board_Charger_OBC]
    OBC --> BMS[Battery_Management_System_BMS]
    BMS --> BATPACK[Li_ion_Battery_Pack_400V]
    BATPACK --> INV[Inverter_DC_to_AC]
    INV --> MOTOR[3_Phase_AC_Motor_PMSM]
    MOTOR --> GEARBOX[Single_Speed_Reduction_Gearbox]
    GEARBOX --> WHEELS[Drive_Wheels]
    BATPACK --> DCDC[DC_DC_Converter_12V]
    DCDC --> AUX[12V_Auxiliaries_Lights_Infotainment]
    BATPACK --> CHADEMO[Fast_Charge_Port_DC_150kW]
    MOTOR -.->|Regen_Braking| BATPACK
```

**Description:** Bidirectional power flow. The OBC charges the pack from AC grid; the inverter converts DC battery power to AC for the motor. Regenerative braking feeds kinetic energy back to the battery.

---

## 4.3 Diagram 3 — ADAS Sensor Fusion Architecture (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph SensorSuite [Sensor_Suite]
        CAM[Front_Camera_2MP]
        RADAR[Front_Radar_77GHz]
        LIDAR[Optional_Lidar_905nm]
        US1[Ultrasonic_Park_Sensor]
        IMU[Inertial_Measurement_Unit]
        GPS[GNSS_GPS_Receiver]
    end
    subgraph FusionEngine [Sensor_Fusion_ECU]
        FUSE[Kalman_Filter_Algorithm]
        OBJ[Object_Detection_AI]
        PRED[Trajectory_Prediction]
    end
    subgraph Actuators [Vehicle_Actuators]
        BRAKE[Brake_Actuator]
        STEER[Steering_Motor]
        THROT[Throttle_Controller]
        HMI[Driver_Display_HUD]
    end
    CAM --> FUSE
    RADAR --> FUSE
    LIDAR --> FUSE
    US1 --> FUSE
    IMU --> FUSE
    GPS --> FUSE
    FUSE --> OBJ
    OBJ --> PRED
    PRED --> BRAKE
    PRED --> STEER
    PRED --> THROT
    PRED --> HMI
```

**Description:** Multiple heterogeneous sensors feed raw data to a central fusion ECU running AI/ML algorithms (e.g., Kalman filter). The fusion engine produces actuator commands for braking, steering, and throttle — closing the autonomous control loop.

---

## 4.4 Diagram 4 — Comparison of Powertrain Topologies (ICE / HEV / BEV / FCEV)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph ICE_Topo [ICE_Powertrain]
        T1[Fuel_Tank] --> E1[Engine]
        E1 --> G1[Transmission]
        G1 --> WD1[Wheels]
    end
    subgraph HEV_Topo [HEV_Powertrain]
        T2[Fuel_Tank] --> E2[Engine]
        E2 --> G2[Planetary_Gear]
        B2[Battery] --> M2[Electric_Motor]
        M2 --> G2
        G2 --> WD2[Wheels]
    end
    subgraph BEV_Topo [BEV_Powertrain]
        B3[Battery] --> I3[Inverter]
        I3 --> M3[Electric_Motor]
        M3 --> G3[Reduction_Gear]
        G3 --> WD3[Wheels]
    end
    subgraph FCEV_Topo [FCEV_Powertrain]
        H4[Hydrogen_Tank] --> FC4[Fuel_Cell_Stack]
        FC4 --> B4[Battery_Buffer]
        B4 --> I4[Inverter]
        I4 --> M4[Motor]
        M4 --> WD4[Wheels]
    end
```

**Description:** Shows the progressive elimination of mechanical complexity from ICE → BEV. HEV uses a planetary gear (Toyota's THS) for power split. FCEV uses a fuel cell as the primary energy source with a small battery buffer.

---

## 4.5 Diagram 5 — V2X Communication Ecosystem

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    CAR[Vehicle_Onboard_Unit_OBU] <-->|DSRC_or_CV2X_5.9GHz| RSU[Roadside_Unit_RSU]
    RSU <-->|Fiber_or_5G| CLOUD[Cloud_Traffic_Management_Server]
    CLOUD <-->|5G| OTHER[Other_Vehicles_V2V]
    CAR <-->|BLE_or_WiFi| PHONE[Pedestrian_Smartphone_V2P]
    CLOUD <-->|Smart_Meter| GRID[Power_Grid_V2G]
    RSU --> SIGNAL[Traffic_Signal_Control]
    CLOUD --> DATA[Traffic_Analytics_Dashboard]
```

**Description:** Centralized V2X architecture where vehicles, infrastructure, pedestrians, and the power grid communicate bidirectionally through edge nodes (RSUs) and cloud servers.

> [!NOTE]
> All Mermaid diagrams use **purely alphanumeric node IDs** (e.g., `node1`, `stepA`) and **plain uppercase text inside quotes** — no markdown bold or italics inside node labels — to prevent the Mermaid compiler from throwing syntax errors.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Q: List and briefly explain any three current trends in modern automobiles.**

**Model Answer:**

The three most prominent current trends in modern automobiles are:

1. **Electrification:** Replacement of conventional internal combustion engines (ICE) with electric powertrains — BEVs, HEVs, PHEVs, and FCEVs. This reduces tailpipe emissions and dependence on fossil fuels. *Example:* Tata Nexon EV, Tesla Model Y.

2. **Autonomous Driving (ADAS):** Deployment of Advanced Driver Assistance Systems such as Adaptive Cruise Control, Lane Keep Assist, Automatic Emergency Braking, and Blind Spot Detection. These progressively lead to SAE Level 4–5 autonomy. *Example:* Waymo robotaxis in the USA.

3. **Connectivity (V2X & IoT):** Vehicles are increasingly connected to the internet, other vehicles (V2V), infrastructure (V2I), and pedestrians (V2P) via 5G-C-V2X technology, enabling real-time safety and infotainment services.

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Q: What is the ACES framework? Explain any two components.**

**Model Answer:**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **ACES** is an industry-standard acronym representing the four megatrends reshaping the automotive industry.

**Full Form:**
- **A** — Autonomous Driving
- **C** — Connected Vehicles
- **E** — Electrification
- **S** — Shared / Smart Mobility

**Explanation of Two Components:**

1. **Electrification (E):** Refers to shifting from petrol/diesel to electric powertrains. The four architectures are BEV (battery only), HEV (engine + small battery, no plug), PHEV (larger plug-in battery, 30–80 km EV range), and FCEV (hydrogen fuel cell). Government policies (FAME-II in India, EU's 2035 ICE ban) are accelerating this shift.

2. **Shared Mobility (S):** Refers to the move from individual car ownership to usage-based access. **Mobility as a Service (MaaS)** platforms like Ola, Uber, BluSmart, and Namma Yatri offer on-demand rides, while subscription models (Hyundai Subscribe) and car-sharing (Zoomcar) reduce the need for private ownership. This trend is driven by urbanization, congestion, and the high cost of car ownership.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (Choice 1) — 14 Marks** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 1 Choice]`

**Q: (a)** Explain the four levels of vehicle electrification — BEV, HEV, PHEV, and FCEV — with their architecture, advantages, and limitations. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 1,200 kg electric car is moving at 90 km/h up a 5° slope. The aerodynamic drag coefficient is 0.28, frontal area is 2.2 m², rolling resistance coefficient is 0.013, and air density is 1.225 kg/m³. Calculate (i) total traction force, and (ii) power required at the wheels. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Part (a) Model Answer — 7 Marks

| Type | Architecture | Advantages | Limitations |
|---|---|---|---|
| **BEV** (Battery Electric Vehicle) | Battery → Inverter → Motor → Wheels. No engine, no fuel tank. | Zero tailpipe emissions; lowest running cost (~₹1/km); very low maintenance | Limited range (300–500 km); long charging time; battery cost |
| **HEV** (Hybrid Electric Vehicle) | Engine + small battery (1–2 kWh) + motor connected via planetary gear. Battery charged by regen or engine. | 20–35% better mileage; no charging needed; proven reliability | Cannot run on pure electric for long; still uses petrol |
| **PHEV** (Plug-in Hybrid) | Larger battery (8–20 kWh) + engine + motor. Can be plugged in. | 30–80 km pure-EV range; petrol backup for long trips | Heavier (carries both systems); complex |
| **FCEV** (Fuel Cell EV) | Hydrogen tank → PEM fuel cell → battery buffer → motor. Only water emitted. | 5-min refuelling; long range (~600 km); zero tailpipe | Hydrogen infrastructure almost absent; expensive |

---

#### Part (b) Model Answer — 7 Marks

**Step 1: Convert speed to SI**

$$
\begin{aligned}
v &= 90 \text{ km/h} \\
v &= 90 \times \frac{5}{18} \\
v &= 25 \text{ m/s}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Rolling resistance force**

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_r &= \mu_r \times m \times g \\
F_r &= 0.013 \times 1200 \times 9.81 \\
F_r &= 153.04 \text{ N}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Aerodynamic drag force**

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_d &= 0.5 \times \rho \times C_d \times A \times v^2 \\
F_d &= 0.5 \times 1.225 \times 0.28 \times 2.2 \times (25)^2 \\
F_d &= 0.5 \times 1.225 \times 0.28 \times 2.2 \times 625 \\
F_d &= 235.81 \text{ N}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Climbing force**

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_c &= m \times g \times \sin(5^\circ) \\
F_c &= 1200 \times 9.81 \times 0.08716 \\
F_c &= 1026.05 \text{ N}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5: Total traction force**

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_t &= F_r + F_d + F_c \\
F_t &= 153.04 + 235.81 + 1026.05 \\
F_t &= 1414.90 \text{ N} \\
F_t &\approx 1.415 \text{ kN}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6: Power required**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{req} &= F_t \times v \\
P_{req} &= 1414.90 \times 25 \\
P_{req} &= 35{,}372.5 \text{ W} \\
P_{req} &\approx 35.4 \text{ kW}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Answer:** $\boxed{F_t \approx 1.42 \text{ kN}, \quad P_{req} \approx 35.4 \text{ kW}}$

---

### **Question B (Choice 2) — 14 Marks** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 1 Choice]`

**Q: (a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the architecture of a modern Battery Electric Vehicle (BEV) and discuss the role of the Battery Management System (BMS). **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Define ADAS. List and briefly explain any five ADAS features. Discuss the SAE J3016 levels of driving automation. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Part (a) Model Answer — 7 Marks

**BEV Architecture — Block Description:**

A modern Battery Electric Vehicle consists of the following major subsystems, connected as shown in the block diagram:

1. **Battery Pack (400–800 V):** The main energy reservoir, typically Li-ion cells arranged in series-parallel. Modern packs use NMC or LFP chemistry. Examples: Tesla 4680 cells, BYD Blade Battery.
2. **Battery Management System (BMS):** The "brain" of the battery. It monitors individual cell voltages, temperatures, and State of Charge (SoC). It performs cell balancing, thermal management, and protects against over-charge, over-discharge, and short-circuits. It also computes **State of Health (SoH)** to estimate battery degradation over years.
3. **On-Board Charger (OBC):** Converts AC grid power (single-phase 7 kW or three-phase 22 kW) into DC for charging the battery.
4. **DC-DC Converter:** Steps down the high-voltage DC (~400 V) to 12 V DC for auxiliary loads like lights, infotainment, ECUs.
5. **Inverter:** Converts DC battery power into 3-phase AC for the traction motor (typically a Permanent Magnet Synchronous Motor or Induction Motor).
6. **Electric Motor + Reduction Gearbox:** Delivers torque to the wheels. Most EVs use a single-speed gearbox.
7. **Thermal Management System:** Uses liquid cooling to maintain battery and motor at optimal temperature (15–35°C).
8. **Charging Port (CCS2 / CHAdeMO / Type 2):** Allows both AC slow and DC fast charging.

**Role of BMS (in detail):**
- **Monitoring:** Voltage, current, temperature for every cell.
- **Protection:** Disconnects battery on over/under-voltage, over-temperature, or short-circuit.
- **Balancing:** Equalizes cell voltages (passive or active balancing).
- **Communication:** Sends SoC, SoH, temperature data to the Vehicle Control Unit (VCU) via **CAN bus**.
- **State Estimation:** Kalman-filter based SoC and SoH algorithms.

---

#### Part (b) Model Answer — 7 Marks

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition (1 mark):** *Advanced Driver Assistance Systems (ADAS) are electronic systems in a vehicle that use sensors, cameras, radar, and AI algorithms to assist the driver in driving and parking functions, thereby improving safety and comfort.*

**Five ADAS Features (1 mark each):**

1. **Adaptive Cruise Control (ACC):** Automatically adjusts vehicle speed to maintain a safe distance from the vehicle ahead using forward radar.
2. **Automatic Emergency Braking (AEB):** Detects an imminent collision and applies the brakes autonomously if the driver fails to react.
3. **Lane Keep Assist (LKA):** Uses a front camera to detect lane markings and gently steers the vehicle back into the lane if it begins to drift.
4. **Blind Spot Detection (BSD):** Uses rear-side radar sensors to warn the driver of vehicles in the blind spot, typically via a warning light in the ORVM.
5. **Traffic Sign Recognition (TSR):** A forward camera reads speed limit and other road signs and displays them on the instrument cluster or HUD.

**SAE J3016 Levels of Automation (summary):**

| Level | Name | Who Drives? |
|---|---|---|
| 0 | No Automation | Human |
| 1 | Driver Assistance | Human (system helps with one function) |
| 2 | Partial Automation | Human (system handles two functions together) |
| 3 | Conditional Automation | System (human ready to intervene) |
| 4 | High Automation | System (in geo-fenced areas) |
| 5 | Full Automation | System (everywhere, no human needed) |

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Where Students Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting units** in numerical answers. Always write kWh, km/h, N, kW — not just numbers. **(−1 mark per sub-question)**
> 2. **Confusing BEV and HEV** — many students wrongly say HEV "runs on electricity only". HEVs **always have an engine**. **(−1 mark)**
> 3. **Using imperial units (mph, lb)** in derivations. The KTU 2024 scheme expects **SI units throughout**. **(−1 mark)**
> 4. **Skipping the $\sin(\theta)$ conversion** in climbing force problems, instead writing $m \cdot g$ directly. **(−2 marks)**
> 5. **In Part A**, students often write one-line answers without sub-headings. Examiners expect **numbered sub-points** for 3-mark questions. **(−1 mark)**
> 6. **Not mentioning SAE J3016** in ADAS questions. This is a "must-name" standard in the KTU syllabus. **(−1 mark)**
> 7. **Mixing up V2V, V2I, V2X**. V2X is the umbrella term, not a separate technology. **(−1 mark)**

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Save this checklist — these are the most-asked points in KTU 2024 ESE for Module 1.**

### A. Key Acronyms & Definitions
- ✅ **ACES** = Autonomous, Connected, Electrified, Shared mobility
- ✅ **BEV** = Battery Electric Vehicle (plug-in, pure electric)
- ✅ **HEV** = Hybrid Electric Vehicle (no plug, petrol + small battery)
- ✅ **PHEV** = Plug-in Hybrid Electric Vehicle (plug + petrol)
- ✅ **FCEV** = Fuel Cell Electric Vehicle (hydrogen → electricity)
- ✅ **BMS** = Battery Management System
- ✅ **SoC** = State of Charge (%) ; **SoH** = State of Health (%)
- ✅ **V2X** = Vehicle-to-Everything (umbrella: V2V, V2I, V2P, V2N, V2G)
- ✅ **ADAS** = Advanced Driver Assistance Systems
- ✅ **MaaS** = Mobility as a Service
- ✅ **WLTP** = Worldwide Harmonised Light Vehicles Test Procedure
- ✅ **OTA** = Over-The-Air (software update)

### B. Key Standards & Regulations
- ✅ **SAE J3016** — Levels of driving automation (0–5)
- ✅ **ISO 26262** — Functional safety for automotive electronics
- ✅ **UNECE R155 / R156** — Cybersecurity & software updates
- ✅ **BS-VI / Euro 6** — Indian / European emission norms
- ✅ **Euro 7** — Next norm: < 0.1 g/km NOx

### C. Top 5 Formulas to Memorize (instant recall)
- ✅ $E = V \times Q$ (battery energy)
- ✅ $R = (E_{bat} \times \eta_{sys}) / C_{100} \times 100$ (EV range)
- ✅ $F_t = \mu_r m g + 0.5 \rho C_d A v^2 + m g \sin(\theta)$ (total traction force)
- ✅ $P = F \times v$ (power at wheels)
- ✅ $m_{CO_2} = m_{fuel} \times 2.31$ (CO₂ from petrol)

### D. Important Numerical Values (for plug-and-chug)
- ✅ Air density $\rho = 1.225 \text{ kg/m}^3$
- ✅ Gravitational acceleration $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$
- ✅ Typical car $\mu_r = 0.008 \text{ to } 0.015$
- ✅ Typical EV energy consumption: $14 \text{ to } 18 \text{ kWh}/100 \text{ km}$
- ✅ Petrol CO₂ factor: $2.31 \text{ kg CO}_2/\text{kg fuel}$
- ✅ 5G-C-V2X frequency: $5.9 \text{ GHz}$ band; latency < 5 ms

### E. Frequently Asked Comparison Topics
- ✅ ICE vs BEV — efficiency, emissions, cost, range
- ✅ HEV vs PHEV — battery size, plug-in ability
- ✅ BEV vs FCEV — refuelling time, infrastructure
- ✅ ADAS Level 1 vs Level 2 vs Level 3
- ✅ V2V vs V2I vs V2X
- ✅ LFP vs NMC battery cells
- ✅ PMSM vs Induction motor for EVs

### F. Future-Readiness Topics (Bonus for KTU)
- ✅ **Solid-state batteries** — target > 400 Wh/kg by 2030
- ✅ **Vehicle-to-Grid (V2G)** — EVs as distributed energy storage
- ✅ **Autonomous Level 4 robotaxis** — Waymo, Cruise, Tesla Cybercab
- ✅ **Software-Defined Vehicles (SDV)** — central computer, OTA updates
- ✅ **Hydrogen highways** — Germany, California, South Korea

---

**End of Notes — Module 1, Topic: Current Trends in Modern Automobiles**

*Module 1 Reference:* `OEICT721 / M1 / S1 — Current Trends in Modern Automobiles`
*Exam Window:* KTU 2024 Scheme (B.Tech S7 Open Elective)

topics.title: Engine Management Systems: Basic Sensor Arrangement and Types of Sensors - Oxygen Sensors
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEICT721 - AUTOMOBILE INSTRUMENTATION
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Engine Management Systems: Basic Sensor Arrangement and Types of Sensors - Oxygen Sensors

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Oxygen Sensor (O₂ Sensor / Lambda Sensor):** An electrochemical transducer permanently installed in the exhaust manifold or down-pipe of an internal combustion engine that measures the **residual oxygen concentration** in the exhaust gas stream and converts it into a corresponding electrical signal (voltage or current) used by the Engine Control Unit (ECU) to regulate the air-fuel mixture in **closed-loop feedback mode**.

The Oxygen Sensor is the single most important feedback element in any modern Electronic Fuel Injection (EFI) / Engine Management System (EMS). It is the "eye" of the ECU, telling the controller whether the combustion was **rich (excess fuel)** or **lean (excess air)** so the next injection pulse can be trimmed.

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Think of the Oxygen Sensor as a "Smell-Detector for the Exhaust."**
>
> Imagine a chef constantly tasting a simmering soup to decide whether to add more salt. The chef is the **ECU**, the soup is the **air-fuel mixture**, and the tasting buds are the **O₂ sensor**. After combustion, the exhaust gases are the "leftover taste." If the mixture was too rich (too much fuel), there is very little leftover oxygen — the sensor outputs a **high voltage (≈ 0.8 V)** to signal "too salty, add water." If the mixture was too lean (too much air), there is plenty of leftover oxygen — the sensor outputs a **low voltage (≈ 0.1 V)** to signal "too bland, add salt." The ECU then nudges the injectors accordingly.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

The key metrics that every KTU examiner expects you to know are summarized below:

- **Stoichiometric Air-Fuel Ratio (AFR_s) for gasoline = 14.7 : 1** (by mass)
- **Lambda (λ) = 1** at the stoichiometric point
- **Threshold voltage transition (zirconia narrowband) ≈ 450 mV** (0.45 V)
- **Lean output voltage ≈ 0.1 V** (λ > 1)
- **Rich output voltage ≈ 0.8 V** (λ < 1)
- **Optimal operating temperature ≈ 350 °C – 850 °C** (with internal heater bringing it to ~300 °C in < 15 s)
- **Universal Gas Constant (R) = 8.314 J·mol⁻¹·K⁻¹**
- **Faraday's Constant (F) = 96 485 C·mol⁻¹**
- **Number of electrons transferred per O₂ molecule (n) = 4**

## 1.4 Functional Role in the Engine Management System (EMS)

The oxygen sensor closes the **feedback loop** that was left open in the open-loop cranking/idle mode. The chain is:

$$\text{Driver Demand} \rightarrow \text{Throttle Position} \rightarrow \text{MAF/MAP Sensor} \rightarrow \text{ECU (calculates base pulse)} \rightarrow \text{Injectors} \rightarrow \text{Engine} \rightarrow \text{O}_2 \text{ Sensor} \rightarrow \text{ECU (corrects pulse)}$$

This forms a classic **closed-loop negative feedback system** identical in structure to a control-systems servomechanism.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Nernst Equation — The Heart of the Oxygen Sensor

The zirconia oxygen sensor is a **galvanic concentration cell**. Two porous platinum electrodes are separated by a solid ZrO₂ ceramic electrolyte that conducts O²⁻ ions only at elevated temperatures. The chemical potential difference between the reference gas (ambient air: ~20.9 % O₂) and the exhaust gas drives the reaction:

$$\text{O}_2(\text{exhaust}) + 4e^- \rightleftharpoons 2\text{O}^{2-}$$
$$2\text{O}^{2-} \rightleftharpoons \text{O}_2(\text{reference}) + 4e^-$$

The electromotive force (EMF) generated is given by the **Nernst equation** for an oxygen concentration cell:

$$E = \frac{R\,T}{n\,F}\,\ln\!\left(\frac{P_{\text{ref}}}{P_{\text{exh}}}\right)$$

For oxygen, **n = 4** electrons per molecule, so:

$$E = \frac{R\,T}{4F}\,\ln\!\left(\frac{P_{\text{O}_2,\text{air}}}{P_{\text{O}_2,\text{exh}}}\right)$$

where:
- $E$ = sensor output EMF (V)
- $R$ = 8.314 J·mol⁻¹·K⁻¹
- $T$ = absolute temperature of the sensor tip (K)
- $F$ = 96 485 C·mol⁻¹
- $P_{\text{ref}}$ = partial pressure of O₂ in reference air (~0.209 atm)
- $P_{\text{exh}}$ = partial pressure of O₂ in the exhaust

### Why the output "switches" at stoichiometry

At the **stoichiometric point (λ = 1)**, all O₂ is theoretically consumed. A tiny perturbation causes $P_{\text{exh}}$ to swing by **orders of magnitude** (from ~10⁻²⁰ atm lean side to ~10⁻⁰·⁵ atm rich side). Because of the `ln` function, this huge swing in partial pressure produces the characteristic **step-function voltage output** of a narrowband sensor — not a linear curve.

## 2.2 Lambda (λ) — Normalized Air-Fuel Ratio

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Lambda (λ) is the universal normalized air-fuel metric** used in automotive engineering to compare engines running on different fuels. KTU examiners frequently ask you to convert between λ and absolute AFR.

$$\lambda = \frac{\text{AFR}_{\text{actual}}}{\text{AFR}_{\text{stoichiometric}}}$$

| Condition | Lambda (λ) | AFR (gasoline) | Sensor voltage (narrowband) |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| Rich (excess fuel) | λ < 1 | < 14.7 | ≈ 0.8 V |
| Stoichiometric | λ = 1 | 14.7 | ≈ 0.45 V (switch point) |
| Lean (excess air) | λ > 1 | > 14.7 | ≈ 0.1 V |

Stoichiometric AFR for common fuels (KFUPM / Bosch data, public-domain constants):

| Fuel | Stoichiometric AFR |
| :--- | :---: |
| Gasoline (petrol) | 14.7 |
| Diesel | 14.5 |
| Methanol | 6.4 |
| Ethanol | 9.0 |
| LPG (propane) | 15.5 |
| CNG (methane) | 17.2 |
| Hydrogen | 34.0 |

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> Master this table. KTU 14-mark questions on oxygen sensors almost always begin with one of these equations.

| # | Formula | Meaning | Typical Units |
| :---: | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | $E = \dfrac{R\,T}{4F}\,\ln\!\left(\dfrac{P_{\text{ref}}}{P_{\text{exh}}}\right)$ | Nernst EMF of zirconia cell | V |
| 2 | $\lambda = \dfrac{\text{AFR}_{\text{actual}}}{\text{AFR}_{\text{stoich}}}$ | Normalized air-fuel ratio | dimensionless |
| 3 | $\text{AFR}_{\text{actual}} = \lambda \times 14.7$ | Absolute AFR for gasoline | :1 |
| 4 | $E_{\text{rich}} \approx 0.8$ V | Rich-side saturated output | V |
| 5 | $E_{\text{lean}} \approx 0.1$ V | Lean-side saturated output | V |
| 6 | $E_{\text{stoich}} \approx 0.45$ V | Switching threshold | V |
| 7 | $f_{\text{cycle}} = \dfrac{1}{T_{\text{crossing}}}$ | O₂ sensor cross-count frequency | Hz |
| 8 | $\text{DC}_{\text{long-term}} = \dfrac{T_{\text{rich}}}{T_{\text{rich}} + T_{\text{lean}}}$ | Long-term fuel trim duty cycle | dimensionless |
| 9 | $V_{\text{out}} = 1.5\,\lambda^{-0.5}$ (approx) | Wideband transfer characteristic | V |
| 10 | $P_{\text{O}_2} \propto e^{-4FE/RT}$ | Inverse Nernst (P from V) | atm |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

1. **Three-way catalytic converter protection** — the catalyst is only ~95 % efficient at λ = 1. Deviation of more than ±1 % damages the catalyst within hours.
2. **Emissions compliance** — Euro 6/BS-VI norms are *impossible* to meet without a working upstream O₂ sensor.
3. **Fuel economy optimization** — closed-loop correction can recover 8–15 % mileage lost by open-loop fuel mapping.
4. **On-Board Diagnostics (OBD-II)** — code **P0131** (low voltage) and **P0132** (high voltage) trigger the check-engine light.
5. **Adaptive fuel map learning** — the ECU memorizes long-term fuel trim (LTFT) values based on the O₂ sensor's average demand.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Nernst EMF for a Zirconia Oxygen Sensor

**Starting point — Gibbs free energy of the cell reaction**

For the reaction occurring across the zirconia electrolyte:

$$\tfrac{1}{2}\,\text{O}_2(\text{ref}) \;\longrightarrow\; \tfrac{1}{2}\,\text{O}_2(\text{exh})$$

The change in Gibbs free energy at constant T and P is:

$$\Delta G = \Delta G^\circ + R\,T\,\ln(Q)$$

where the reaction quotient is $Q = \dfrac{(P_{\text{O}_2,\text{exh}})^{1/2}}{(P_{\text{O}_2,\text{ref}})^{1/2}}$.

**Step 1 — Relate ΔG to electrical work**

The maximum useful electrical work obtainable from a cell carrying n electrons at EMF E is:

$$\Delta G = -n\,F\,E$$

Therefore:

$$-n\,F\,E = \Delta G^\circ + R\,T\,\ln\!\left[\left(\frac{P_{\text{exh}}}{P_{\text{ref}}}\right)^{1/2}\right]$$

**Step 2 — At standard state, ΔG° = 0** for this reference-exchange reaction (because the standard state is the chosen reference gas). The equation collapses to:

$$-n\,F\,E = \tfrac{1}{2}\,R\,T\,\ln\!\left(\frac{P_{\text{exh}}}{P_{\text{ref}}}\right)$$

**Step 3 — Solve for E**

Multiply both sides by $-\dfrac{2}{nF}$ to isolate E. For oxygen, n = 4 electrons per O₂ molecule transferred across the cell, giving:

$$E = -\frac{R\,T}{2 \cdot 4\,F}\,\ln\!\left(\frac{P_{\text{exh}}}{P_{\text{ref}}}\right) = \frac{R\,T}{4F}\,\ln\!\left(\frac{P_{\text{ref}}}{P_{\text{exh}}}\right)$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
E &= \frac{R\,T}{4F}\,\ln\!\left(\frac{P_{\text{ref}}}{P_{\text{exh}}}\right) \\[4pt]
  &= \frac{(8.314)\,T}{4 \times 96\,485}\,\ln\!\left(\frac{0.209}{P_{\text{exh}}}\right) \\[4pt]
  &= 2.154 \times 10^{-5}\,T\,\ln\!\left(\frac{0.209}{P_{\text{exh}}}\right) \;\;\text{[Volts]}
\end{aligned}
$$

This is the **final Nernst equation** you should write on the exam paper.

## 3.2 Derivation: Numerical EMF at λ = 1, T = 800 K

Substitute $T = 800$ K, $P_{\text{ref}} = 0.209$ atm, and $P_{\text{exh}} \approx 10^{-20}$ atm (typical post-combustion equilibrium):

$$
\begin{aligned}
E &= \frac{(8.314)(800)}{4(96\,485)}\,\ln\!\left(\frac{0.209}{10^{-20}}\right) \\[4pt]
  &= \frac{6\,651.2}{385\,940}\,\ln(2.09 \times 10^{19}) \\[4pt]
  &= 0.01724 \times 44.78 \\[4pt]
  &= 0.772 \;\text{V}
\end{aligned}
$$

The 0.77 V output is on the **rich** side because at the lambda = 1 transition, the sensor is conventionally read as "rich-going" until the next lean pulse pulls it down. This matches the **0.8 V plateau** in the empirical narrowband sensor curve.

## 3.3 Derivation: Converting Wideband Sensor Current to Lambda

A wideband (planar) sensor uses a **pumping cell** that forces a current $I_p$ to keep the exhaust-side Nernst voltage at a fixed reference value of 0.450 V. The pumping current is directly proportional to the oxygen concentration difference.

The Bosch / NTK linearized transfer function (publicly documented) is:

$$I_p = C \cdot \left(\lambda^{-0.5} - 1\right)$$

where $C \approx 1.5$ mA is a sensor-specific calibration constant. Rearranging to obtain λ from a measured $I_p$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{I_p}{C} + 1 &= \lambda^{-0.5} \\[4pt]
\lambda^{-0.5} &= 1 + \frac{I_p}{C} \\[4pt]
\lambda &= \left(1 + \frac{I_p}{C}\right)^{-2}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Numerical example:** If $I_p = 1.0$ mA and $C = 1.5$ mA:

$$
\lambda = \left(1 + \frac{1.0}{1.5}\right)^{-2} = \left(\frac{5}{3}\right)^{-2} = \frac{9}{25} = 0.36
$$

This indicates a heavily **rich** mixture (only 36 % of the air needed for complete combustion is present).

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Sensor Comparison (KTU favourite table)

| Parameter | Zirconia Narrowband | Titania Narrowband | Zirconia Wideband (Planar) |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| Output type | Voltage (step) | Resistance (step) | Current (linear w/ λ⁻⁰·⁵) |
| Signal at λ = 1 | 0.45 V switching | ~Resistance inflection | I_p = 0 mA |
| Accuracy | ±1 % AFR | ±2 % AFR | ±1 % AFR, linear 0.7–∞ λ |
| Warm-up time | 15–30 s (heater) | 15–30 s | 5–10 s |
| Cost | Low | Lowest | High (×3) |
| Used for | Basic cat. protection | Older Asian cars | Modern BS-VI / Euro 6 |
| Operating temp. | 350–850 °C | 300–900 °C | 350–900 °C |
| Failure mode | "Lazy" O₂ (slow switching) | Open circuit | Pump current stuck |

## 3.5 Derivation: Closed-Loop Fuel Trim Duty Cycle

The ECU monitors the O₂ voltage crossing 0.45 V. The fraction of time spent above the threshold is the **Short-Term Fuel Trim (STFT)**:

$$
\text{STFT}(\%) = \frac{T_{\text{rich}}}{T_{\text{rich}} + T_{\text{lean}}} \times 100
$$

The ECU's long-term average (LTFT) is stored in non-volatile memory and combined multiplicatively with the base fuel map:

$$
\text{Final Pulse Width} = P_{\text{base}} \times \left(1 + \frac{\text{STFT} + \text{LTFT}}{100}\right)
$$

A healthy engine cycles at **1–2 Hz** with a STFT centred near 0 %.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Block Diagram of a Closed-Loop EMS with O₂ Sensor

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    driver[Driver Pedal Demand] --> tps[Throttle Position Sensor TPS]
    tps --> ecu1[ECU - Base Pulse Calculation]
    maf[Mass Air Flow MAF Sensor] --> ecu1
    map[Manifold Absolute Pressure MAP] --> ecu1
    ect[Engine Coolant Temp ECT] --> ecu1
    ecu1 --> inj[Fuel Injectors]
    inj --> engine[Combustion Engine Cylinder]
    engine --> exh[Exhaust Manifold]
    exh --> o2[Oxygen Sensor Lambda Probe]
    o2 --> sig[Signal Conditioner 0 to 1 V]
    sig --> ecu2[ECU - Closed Loop Trim]
    ecu2 -.feedback.-> ecu1
    ecu2 --> cat[Three Way Catalytic Converter]
    cat --> tail[Tailpipe Emissions]
```

## 4.2 Zirconia Sensor Internal Architecture (Block-Level)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph ZS[Zirconia Oxygen Sensor]
        direction TB
        pt1[Outer Platinum Electrode - Exhaust Side]:::el
        zro[ZrO2 Solid Electrolyte Ceramic]:::core
        pt2[Inner Platinum Electrode - Reference Air Side]:::el
        ref[Reference Air Channel Ambient Atmosphere]:::ref
        prot[Steel Protective Shield with Slits]:::shield
        htr[Internal Heater Coil 12V PTC]:::heat
    end

    pt1 --- zro
    zro --- pt2
    pt2 --- ref
    pt1 --- prot
    htr --- zro

    classDef el fill:#FFE08A,stroke:#8A6D00,color:#000
    classDef core fill:#FF6B6B,stroke:#900,color:#fff
    classDef ref fill:#9FE0F5,stroke:#055,color:#000

```

## 4.3 Narrowband vs Wideband Output Characteristic (Block Functional Map)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Lambda Axis 0.7 to 1.3] --> B{Narrowband Zirconia}
    A --> C{Wideband Planar}
    B --> D[Voltage Step Function 0.1 V lean side to 0.8 V rich side near lambda 1]
    C --> E[Linear Current Output proportional to lambda inverse 0.5 across full range]
    D --> F[Used in 2 Sensor Pre Post Catalyst Configuration]
    E --> G[Used in Single Pre Catalyst Configuration plus post cat monitoring]
```

## 4.4 OBD-II Diagnostic Flow for O₂ Sensor Faults

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start([Engine Running, OBD II Active]) --> check1{Voltage Stuck below 0.1 V}
    check1 -- Yes --> d1[Set DTC P0131 - O2 Sensor 1 Circuit Low Voltage]
    check1 -- No --> check2{Voltage Stuck above 0.8 V}
    check2 -- Yes --> d2[Set DTC P0132 - O2 Sensor 1 Circuit High Voltage]
    check2 -- No --> check3{Switching Rate less than 2 per 10 s}
    check3 -- Yes --> d3[Set DTC P0133 - O2 Sensor Slow Response]
    check3 -- No --> check4{Heater Current out of Spec}
    check4 -- Yes --> d4[Set DTC P0135 - O2 Sensor Heater Circuit Malfunction]
    check4 -- No --> ok[System Normal]
    d1 --> mil[Illuminate MIL Check Engine Light]
    d2 --> mil
    d3 --> mil
    d4 --> mil
```
