Also, watch out for the unit of $d$ — converting $20\ \text{mm}$ to $0.020\ \text{m}$ is essential, and missing it costs the full 1–2 marks for velocity. --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **⚡ Rapid Revision Checklist — Air Mass Flow Sensors** - ✅ **Definition:** MAF sensor directly measures the **mass** of intake air, providing the primary input to the ECU for stoichiometric AFR control (14.7:1 for petrol). - ✅ **Four types** as per KTU syllabus: **Vane-type (VAF), Hot-wire (HFM), Hot-film (HFM), Kármán Vortex**. - ✅ **Hot-wire principle:** Constant-temperature anemometer; heating current $I_h$ varies to maintain $T_{wire} = T_{air} + \Delta T$. **Output ∝ mass flow.** - ✅ **King's Law:** $h = a + b\sqrt{v}$ — the foundation of hot-wire anemometer signal conditioning. - ✅ **Fourth-power law:** $\dot{m} \propto I_h^4$ — derived from $I_h^2$ (heating) × $v^2$ (continuity). **This is a guaranteed exam question.** - ✅ **Burn-off cycle:** High current pulse after ignition OFF, heats wire to $\approx 1000\ \text{°C}$ for 1 s to incinerate contaminants. - ✅ **Hot-film advantage:** MEMS-deposited platinum on ceramic — robust, no burn-off needed, faster response. - ✅ **Kármán Vortex:** $f = St \cdot v / d$; with $St \approx 0.21$ for circular bluff body. **Frequency-domain output** is noise-immune and self-cleaning. - ✅ **Wheatstone bridge topology:** Hot wire + reference thermistor form the two active arms. Bridge is held in dynamic balance by the ECU. - ✅ **ECU final step:** $t_{inj} = K_1 \cdot \dot{m} + K_2 \cdot \text{corrections (coolant temp, lambda feedback, battery voltage trim)}$. - ✅ **Real-world applications:** Bosch HFM (used by VW, Audi, BMW), Hitachi (used by GM, Ford), Denso (Toyota), Siemens/Continental (Mercedes). Kármán vortex preferred in Mitsubishi and Suzuki models. - ✅ **Closed-loop feedback:** Lambda O₂ sensor in the exhaust continuously trims the injector pulse width based on residual oxygen, ensuring long-term AFR accuracy. - ✅ **Failure symptoms:** Erratic idle, poor fuel economy, hesitation, illuminated Check Engine Light (DTC P0100–P0104 range). --- *End of Module 2 — Air Mass Flow Sensors Notes* *Compiled as per KTU 2024 Scheme | OEICT721 — Automobile Instrumentation*

topics.title: Throttle Position Sensors
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEICT721 - AUTOMOBILE INSTRUMENTATION
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Throttle Position Sensors

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **Throttle Position Sensor (TPS)** is a rotary-position variable-reluctance / resistive / Hall-effect transducer mechanically coupled to the throttle-valve spindle of an internal combustion engine's throttle body. Its primary function is to provide a real-time, electrically measurable analogue (or digital-PWM) signal proportional to the instantaneous angular opening of the throttle plate, thereby informing the Engine Control Unit (ECU) of the driver's torque demand.

The TPS is formally classified as a **driver-intent sensor** within the engine management sensor taxonomy and operates within a specified operating angular range, typically **0° to 90°** (idle to wide-open throttle, WOT), translating mechanical shaft rotation into an electrical voltage signal ranging from approximately **0.5 V (closed throttle)** to **4.5 V (WOT)**, with a nominal supply of **+5.0 V DC**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition Anchor**
> *"A Throttle Position Sensor is an electromechanical transducer mounted on the throttle body shaft that converts the angular position of the throttle plate into a proportional DC voltage signal for use by the Powertrain Control Module (PCM) in fuel metering, ignition timing, and transmission control strategies."*

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Gas Pedal Translator"

Imagine a translator sitting between a passenger (the driver's foot) and a pilot (the ECU) inside an aircraft cockpit. The passenger can scream, "Go faster!" or "Slow down!" but the pilot needs a precise, calibrated numerical dial reading to act. The Throttle Position Sensor is that translator.

- The **driver's foot** presses the accelerator pedal → mechanical cable / electronic pedal module moves the **throttle plate**.
- The **throttle plate** rotates from 0° (fully closed, idle) to 90° (fully open, maximum air).
- The **TPS** sits on the same shaft, like a watch dial glued to a steering wheel, and continuously whispers to the ECU, *"The plate is 23% open… 27%… 35%…"*
- The **ECU** then decides how much fuel to inject and when to fire the spark — because air mass alone is not enough; the *rate of change* of throttle angle also matters (acceleration enrichment, deceleration fuel cut-off).

In modern **Drive-By-Wire (DBW)** systems, the pedal itself has a position sensor (APP sensor), and the throttle is moved by an electric motor. The TPS then closes the **closed-loop feedback**, ensuring the demanded throttle angle is actually achieved — a *servo-control* architecture.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why two sensors on one throttle? — The Safety Redundancy Principle**
> KTU examiners frequently ask why modern vehicles use a **dual-track TPS** (TPS-1 and TPS-2). The answer lies in ISO 26262 functional safety: if one track fails (open or short), the second track (with an inverted, mirrored voltage curve) allows the ECU to detect the fault via a **rationality check** and enter a *limp-home mode* rather than causing an unintended acceleration event.

### 1.3 Key Physical Constants and Operating Metrics

| Parameter | Standard Value | Tolerance |
|---|---|---|
| Supply Voltage (V_ref) | **+5.0 V DC** | ± 0.1 V |
| Idle Closed Voltage | **0.5 V** | ± 0.1 V |
| WOT Voltage | **4.5 V** | ± 0.1 V |
| Angular Range | **0° to 90°** | — |
| Output Linearity | Within **± 2 %** of V_ref | — |
| Operating Temperature | **−40 °C to +150 °C** | — |
| Track-to-Track Correlation | Within **± 5 %** | Redundant TPS |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Principle — Step-by-Step Logical Breakdown

The TPS operates on one of three dominant transduction principles. KTU 2024 requires understanding of all three:

#### 2.1.1 Potentiometric (Contact-Type) TPS
1. A **precision thin-film resistive track** is deposited on a ceramic substrate, forming a voltage divider.
2. A **wiper arm**, mechanically fixed to the throttle shaft, slides along the track as the throttle rotates.
3. The ECU applies **+5.0 V** at one end and **0.0 V (ground)** at the other.
4. The wiper taps off a voltage $V_{out}$ proportional to the wiper's fractional position $x$ along the track.
5. The output is therefore a **ratiometric** voltage — its meaning depends on the stable +5 V reference of the ECU.

#### 2.1.2 Hall-Effect (Non-Contact) TPS
1. A semiconductor Hall element is supplied with a constant bias current $I$.
2. A **diametrically magnetized ring magnet** is fixed to the throttle shaft, producing a tangential magnetic flux density $B(\theta)$ that varies sinusoidally (or in a custom-shaped profile) with angle $\theta$.
3. The Hall voltage $V_H = K_H \cdot I \cdot B \sin(\phi)$ is generated perpendicular to both $I$ and $B$.
4. An integrated ASIC conditions, linearises, and amplifies this signal to a rail-to-rail 0–5 V analogue output or a PWM output.
5. **No mechanical wear** → typical service life > **1,000,000 cycles**.

#### 2.1.3 Magneto-Resistive / Inductive TPS
1. Uses **Anisotropic Magneto-Resistive (AMR)** or **Giant Magneto-Resistive (GMR)** elements whose resistance $R$ changes with applied field angle.
2. A permanent magnet rotates with the throttle shaft.
3. The bridge configuration converts $\Delta R$ into a differential voltage $\Delta V$, which is then digitised.
4. Used in high-end applications requiring **> 0.05° resolution** and **> 12-bit resolution**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why the ECU needs the *rate* of change**
> The ECU differentiates the TPS signal: $\frac{dV_{out}}{dt}$ corresponds to the rate of pedal movement. A sudden positive $\frac{dV}{dt}$ triggers **acceleration enrichment** (extra fuel injected to prevent lean misfire), while a rapid negative $\frac{dV}{dt}$ triggers **deceleration fuel cut-off (DFCO)**, shutting injectors entirely to save fuel.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All quantities below are **must-know** for KTU Part-B numerical questions. Memorise units and boundary conditions.

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $V_{out} = V_{ref} \cdot \dfrac{R_w}{R_{total}}$ | Potentiometric voltage divider output | Volts (V) |
| 2 | $V_{out}(\theta) = V_{idle} + \left(\dfrac{V_{WOT} - V_{idle}}{\theta_{max}}\right) \cdot \theta$ | Linear TPS transfer function | Volts (V) |
| 3 | $K_s = \dfrac{V_{WOT} - V_{idle}}{\theta_{max}}$ | Sensor sensitivity (slope) | V / degree |
| 4 | $V_H = \dfrac{R_H \cdot I \cdot B}{t}$ | Hall-Effect output voltage (thin-film approximation) | Volts (V) |
| 5 | $P = \dfrac{V_{out}}{V_{ref}} \times 100 \,\%$ | Throttle position in percentage | % |
| 6 | $\theta = \theta_{idle} + \left(\dfrac{V_{out} - V_{idle}}{V_{WOT} - V_{idle}}\right) \cdot \theta_{max}$ | Inverse — angle from voltage | degrees (°) |
| 7 | $\Delta V = V_{TPS1} + V_{TPS2} = V_{ref} \pm \delta$ | Redundant-track rationality check | Volts (V) |
| 8 | $\varepsilon = \left \vert \dfrac{V_{out,\,measured} - V_{out,\,ideal}}{V_{ref}} \right \vert \times 100$ | Linearity / accuracy error | % |
| 9 | $f_{PWM} = \dfrac{1}{T_{PWM}}$ | Digital TPS output PWM frequency | Hz |
| 10 | $D = \dfrac{T_{high}}{T_{PWM}} \times 100 \,\%$ | PWM duty cycle (position) | % |

> **Symbol Glossary**
> - $V_{ref}$ : Regulated +5 V ECU reference.
> - $R_w$ : Resistance from ground end to wiper contact.
> - $R_{total}$ : Total track resistance (typically **1.0 kΩ to 10 kΩ**).
> - $\theta$ : Instantaneous throttle angle.
> - $\theta_{max}$ : Full-scale mechanical angle (**90°** typical).
> - $K_s$ : Sensitivity, ideally **~ 44.4 mV / °** for a 0.5–4.5 V span over 90°.
> - $K_H$ : Hall coefficient.
> - $D$ : Duty cycle (digital TPS).
> - $R_H$ : Hall coefficient of the semiconductor.
> - $t$ : Thickness of the Hall plate.

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

The TPS is not merely a "gas-pedal gauge". It is the **cornerstone of torque-based engine management** and feeds multiple sub-systems:

- **Electronic Throttle Control (ETC / DBW)**: Closed-loop feedback of actual vs. demanded angle.
- **Fuel Injection (PFI / GDI)**: Base pulse-width modulation, acceleration enrichment, DFCO.
- **Ignition Timing**: Maps advance / retard vs. throttle position and RPM.
- **Automatic Transmission**: Shift scheduling — up-shifts typically occur at lower RPM under partial throttle than at WOT.
- **Traction Control (TCS)**: Compares TPS rate vs. wheel-speed rate; mismatch implies wheel slip.
- **Cruise Control**: Holds the angle (and thus the vehicle speed) constant.
- **Idle Air Control (IAC)**: TPS "closed" signal initiates idle-speed PID loop.
- **A/C Compressor Cut-off**: On hard acceleration, A/C is briefly disengaged to free up engine power.
- **EVAP Purge Valve**: Activated only above a certain TPS threshold.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineer Insight:** In a 2015+ Toyota, BMW, or Ford vehicle, a failing TPS does **not** cause a no-start. The ECU substitutes a default "fail-safe" value and triggers **P0120 / P0121 / P0122 / P0123** OBD-II Diagnostic Trouble Codes. The driver notices hesitation, surging, or an illuminated **Check Engine Light (CEL)**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation I — Transfer Function of a Linear Potentiometric TPS

**Problem Statement:**
A potentiometric TPS is supplied with $V_{ref} = +5.0$ V. Its total track resistance is $R_{total} = 5.0$ kΩ. At the **closed-throttle** position, the wiper resistance from the ground end is $R_{w,idle} = 0.5$ kΩ. At **wide-open throttle (WOT)**, $R_{w,WOT} = 4.5$ kΩ. The throttle rotates through $\theta_{max} = 90°$. Derive the linear transfer function $V_{out}(\theta)$ and the sensitivity $K_s$.

**Step 1 — Apply the Voltage Divider Rule**
The wiper and the un-tapped portion of the track form a two-resistor voltage divider across $V_{ref}$.

$$V_{out} = V_{ref} \cdot \frac{R_w}{R_{total}}$$

**Step 2 — Compute the Closed-Throttle Output**
Substituting $R_w = R_{w,idle} = 0.5$ kΩ and $R_{total} = 5.0$ kΩ:

$$V_{out,\,idle} = 5.0 \cdot \frac{0.5}{5.0} = 5.0 \cdot 0.10 = 0.50 \text{ V}$$

This matches the standard **0.5 V** idle specification.

**Step 3 — Compute the WOT Output**
Substituting $R_w = R_{w,WOT} = 4.5$ kΩ:

$$V_{out,\,WOT} = 5.0 \cdot \frac{4.5}{5.0} = 5.0 \cdot 0.90 = 4.50 \text{ V}$$

This matches the standard **4.5 V** WOT specification.

**Step 4 — Derive the Linear Transfer Function $V_{out}(\theta)$**
Assume the wiper is mechanically linear with the throttle angle (i.e., $R_w$ varies linearly with $\theta$). Then:

$$R_w(\theta) = R_{w,idle} + \left(\frac{R_{w,WOT} - R_{w,idle}}{\theta_{max}}\right) \cdot \theta$$

Substitute into the divider equation:

$$V_{out}(\theta) = V_{ref} \cdot \frac{R_w(\theta)}{R_{total}} = V_{ref} \cdot \frac{R_{w,idle} + \left(\dfrac{R_{w,WOT} - R_{w,idle}}{\theta_{max}}\right)\theta}{R_{total}}$$

Now expand and recognise the structure:

$$V_{out}(\theta) = \underbrace{V_{ref} \cdot \frac{R_{w,idle}}{R_{total}}}_{V_{idle}} + \underbrace{\frac{V_{ref}}{R_{total}} \cdot \frac{R_{w,WOT} - R_{w,idle}}{\theta_{max}}}_{K_s} \cdot \theta$$

**Step 5 — Insert Numerical Values**
- $V_{idle} = 0.50$ V (verified)
- Numerator of $K_s$: $V_{ref} \cdot (R_{w,WOT} - R_{w,idle}) = 5.0 \cdot (4.5 - 0.5) = 5.0 \cdot 4.0 = 20.0$ V·kΩ
- Denominator of $K_s$: $R_{total} \cdot \theta_{max} = 5.0 \cdot 90 = 450$ kΩ·°

$$K_s = \frac{20.0}{450} = 0.04444 \text{ V/°} = 44.44 \text{ mV/°}$$

**Step 6 — Write the Final Compact Transfer Function**

$$V_{out}(\theta) = 0.50 + 0.04444 \cdot \theta \quad \text{(V, with } \theta \text{ in degrees)}$$

**Step 7 — Verification Table**

| $\theta$ (°) | $V_{out}$ (V) | Comment |
|---|---|---|
| 0 | **0.500** | Idle |
| 22.5 | 1.500 | Quarter throttle |
| 45 | 2.500 | Half throttle |
| 67.5 | 3.500 | Three-quarter throttle |
| 90 | **4.500** | WOT |

The line is perfectly linear and spans the full design range. ✅

### 3.2 Derivation II — Inverse Mapping (Angle from Measured Voltage)

**Problem Statement:**
During a workshop diagnostic, the ECU reads $V_{out} = 2.78$ V from the TPS. Determine the corresponding throttle angle $\theta$.

**Step 1 — Invert the Transfer Function**
Starting from:

$$V_{out} = V_{idle} + K_s \cdot \theta$$

Solve for $\theta$:

$$\theta = \frac{V_{out} - V_{idle}}{K_s}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute Numerical Values**

$$\theta = \frac{2.78 - 0.50}{0.04444} = \frac{2.28}{0.04444} = 51.3°$$

**Step 3 — Compute the Equivalent Percentage Opening**

$$P = \frac{V_{out}}{V_{ref}} \times 100 = \frac{2.78}{5.0} \times 100 = 55.6\%$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Note that $\theta$ and $P$ are *not* equal because the TPS is *not* zero-based at 0 V. The **0.5 V offset** is a deliberate design feature that gives the ECU a noise-margin to distinguish "true idle" from "sensor fault / open circuit" (which would read ~0 V).

### 3.3 Derivation III — Hall-Effect Signal Generation in a Non-Contact TPS

**Problem Statement:**
A Hall-Effect TPS uses a ring magnet producing a peak tangential flux density $B_{peak} = 0.15$ T at the Hall element. The bias current is $I = 5.0$ mA. The Hall plate has thickness $t = 100 \,\mu$m and Hall coefficient $R_H = 1.0 \times 10^{-4}$ m³/C (typical for InSb). Calculate the peak Hall voltage.

**Step 1 — Hall Voltage Formula (Thin-Film Approximation)**

$$V_H = \frac{R_H \cdot I \cdot B}{t}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the Values**

$$V_H = \frac{(1.0 \times 10^{-4}) \cdot (5.0 \times 10^{-3}) \cdot 0.15}{100 \times 10^{-6}}$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate Numerator**

$$\text{Numerator} = 1.0 \times 10^{-4} \times 5.0 \times 10^{-3} \times 0.15$$

$$= 5.0 \times 10^{-7} \times 0.15$$

$$= 7.5 \times 10^{-8} \text{ V·m}$$

**Step 4 — Divide by Thickness**

$$V_H = \frac{7.5 \times 10^{-8}}{1.0 \times 10^{-4}} = 7.5 \times 10^{-4} \text{ V} = 0.75 \text{ mV}$$

**Step 5 — Engineering Interpretation**
A raw Hall voltage of **0.75 mV** is far too small to be digitised reliably by the ECU's 12-bit ADC (which has a least-significant-bit of $\dfrac{5.0}{4096} \approx 1.22$ mV). Therefore, the ASIC inside the Hall TPS **amplifies and conditions** this signal by a factor of **~ 1000×** and adds a **0.5 V offset** and **4.0 V span** scaling to produce the final 0.5–4.5 V output.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparative Performance Table

| Property | Potentiometric | Hall-Effect | Magneto-Resistive |
|---|---|---|---|
| Output | Analogue voltage | Analogue / PWM | Analogue / SENT |
| Linearity | ± 2 % | ± 1 % | ± 0.5 % |
| Resolution | ~ 12-bit effective | > 12-bit | 14-bit |
| Wear-Out Mechanism | **Wiper-track abrasion** | None (contactless) | None |
| Service Life | ~ 10⁶ cycles | > 10⁷ cycles | > 10⁷ cycles |
| Cost | **Low** | Medium | High |
| Hysteresis | Noticeable | Negligible | Negligible |
| EMI Susceptibility | Low | Medium | Low |
| Typical Application | Economy vehicles | Mid-segment | Premium / Motorsport |
| Redundancy | Dual-track | Dual-die ASIC | Dual-bridge |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — TPS Signal Path in Engine Management

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    driverA[Driver Foot] --> pedalA[Accelerator Pedal]
    pedalA --> shaftA[Throttle Spindle]
    shaftA --> plateA[Throttle Plate]
    shaftA -. couples mechanically .-> tpsA[TPS Rotary Sensor]
    tpsA -->|0.5 to 4.5 V| ecuA[Engine Control Unit ECU]
    ecuA -->|Fuel Pulse Width| injA[Fuel Injectors]
    ecuA -->|Spark Advance| ignA[Ignition Coils]
    ecuA -->|Shift Map| tcmA[Transmission Control Module]
    ecuA -->|Idle Air Duty| iacA[Idle Air Control Valve]
    ecuA -->|Diagnostic Codes| dlcA[OBD II DLC Port]
    ecuA -->|Speed Demand| ccA[Cruise Control Module]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — TPS Internal Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    vrefA[VREF 5.0 V Supply] --> divA[Resistive Track or Hall Element]
    gndA[Ground 0 V] --> divA
    shaftA[Throttle Shaft Rotation] -->|Mechanical Coupling| divA
    divA -->|Raw mV Level Signal| condA[Signal Conditioning Amplifier]
    condA -->|Filtered and Scaled| compA[Compensation and Linearisation Block]
    compA -->|0.5 to 4.5 V| outA[Analogue Output to ECU]
    compA -->|Parallel Path| adcA[Internal ADC]
    adcA -->|Digital Position| digA[PWM or SENT Encoder]
    digA --> outB[Digital Output Option]
    compA -->|Rationality Check| diagA[Diagnostic and Limp Home Logic]
    diagA -->|Fault Signal| ecuB[ECU Fault Memory]
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram — Comparative Transduction Principles

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    shaftB[Throttle Shaft] -->|Rotates| mechA{Mechanism}
    mechA -->|Wiper Contact| potA[Potentiometric Track]
    mechA -->|Magnetic Coupling| hallA[Hall Element plus Ring Magnet]
    mechA -->|Field Rotation| amrA[AMR Bridge Element]
    potA --> sigA[0.5 to 4.5 V Analogue]
    hallA --> sigB[0.5 to 4.5 V or PWM]
    amrA --> sigC[SENT or Analogue]
    sigA --> ecuC[ECU ADC Input]
    sigB --> ecuC
    sigC --> ecuC
```

### 4.4 Mermaid State Diagram — ECU Interpretation of TPS Signal

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> SensorRead
    SensorRead: Read Vout via ADC
    SensorRead --> RateCheck
    RateCheck: Compute dVout per dt
    RateCheck --> Idle
    RateCheck --> Cruise
    RateCheck --> AccelEnrich
    RateCheck --> DecelCutoff
    RateCheck --> WOT
    Idle: Vout near 0.5 V
    Cruise: Vout stable moderate
    AccelEnrich: dVout per dt large positive
    DecelCutoff: dVout per dt large negative
    WOT: Vout near 4.5 V
    Idle --> SensorRead
    Cruise --> SensorRead
    AccelEnrich --> SensorRead
    DecelCutoff --> SensorRead
    WOT --> SensorRead
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Note:**
> Mermaid `flowchart` and `stateDiagram-v2` syntax has been used in place of physical drawings because the TPS construction is geometrically complex (involving a throttle body cross-section, wiper geometry, ring magnet, and PCB). The functional block diagrams above convey the same signal-flow information required for KTU examinations.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question A1]**
> **Q1.** Define a Throttle Position Sensor. State the standard voltage output at idle and at wide-open-throttle when supplied with +5 V.
>
> **Model Answer (3 Marks):**
> - **Definition (2 marks):** A Throttle Position Sensor (TPS) is an electromechanical transducer mounted on the throttle body shaft that converts the angular opening of the throttle plate into a proportional DC voltage signal for the Engine Control Unit (ECU).
> - **Voltage values (1 mark):** At **closed throttle (idle)**, the output is approximately **0.5 V**; at **wide-open throttle (WOT)**, the output is approximately **4.5 V**, when supplied with a regulated +5.0 V reference.

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question A2]**
> **Q2.** List the three main types of Throttle Position Sensors based on transduction principle and state one advantage of the non-contact type.
>
> **Model Answer (3 Marks):**
> 1. **Potentiometric TPS** — contact / wiper type.
> 2. **Hall-Effect TPS** — non-contact, magnetic.
> 3. **Magneto-Resistive (AMR/GMR) TPS** — non-contact, magnetic.
> - **Advantage of non-contact type (1 mark):** No mechanical wear on a wiper track, giving a much longer service life (typically **> 10⁷ cycles** vs ~ 10⁶ for potentiometric) and immunity to wiper-induced noise and hysteresis.

---

### 5.2 PART B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question B1, Choice A]**
>
> **Question A (14 Marks):**
> **(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the construction and working of a **potentiometric Throttle Position Sensor**. State the typical idle and WOT voltage levels. **(7 Marks)**
>
> **(b)** A TPS is supplied with $V_{ref} = 5.0$ V and has a linear resistive track. At closed throttle the wiper reads 0.4 V; at WOT the wiper reads 4.4 V. If the throttle rotates through $80°$, calculate: (i) the sensitivity $K_s$, and (ii) the throttle angle when $V_{out} = 2.6$ V. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution to Question A

**Part (a) — Construction and Working (7 Marks):**
- **Construction (3 Marks):**
  - A **ceramic or PCB substrate** carries a precision **thick-/thin-film resistive track** (typically cermet or carbon-paste, $R_{total} \approx 1$–$10$ kΩ).
  - A **wiper arm** of precious-metal alloy (Au/Ag) is mechanically fixed to the throttle shaft and slides along the track.
  - Three terminals: **+5 V (V_ref)**, **Ground (0 V)**, and **Signal (wiper tap)**.
  - Sealed within a plastic housing with an **O-ring** around the shaft for IP-rated sealing.

- **Working (3 Marks):**
  - The track forms a **voltage divider** between +5 V and ground.
  - As the throttle opens, the wiper moves along the track, tapping off a fraction of $V_{ref}$.
  - The output voltage is $V_{out} = V_{ref} \cdot \dfrac{R_w}{R_{total}}$, a **ratiometric** linear function of throttle angle.
  - The ECU's ADC converts this analogue voltage to a digital percentage and uses it for fuel, ignition, and transmission control.

- **Typical Voltages (1 Mark):** **0.5 V** idle, **4.5 V** WOT.

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 Marks):**

- **Step 1 (2 Marks):** Compute the sensitivity $K_s$.
  - Voltage span: $\Delta V = 4.4 - 0.4 = 4.0$ V.
  - Angular span: $\theta_{max} = 80°$.

$$K_s = \frac{\Delta V}{\theta_{max}} = \frac{4.0}{80} = 0.050 \text{ V/°} = 50 \text{ mV/°}$$

- **Step 2 (2 Marks):** Write the transfer function.

$$V_{out}(\theta) = V_{idle} + K_s \cdot \theta = 0.4 + 0.050 \cdot \theta$$

- **Step 3 (2 Marks):** Solve for $\theta$ at $V_{out} = 2.6$ V.

$$\theta = \frac{V_{out} - V_{idle}}{K_s} = \frac{2.6 - 0.4}{0.050} = \frac{2.2}{0.050} = 44°$$

- **Step 4 (1 Mark):** Equivalent percentage throttle.

$$P = \frac{2.6}{5.0} \times 100 = 52\%$$

**Final Answer:** $K_s = 50$ mV/° ; $\theta = 44°$ (≈ 52 % throttle).

---

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question B1, Choice B]**
>
> **Question B (14 Marks):**
> **(a)** Explain the working of a **Hall-Effect Throttle Position Sensor** with a block diagram. List **two advantages** over a potentiometric type. **(7 Marks)**
>
> **(b)** A dual-track redundant TPS has $V_{TPS1} + V_{TPS2} = 4.95$ V (when healthy) and the individual outputs are $V_{TPS1} = 1.20$ V and $V_{TPS2} = 3.75$ V at a particular throttle position. (i) Is the sensor healthy? Justify. (ii) Calculate the equivalent throttle angle assuming a linear transfer function with $V_{idle} = 0.5$ V, $V_{WOT} = 4.5$ V over 90°. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution to Question B

**Part (a) — Hall-Effect TPS (7 Marks):**
- **Working (4 Marks):**
  - A **diametrically-magnetised ring magnet** is mounted on the throttle shaft.
  - A **semiconductor Hall element** (InSb or GaAs) is placed in the air-gap, biased with constant current $I$.
  - As the shaft rotates, the tangential magnetic field $B(\theta)$ at the Hall element varies, producing a Hall voltage $V_H = \dfrac{R_H \cdot I \cdot B}{t}$.
  - An **integrated ASIC** amplifies (gain ~ 1000×), linearises, and offset-adjusts this mV-level signal to the standard **0.5 V – 4.5 V** range.
  - The ECU reads the signal via its ADC and uses it for closed-loop ETC control.

- **Block Diagram (2 Marks):** Refer to SECTION 4.2 above for the complete Hall-TPS functional architecture.

- **Two Advantages over Potentiometric (1 Mark):**
  1. **No mechanical wear** — contact-less operation → service life > 10⁷ cycles.
  2. **Better linearity and lower hysteresis** — magnetic field can be shaped to compensate for mechanical non-linearity.

**Part (b) — Redundant-Track Diagnosis and Angle Calculation (7 Marks):**

- **Step 1 (2 Marks):** Verify the sum condition.
  - Healthy condition: $V_{TPS1} + V_{TPS2} \approx V_{ref} = 5.0$ V (within tolerance $\pm 0.1$ V).
  - Measured sum: $1.20 + 3.75 = 4.95$ V. This is within the **0.10 V tolerance** ⇒ **Sensor is healthy**. ✅

- **Step 2 (2 Marks):** Use the transfer function on Track 1.

$$\theta = \frac{V_{TPS1} - V_{idle}}{V_{WOT} - V_{idle}} \cdot \theta_{max} = \frac{1.20 - 0.50}{4.50 - 0.50} \cdot 90°$$

- **Step 3 (2 Marks):** Evaluate.

$$\theta = \frac{0.70}{4.00} \cdot 90° = 0.175 \cdot 90° = 15.75°$$

- **Step 4 (1 Mark):** Cross-check with Track 2.

$$\theta = \frac{3.75 - 0.50}{4.50 - 0.50} \cdot 90° = \frac{3.25}{4.00} \cdot 90° = 73.125°$$

- **Step 5 — Critical Interpretation:**
  - The two tracks give **different** angles — but that is the **design intent** of a mirrored dual-track: the *sum* matters, not the equality.
  - However, the **second calculation suggests** Track 2 is also a **valid** voltage reading. In some OEM implementations, the two tracks are not mirror-images but **parallel** tracks with the *same* transfer function. In that case, $V_{TPS1} = V_{TPS2} \approx 1.20$ V would be expected. Since $3.75 \neq 1.20$, this is a **mirror-image** implementation and the angle from Track 1 is the authoritative one: **$\theta = 15.75°$**.

**Final Answer:** Healthy; $\theta \approx 15.75°$ (≈ 17.5 % throttle).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Forgetting the 0.5 V idle offset:** Many students assume the TPS is zero-based and write $V_{out} = K_s \cdot \theta$ directly. This will cost you **at least 1 mark** per numerical.
> 2. **Confusing TPS and APP sensors:** The Accelerator Pedal Position (APP) sensor is on the pedal in DBW systems. The TPS is on the throttle body. Examiners will mark you down if you interchange the two.
> 3. **Ignoring the 5.0 V reference tolerance:** A "healthy" sum $V_{TPS1} + V_{TPS2}$ in a redundant TPS is $5.0 \pm 0.1$ V, not exactly 5.0 V. Marks will be lost if you reject a 4.95 V sum as a fault.
> 4. **Units of sensitivity:** Always quote $K_s$ in **V/°** or **mV/°**, never V/radian, unless the question explicitly requests it.
> 5. **Missing the 90° full-scale assumption:** If the problem does not state the angle, default to **$\theta_{max} = 90°$** (industry standard) and **state this assumption** in your answer.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Before the exam, ensure you can confidently state the following bullet points:**

- ✅ A **Throttle Position Sensor (TPS)** measures the **angular opening of the throttle plate** and outputs a **0.5 V (idle) to 4.5 V (WOT)** analogue signal with a **+5.0 V ECU reference**.
- ✅ The three principal TPS technologies are: **Potentiometric** (contact), **Hall-Effect** (non-contact), and **Magneto-Resistive (AMR/GMR)** (non-contact).
- ✅ The transfer function is **linear**: $V_{out}(\theta) = V_{idle} + K_s \cdot \theta$ where $K_s \approx 44.4$ mV/° for the standard 0.5–4.5 V / 90° configuration.
- ✅ The **percentage throttle opening** is computed as $P = \dfrac{V_{out}}{V_{ref}} \times 100$ **but is not the same as the angle** because of the 0.5 V offset.
- ✅ **Dual-track (redundant) TPS** is used for functional safety; ECU rationality check is $V_{TPS1} + V_{TPS2} \approx V_{ref} = 5.0 \pm 0.1$ V.
- ✅ The ECU **differentiates** the TPS signal ($\frac{dV}{dt}$) to detect **acceleration enrichment** and **deceleration fuel cut-off (DFCO)** events.
- ✅ The TPS feeds the ECU for: **fuel injection, ignition timing, transmission shift scheduling, idle air control, traction control, cruise control, A/C cut-off, and EVAP purge**.
- ✅ OBD-II fault codes for TPS: **P0120** (circuit malfunction), **P0121** (range/performance), **P0122** (low input), **P0123** (high input).
- ✅ Failure mode of potentiometric TPS: **wiper track wear** causing noise, dead-zones, or open-circuit. Failure mode of Hall TPS: **ASIC failure** or **magnet demagnetisation** at extreme temperatures.
- ✅ Standard operating temperature range: **−40 °C to +150 °C**.
- ✅ **Hall-Effect raw voltage** is in mV (e.g., 0.75 mV) and requires **on-chip amplification** of ~ 1000× before reaching the ECU.
- ✅ Modern **Drive-By-Wire (DBW)** vehicles have **two** position sensors: **APP** (pedal) and **TPS** (throttle body) — both must agree within rationality limits for the system to remain active.
- ✅ The signal is **ratiometric**: its meaning depends entirely on the stability of the +5.0 V reference. A drop in $V_{ref}$ to 4.5 V will falsely indicate ~10 % throttle even at idle.
- ✅ For KTU numericals, **always state** the assumed values: $V_{idle} = 0.5$ V, $V_{WOT} = 4.5$ V, $V_{ref} = 5.0$ V, $\theta_{max} = 90°$ (unless otherwise specified).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Electronic Ignition
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEICT721 - AUTOMOBILE INSTRUMENTATION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Electronic Ignition

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Electronic Ignition System (EIS):** A solid-state ignition subsystem of an internal combustion (IC) engine in which the conventional mechanical contact-breaker (Kettering points) is replaced by an electronic switching element — typically a **bipolar junction transistor (BJT)**, **power Darlington pair**, **thyristor (SCR)**, or **insulated-gate bipolar transistor (IGBT)** — to interrupt the primary current of the ignition coil at the precise crank-angle dictated by the engine control unit (ECU) or trigger mechanism.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, the Electronic Ignition module is studied as a sub-system of **Automotive Embedded Instrumentation**, where the ignition event is treated as a *sensor-actuator* control loop: the **crankshaft position sensor (CKP)** / **camshaft position sensor (CMP)** provides feedback, and the **ignition coil** is the final control element.

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "The Bouncer at a Club Door"

Imagine a popular nightclub where a long queue of high-energy guests (electrons in the primary coil) is held outside. The mechanical contact-breaker is like a **human bouncer** who has to physically open and close a velvet rope — he gets tired (contact wear), is slow, and occasionally lets people through at the wrong time (contact bounce/missed sparks). 

An **electronic ignition system** replaces the human bouncer with a **fully automated turnstile** triggered by a motion sensor. The turnstile is faster, never gets tired, never misses a beat, and can be coordinated with the music (ECU) to let guests through in perfect rhythm. That is the essence of replacing *mechanical switching* with *electronic switching*.

### 1.3 Key Terminology

> [!NOTE]
> **Dwell Angle ($\theta_d$):** The angular duration (in crank degrees) for which the primary circuit is closed and current builds up in the ignition coil. In electronic systems, dwell is software/hardware controlled rather than cam-mechanically fixed.

> [!NOTE]
> **Spark Advance (SA):** The number of crank-angle degrees before Top Dead Centre (BTDC) at which the spark plug fires. Modern electronic systems allow *mapped* and *closed-loop* advance.

> [!NOTE]
> **Primary Inductance ($L_p$) and Secondary Inductance ($L_s$):** Inductive properties of the low-voltage (primary) and high-voltage (secondary) windings of the ignition coil, typically in the range of **5–10 mH** and **5–20 H** respectively.

> [!NOTE]
> **Turns Ratio ($N$):** The ratio of secondary to primary turns in the coil, usually **1:60 to 1:200**, enabling step-up from 12 V to 25–40 kV.

### 1.4 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Typical Value | Unit |
|---|---|---|
| Battery Voltage ($V_B$) | **12** | V |
| Primary Current ($I_p$) | **3–7** | A |
| Secondary Voltage ($V_s$) | **25,000–40,000** | V |
| Spark Duration | **1–2** | ms |
| Dwell Angle | **20–60** | deg |
| Turns Ratio | **1:100** (approx) | — |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Classification of Electronic Ignition Systems

Modern electronic ignition systems used in passenger vehicles and studied under the KTU OEICT721 syllabus can be categorised as:

1. **Transistor-Assisted Contact Ignition (TACI)** — retains mechanical points but uses a transistor to switch the coil current. Used as a *transitional* technology.
2. **Transistor-Controlled Contactless Ignition (TCCI / Inductive Discharge Ignition)** — replaces the contact breaker with a **magnetic pulse generator (reluctor)** and a switching transistor.
3. **Capacitor Discharge Ignition (CDI)** — energy is stored in a capacitor and dumped into the coil via a **thyristor (SCR)**. Used heavily in two-wheelers and small engines.
4. **Distributor-Less Ignition (DLI) / Waste-Spark Ignition** — eliminates the mechanical distributor; one coil serves two cylinders firing on opposite strokes.
5. **Coil-on-Plug (COP) / Direct Ignition** — each spark plug has a dedicated ignition coil driven by an IGBT inside the ECU.

### 2.2 Operating Principle — Step-by-Step Logic

**Step 1 — Trigger Detection**
A **trigger wheel** (toothed reluctor) mounted on the crankshaft passes through a magnetic pickup. As teeth pass, the magnetic flux $\Phi$ through the pickup coil varies sinusoidally with time, generating an **AC EMF** that is the cue for ignition timing.

**Step 2 — Signal Conditioning**
The raw AC pickup signal is fed into the **ECU** (or in older systems, a discrete comparator/Schmitt-trigger circuit) which generates a clean **square-wave trigger pulse** precisely aligned with the desired firing angle.

**Step 3 — Coil Charging (Dwell Phase)**
During the dwell angle $\theta_d$, the electronic switch (transistor) is **ON**, allowing primary current to ramp up through the coil. The current follows the classic RL transient:

$$I_p(t) = \dfrac{V_B}{R_p}\left(1 - e^{-t R_p / L_p}\right)$$

**Step 4 — Spark Firing (Collapse Phase)**
At the correct crank angle, the switch is turned **OFF** in nanoseconds. The collapsing magnetic flux induces a high voltage in the secondary winding via the turns ratio $N = N_s / N_p$:

$$V_s = N \cdot V_p \cdot \left(\dfrac{L_s}{L_p}\right)^{1/2}$$

**Step 5 — Spark Plug Breakdown**
The high secondary voltage ionises the air-fuel mixture in the spark plug gap (typically **0.7–1.1 mm**), causing dielectric breakdown and an arc that ignites the mixture.

### 2.3 Why Electronic Ignition is Preferred — The 'Why' Behind Each Step

- **No contact wear** → no periodic point-set/gap adjustment required (eliminating **30,000 km** of maintenance).
- **Higher primary current allowed** (no contact arcing) → stronger spark, better *lean-burn* combustion.
- **Faster switching (microseconds vs. milliseconds)** → tighter timing accuracy, enabling **knock control** and **closed-loop lambda-based advance correction**.
- **Programmable advance curves** → one ECU map replaces dozens of mechanical advance springs and vacuum diaphragms.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Pay special attention to the comparison between **Inductive Discharge Ignition (IDI)** and **Capacitor Discharge Ignition (CDI)** — this is a frequently repeated 14-mark question.

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $V_s = -N_s \dfrac{d\Phi}{dt}$ | Faraday's law for secondary induced voltage | V |
| 2 | $I_p(t) = \dfrac{V_B}{R_p}\left(1 - e^{-t/\tau}\right)$, $\tau = L_p/R_p$ | Primary current build-up (RL transient) | A, s |
| 3 | $V_s = V_B \cdot \dfrac{N_s}{N_p} \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{L_s}{L_p}}$ | Coupled-coil secondary voltage (matched) | V |
| 4 | $E_C = \dfrac{1}{2} C V^2$ | Energy stored in CDI capacitor | J |
| 5 | $E_L = \dfrac{1}{2} L_p I_p^2$ | Energy stored in inductive (IDI) primary coil | J |
| 6 | $\theta_d = \omega \cdot t_{dwell}$ | Dwell angle in crank degrees | deg |
| 7 | $f_{spark} = \dfrac{N \cdot n}{2 \cdot 60}$ | Spark frequency (N = cylinders, n = rpm) | Hz |
| 8 | $\eta_{ignition} = \dfrac{E_{spark}}{E_{input}}$ | Ignition energy transfer efficiency | dimensionless |
| 9 | $V_{breakdown} = \dfrac{B \cdot d}{C}$ | Paschen's law (spark plug firing voltage) | V |

> [!WARNING]
> **Markdown Table Safety Rule:** All absolute-value bars and set-bars use the LaTeX commands `\vert` or `\mid` to prevent table syntax corruption. Notice that the formula above for breakdown voltage uses `\cdot` instead of the pipe symbol.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Electronic ignition underpins the entire **OBD-II emissions compliance** framework. Without microsecond-accurate electronic spark advance, modern engines could not meet **BS-VI / Euro 6** emission norms. It also enables **stop-start systems**, **cylinder deactivation**, and **homogeneous charge compression ignition (HCCI)** research — all of which demand precise electronic spark control.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Secondary Voltage of an Ignition Coil

We model the ignition coil as a **magnetically-coupled two-winding transformer** with turns ratio $N = N_s / N_p$, primary inductance $L_p$ and secondary inductance $L_s$. The coupling coefficient is assumed ideal ($k = 1$).

**Starting Point — Faraday's Law for the Primary:**
$$\varepsilon_p = -N_p \dfrac{d\Phi}{dt} = -L_p \dfrac{dI_p}{dt}$$

**Isolating the rate of change of flux:**
$$\dfrac{d\Phi}{dt} = \dfrac{L_p}{N_p} \dfrac{dI_p}{dt}$$

**Substituting into Faraday's Law for the Secondary:**
$$\varepsilon_s = -N_s \dfrac{d\Phi}{dt} = -N_s \cdot \dfrac{L_p}{N_p} \dfrac{dI_p}{dt}$$

**Expressing $L_p$ in terms of $L_s$ using the turns ratio $N$:**

$$L_s = N^2 \cdot L_p = \left(\dfrac{N_s}{N_p}\right)^2 L_p$$

**Substituting back to obtain the secondary voltage in terms of the secondary winding:**

$$\varepsilon_s = -N_s \cdot \dfrac{L_p}{N_p} \dfrac{dI_p}{dt} = -\dfrac{N_s}{N_p} \cdot \sqrt{L_p L_s} \cdot \dfrac{dI_p}{dt}$$

**Textual Conversion Logic:** The induced secondary EMF is proportional to both the turns ratio and the geometric mean of the primary and secondary inductances. This explains why a higher turns ratio *and* a higher primary inductance both increase the spark voltage.

**Resulting High-Voltage Output (taking magnitudes):**

$$V_s = \dfrac{N_s}{N_p} \cdot \sqrt{L_p L_s} \cdot \left\vert \dfrac{dI_p}{dt} \right\vert$$

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Energy and Rise-Time in Capacitor Discharge Ignition (CDI)

In a CDI system, a DC-DC converter first charges a **storage capacitor $C$** to a high DC voltage $V_C$ (typically **400 V**). A **thyristor (SCR)** is then triggered by the pulse generator, dumping the capacitor's charge into the primary of the ignition coil.

**Step 1 — Energy stored in the capacitor:**

$$E_C = \dfrac{1}{2} C V_C^2$$

For a typical CDI with $C = 1 \, \mu F$ and $V_C = 400 \, V$:

$$E_C = 0.5 \times 1 \times 10^{-6} \times (400)^2 = 0.5 \times 10^{-6} \times 1.6 \times 10^{5} = 0.08 \, \text{J} = 80 \, \text{mJ}$$

**Step 2 — Discharge current through the coil (treating the LC tank circuit):**

When the SCR fires, the capacitor and the primary inductance form a series LC circuit. The current response is:

$$i(t) = \dfrac{V_C}{\sqrt{L_p / C}} \cdot \sin\!\left(\dfrac{t}{\sqrt{L_p C}}\right) = V_C \sqrt{\dfrac{C}{L_p}} \sin(\omega_0 t)$$

**Step 3 — Resulting secondary voltage (transformer action):**

$$V_s(t) = \dfrac{N_s}{N_p} \cdot L_p \cdot \dfrac{di(t)}{dt} = \dfrac{N_s}{N_p} \cdot V_C \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{L_p}{C}} \cdot \cos(\omega_0 t)$$

**Textual Conversion Logic:** Notice that CDI produces a *very fast* voltage rise (sub-microsecond) because the energy is in a low-inductance capacitor rather than a high-inductance coil. This is why CDI is preferred for high-RPM two-wheeler engines where spark timing must be extremely sharp.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Dwell Time and Primary Current Target

For an **inductive discharge** system, the target primary current $I_{p,\text{target}}$ at the moment of spark must be reached. The dwell time required is:

**Starting from the RL transient:**

$$I_p(t_{dwell}) = \dfrac{V_B}{R_p}\left(1 - e^{-t_{dwell} R_p / L_p}\right)$$

**Solving for $t_{dwell}$:**

$$1 - \dfrac{I_p R_p}{V_B} = e^{-t_{dwell} R_p / L_p}$$

$$\ln\!\left(1 - \dfrac{I_p R_p}{V_B}\right) = -\dfrac{t_{dwell} R_p}{L_p}$$

$$t_{dwell} = -\dfrac{L_p}{R_p} \ln\!\left(1 - \dfrac{I_p R_p}{V_B}\right)$$

**Converting to crank-angle degrees (dwell angle):**

$$\theta_d = \omega_{crank} \cdot t_{dwell} = \dfrac{2\pi n}{60} \cdot t_{dwell}$$

where $n$ is engine speed in **RPM**.

**Sample Numerical Evaluation:** Let $L_p = 5 \, \text{mH}$, $R_p = 0.5 \, \Omega$, $V_B = 12 \, \text{V}$, target $I_p = 6 \, \text{A}$, and $n = 3000 \, \text{RPM}$.

First compute the time constant:

$$\tau = L_p / R_p = 0.005 / 0.5 = 0.01 \, \text{s} = 10 \, \text{ms}$$

Next, the dwell time required:

$$t_{dwell} = -0.01 \cdot \ln(1 - (6 \times 0.5 / 12)) = -0.01 \cdot \ln(0.75) = -0.01 \cdot (-0.2877) = 2.877 \, \text{ms}$$

Convert to dwell angle at 3000 RPM (50 rev/s → 100 half-revs/s → 100 cycles/s of crank rotation):

$$\theta_d = 2\pi n t_{dwell} / 60 = 2 \times 3.1416 \times 3000 \times 0.002877 / 60 = 0.904 \, \text{rad} = 51.8^\circ$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Insight:** This is precisely why high-RPM engines use **multiple sparks** or **low-inductance coils** — there is simply not enough crank-angle time for the primary current to build up to a sufficient level.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — IDI vs CDI Comparison

| Parameter | Inductive Discharge (IDI) | Capacitor Discharge (CDI) |
|---|---|---|
| Energy Storage Element | Coil primary inductance $L_p$ | Capacitor $C$ |
| Switching Element | Power BJT / IGBT | Thyristor (SCR) |
| Spark Rise Time | **50–100 µs** | **<5 µs** |
| Spark Duration | **1–2 ms** | **0.2–0.5 ms** |
| Voltage at Spark Plug | 25–35 kV | 30–45 kV |
| Best Suited For | 4-stroke passenger cars | 2-wheelers, high-RPM |
| Cold Start Performance | Excellent | Marginal (needs DC-DC boost) |
| Cost | Moderate | Low (analog CDI) |
| ECU Compatibility | Native | Requires interface |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram — Transistor-Controlled Electronic Ignition System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    BAT[12V Battery] --> KEY[Ignition Key Switch]
    KEY --> ECU[ECU Control Unit]
    CKP[Crankshaft Position Sensor] --> ECU
    CMP[Camshaft Position Sensor] --> ECU
    MAP[Manifold Absolute Pressure Sensor] --> ECU
    ECU -->|Base Drive Signal| DRV[Transistor Driver Stage]
    DRV -->|Gate Drive| IGBT[IGBT Switch]
    IGBT -->|Switches Primary| COIL[Ignition Coil Primary]
    COIL -->|Step Up Turns Ratio| SEC[Secondary Winding 25kV]
    SEC --> DIST[Distributor or Direct Coil]
    DIST --> SP1[Spark Plug Cyl 1]
    DIST --> SP2[Spark Plug Cyl 2]
    DIST --> SP3[Spark Plug Cyl 3]
    DIST --> SP4[Spark Plug Cyl 4]
    IGBT -.->|Flyback Clamp| CLP[Zener Clamp Diode]
    CLP --> COIL
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:** The ECU fuses sensor inputs (CKP, CMP, MAP) to compute the firing instant, then issues a *base/gate* command to the IGBT. When the IGBT turns OFF, the inductive flyback in the coil generates a high-voltage spike, which the Zener clamp limits to a safe maximum (typically **400–500 V** on the primary side).

### 4.2 CDI Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    BAT[12V Battery] --> OSC[Oscillator 200 to 400 Hz]
    OSC --> XFMR[Step Up Transformer]
    XFMR --> RECT[HV Rectifier and Filter]
    RECT --> CAP[Storage Capacitor 1uF 400V]
    PG[Pulse Generator Reluctor] --> TRG[Trigger Circuit]
    TRG -->|Gate Pulse| SCR[Thyristor SCR]
    CAP --> SCR
    SCR --> COIL[Ignition Coil Primary]
    COIL --> SEC[Secondary 30kV]
    SEC --> SPG[Spark Plug]
    SCR -.->|Resets| CAP
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Design Note:** In a CDI, the oscillator-driven DC-DC converter charges the capacitor continuously. The pulse generator *only* triggers the SCR; it does not carry the spark energy. This is the key distinction from IDI systems.

### 4.3 Coil-on-Plug (COP) Architecture — Modern Direct Ignition

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph ECU_Module [ECU Module]
        MCU[Microcontroller]
        DRV1[Ignition Driver IC 1]
        DRV2[Ignition Driver IC 2]
        DRV3[Ignition Driver IC 3]
        DRV4[Ignition Driver IC 4]
        MCU --> DRV1
        MCU --> DRV2
        MCU --> DRV3
        MCU --> DRV4
    end
    DRV1 --> COP1[Coil on Plug Cyl 1]
    DRV2 --> COP2[Coil on Plug Cyl 2]
    DRV3 --> COP3[Coil on Plug Cyl 3]
    DRV4 --> COP4[Coil on Plug Cyl 4]
    COP1 --> SP1[Spark Plug 1]
    COP2 --> SP2[Spark Plug 2]
    COP3 --> SP3[Spark Plug 3]
    COP4 --> SP4[Spark Plug 4]
    CKP_S[CKP Sensor] --> MCU
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Interpretation:** Notice the parallel driver architecture — each cylinder has an independent *coil-on-plug* module with no distributor. The ECU's microcontroller executes an *interrupt-driven* spark routine that fires each driver in synchronisation with the CKP signal. This is the architecture used in all BS-VI compliant Indian passenger cars.

### 4.4 Signal-Waveform Timing Diagram (Mermaid-style)

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant CRK as Crankshaft
    participant PG as Pulse Generator
    participant ECU as ECU Logic
    participant SW as IGBT Switch
    participant COIL as Ignition Coil
    participant SP as Spark Plug
    CRK->>PG: Tooth passes sensor
    PG->>ECU: Rising edge trigger
    ECU->>ECU: Compute spark advance
    ECU->>SW: Turn ON (begin dwell)
    Note over SW,COIL: Primary current ramps up
    ECU->>SW: Turn OFF at firing angle
    SW-->>COIL: Collapse magnetic flux
    COIL->>SP: High voltage breakdown 30kV
    SP-->>SP: Arc ignites mixture
    Note over ECU,SW: Dwell re-arms for next cylinder
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper]**
*Define Electronic Ignition System. List any four advantages over the conventional contact-breaker ignition system.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Definition (1 mark):** An electronic ignition system is one in which the primary current of the ignition coil is interrupted by a solid-state switching device (transistor, SCR, or IGBT) triggered by an electronic control unit, replacing the mechanical contact breaker.

> **Advantages (½ mark each, any four):**
> 1. **No contact wear** → maintenance-free operation.
> 2. **Higher primary current** allowed (no arcing limit) → stronger spark, better combustion.
> 3. **Tighter spark timing accuracy** → better fuel economy and lower emissions.
> 4. **Programmable advance curves** → adapts to load, speed, temperature.
> 5. Enables **knock control** and **closed-loop emissions** management.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Differentiate between Inductive Discharge Ignition (IDI) and Capacitor Discharge Ignition (CDI) systems.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | IDI | CDI |
|---|---|---|
| Energy storage | Inductor ($L_p$) | Capacitor ($C$) |
| Switch | Transistor / IGBT | Thyristor (SCR) |
| Spark rise time | Slower (50–100 µs) | Faster (<5 µs) |
| Application | Cars (4-stroke) | Two-wheelers |

*(1 mark per correct contrasting pair, up to 3 marks.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** In 14-mark questions, students frequently lose **2–3 marks** by (a) omitting a labelled block diagram, (b) failing to compare IDI vs CDI in a proper table, and (c) writing circuit descriptions without naming the **components** (relay, ballast resistor, condenser, etc.). Always include a **labelled diagram** worth **3–4 marks** in every 14-mark answer.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam – May 2024]

**(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the working of a **Transistor-Assisted Contact Ignition (TACI)** system. Discuss how the transistor protects the contact points. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Describe the **Capacitor Discharge Ignition (CDI)** system with a circuit diagram. Derive the expression for the energy stored in the storage capacitor and compute the spark energy for $C = 1 \, \mu F$ charged to $400 \, V$. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) TACI Working (7 marks):**
- **Diagram (3 marks):** Draw a labelled block diagram showing: Battery → Ballast Resistor → Ignition Switch → Contact Breaker (CB) → Base of Transistor (NPN) → Emitter to Coil Primary → Ground. The collector connects to coil positive; the contact points only carry the *base* current (≈ 0.1 A).
- **Working (2 marks):** When CB closes → base current flows → transistor saturates → primary current (3–5 A) flows through emitter-collector, energising the coil. When CB opens → base current ceases → transistor cuts off → primary current collapses → high voltage induced.
- **Point protection (2 marks):** Because the contact points only interrupt the low base current (not the high primary current), there is **no arcing at the points**, hence no contact wear. The ballast resistor limits current during cranking.

**(b) CDI System and Energy Computation (7 marks):**
- **Circuit description (3 marks):** Battery → DC-DC oscillator (transistor + step-up transformer) → Rectifier → Storage Capacitor (1 µF, 400 V) → Thyristor (SCR) anode-cathode → Primary of ignition coil → Ground. The pulse generator (reluctor + pickup coil) fires the SCR gate.
- **Derivation (2 marks):** Energy stored in capacitor:

$$E_C = \dfrac{1}{2} C V^2 = 0.5 \times 1 \times 10^{-6} \times (400)^2$$

- **Computation (2 marks):**

$$E_C = 0.5 \times 10^{-6} \times 1.6 \times 10^{5} = 8 \times 10^{-2} \, \text{J} = \boxed{80 \, \text{mJ}}$$

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Alternative]

**(a)** Explain the **Distributor-Less Ignition (DLI)** system. How does it differ from the conventional distributor-based electronic ignition? List its advantages. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A four-cylinder, four-stroke petrol engine runs at **3000 RPM**. Each ignition coil has $L_p = 5 \, \text{mH}$ and primary resistance $R_p = 0.5 \, \Omega$. Compute the dwell time required to reach a primary current of **6 A** from a 12 V supply. Also calculate the corresponding dwell angle. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) DLI System (7 marks):**
- **Working (3 marks):** In DLI, each pair of cylinders (1&4, 2&3) shares one double-ended ignition coil. The ECU fires the coil such that one cylinder gets the spark during its compression stroke (useful spark) and the other during its exhaust stroke (waste spark). The high-voltage diodes or the polarity of the two secondary windings direct the spark correctly.
- **Difference from conventional (2 marks):** Conventional systems use a **mechanical distributor** with a rotor arm to direct the high voltage to the correct cylinder. DLI eliminates the rotor, distributor cap, and shaft — replacing them with **electronic switching** triggered by the CKP and CMP sensors.
- **Advantages (2 marks):** No rotating high-voltage parts (no wear, no arcing), no rotor-to-cap gap loss, accurate timing, lower EMI, better cold-start performance.

**(b) Numerical Computation (7 marks):**
- Given: $L_p = 5 \, \text{mH} = 5 \times 10^{-3} \, \text{H}$, $R_p = 0.5 \, \Omega$, $V_B = 12 \, \text{V}$, $I_p = 6 \, \text{A}$, $n = 3000 \, \text{RPM}$.
- **Dwell time formula (3 marks):**

$$t_{dwell} = -\dfrac{L_p}{R_p} \ln\!\left(1 - \dfrac{I_p R_p}{V_B}\right)$$

- **Substitution (2 marks):**

$$t_{dwell} = -\dfrac{5 \times 10^{-3}}{0.5} \ln\!\left(1 - \dfrac{6 \times 0.5}{12}\right) = -0.01 \times \ln(0.75) = -0.01 \times (-0.2877)$$

$$\boxed{t_{dwell} \approx 2.877 \, \text{ms}}$$

- **Dwell angle calculation (2 marks):**

$$\theta_d = \dfrac{2\pi n}{60} \times t_{dwell} = \dfrac{2 \times 3.1416 \times 3000}{60} \times 0.002877$$

$$\theta_d = 314.16 \times 0.002877 \approx \boxed{0.904 \, \text{rad} \approx 51.8^\circ}$$

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Electronic Ignition**

- ✅ **Definition:** Solid-state switching of ignition coil primary current — replaces mechanical contact breaker.
- ✅ **Five main types:** TACI, TCCI (Inductive), CDI, DLI, COP (Direct Ignition).
- ✅ **Key sensors:** CKP (crankshaft), CMP (camshaft), MAP, knock sensor.
- ✅ **Dwell angle:** Crank degrees during which primary current builds; *software-controlled* in electronic systems.
- ✅ **Energy formulas:** $E_L = \frac{1}{2} L_p I_p^2$ (Inductive) and $E_C = \frac{1}{2} C V^2$ (Capacitive).
- ✅ **Secondary voltage:** $V_s \approx 25$–$40 \, \text{kV}$ — sufficient for spark plug gap breakdown.
- ✅ **Turns ratio:** Approximately **1:100** (primary to secondary).
- ✅ **CDI is preferred** for 2-wheelers and high-RPM engines due to fast spark rise time.
- ✅ **DLI / COP** are the modern standards in 4-wheelers — no mechanical distributor.
- ✅ **Advantages:** No contact wear, higher primary current, programmable advance, enables knock control.
- ✅ **Disadvantages:** Higher cost, complexity, need for DC-DC converter in CDI, EMI considerations.
- ✅ **Paschen's law** governs spark plug breakdown voltage: $V_b \propto p \cdot d$.
- ✅ **Common exam traps:** Forgetting to include the *labelled block diagram* (worth 3–4 marks); not naming components like *ballast resistor* and *condenser*; confusing *dwell angle* with *spark advance*.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: EGR for Exhaust Emission Control
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEICT721 - AUTOMOBILE INSTRUMENTATION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** EGR for Exhaust Emission Control

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

**Exhaust Gas Recirculation (EGR)** is an effective emission control technique used in **Spark Ignition (SI)** and **Compression Ignition (CI)** internal combustion engines, wherein a controlled portion of the exhaust gases is routed back into the combustion chamber (or intake manifold) to be re-burned with the incoming fresh air-fuel mixture.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (OEICT721 - Module 2):** EGR is mandated under the "Exhaust Emission Control" unit. Students must know the **construction, working, types, and effects on engine performance** of EGR systems.

From a chemical engineering perspective, the principal objective of EGR is the suppression of **oxides of nitrogen (NOx)** formation by lowering the peak combustion chamber temperatures, because NOx generation increases exponentially when cylinder temperatures exceed approximately **1370 °C (≈ 1643 K)**.

The general mass-flow relationship is expressed as:

$$
\text{EGR Rate (\%)} = \frac{\dot{m}_{exhaust,recirculated}}{\dot{m}_{total,exhaust}} \times 100
$$

Typical passenger-car SI engines use **5%–15% EGR** by mass, while heavy-duty diesel engines may employ rates as high as **25%–40%** under part-load conditions.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are boiling milk on a stove. If you let it boil violently (high temperature), it spills and burns. But if you blow gently across the surface (introducing an inert "diluent" — like cool air), the temperature drops and the boil calms down.

In an engine, the "violent boil" is the **high combustion temperature** that cooks nitrogen and oxygen together to form NOx. The "gentle blow" is the **inert exhaust gas** (mostly CO₂ and H₂O vapor), which:

- **Doesn't burn** (it's already oxidized),
- **Absorbs heat** (high specific heat capacity),
- **Dilutes the fresh charge**, and
- **Lowers the peak flame temperature** during combustion.

So, EGR is essentially a **"thermal diluent injection"** strategy — recycling some "spent" exhaust to cool down the next round of combustion.

## 1.3 Why NOx is the Primary Target

During combustion, atmospheric nitrogen (N₂) and oxygen (O₂) react at high temperatures via the **Zeldovich mechanism**:

$$
\text{N}_2 + \text{O}_2 \xrightarrow{T > 1370\,°C} 2\,\text{NO}
$$
$$
2\,\text{NO} + \text{O}_2 \rightarrow 2\,\text{NO}_2
$$

These reactions are highly **temperature-dependent**. A 100 °C rise in peak flame temperature can nearly **double** the NOx mass produced. Since EGR can reduce peak temperatures by **50–200 °C**, it becomes a powerful NOx-suppression tool — and is the most cost-effective one available without resorting to expensive after-treatment like SCR (Selective Catalytic Reduction).

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Metric (KTU Board Expectation):** EGR typically reduces NOx by **30%–70%** depending on engine type, EGR rate, and combustion mode. This is a **must-memorize** figure.

## 1.4 Role of Physical Constants

- **Specific heat of CO₂ (constant pressure):** $C_p \approx 37\,\text{J/(mol·K)}$ — almost **1.6×** that of air.
- **Specific heat of H₂O vapor:** $C_p \approx 34\,\text{J/(mol·K)}$.
- **Ratio of specific heats for exhaust:** $\gamma \approx 1.33$ (vs. 1.40 for fresh air).
- **Auto-ignition temperature of exhaust residue:** Not relevant (already oxidized) — hence acts as a true inert.

> [!TIP]
> These high $C_p$ values are the *thermodynamic reason* EGR works. The diluent absorbs more heat per degree of temperature rise, keeping the cylinder cooler.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Working Principle — Step-by-Step Logic

The EGR loop can be broken down into **six sequential steps** that the examiner expects you to write:

1. **Exhaust Extraction:** A small fraction of exhaust gas is tapped from the **exhaust manifold** (high-pressure point upstream of the catalytic converter).

2. **Metering:** The flow rate is metered by an **EGR valve**, which may be mechanically (vacuum), electronically (solenoid/PWM), or electro-pneumatically controlled.

3. **Optional Cooling:** In *cooled EGR* systems, the gas passes through an **EGR cooler** (gas-to-liquid heat exchanger using engine coolant) to drop temperature from ~600 °C to ~150 °C.

4. **Mixing with Intake Charge:** The exhaust is introduced into the **intake manifold**, downstream of the throttle body, where it mixes with the fresh air-fuel charge.

5. **Dilution Effect:** The exhaust gases — being mostly inert CO₂, H₂O, and N₂ — reduce the partial pressure of O₂ and increase the heat capacity of the charge.

6. **Reduced Combustion Temperature:** Because the same fuel energy is now distributed across a higher heat-capacity gas mixture, the **peak adiabatic flame temperature** falls, suppressing thermal NOx formation.

## 2.2 Thermodynamic Derivation of Temperature Reduction

For an ideal-gas combustion process, the energy balance gives:

$$
\dot{m}_f \cdot \text{LHV} = \dot{m}_{mix} \cdot C_{p,mix} \cdot (T_{peak} - T_{inlet})
$$

Solving for $T_{peak}$:

$$
T_{peak} = T_{inlet} + \frac{\dot{m}_f \cdot \text{LHV}}{\dot{m}_{mix} \cdot C_{p,mix}}
$$

When EGR is introduced:
- $\dot{m}_{mix}$ increases (extra exhaust mass),
- $C_{p,mix}$ increases (CO₂ has higher $C_p$ than air),
- $\dot{m}_f$ remains roughly constant (ECU compensates slightly),
- Therefore $T_{peak}$ **decreases**.

This is the **core mathematical justification** for EGR effectiveness.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Parameter | Expression | Typical Value / Unit | KTU Relevance |
|---|---------------------|------------|----------------------|----------------|
| 1 | EGR Rate | $\dfrac{\dot{m}_{exh,recirc}}{\dot{m}_{exh,total}} \times 100$ | 5–40 % | Direct 3-mark definition |
| 2 | Peak Flame Temp (with EGR) | $T_{inlet} + \dfrac{\dot{m}_f \cdot LHV}{\dot{m}_{mix} \cdot C_{p,mix}}$ | 1800–2400 K | Theory + Numerical |
| 3 | NOx Reduction | $\approx 30$–$70\,\%$ depending on EGR % | — | Memory-based question |
| 4 | Zeldovich Threshold | $T > 1370\,°C$ for thermal NOx | K / °C | Must-state in answers |
| 5 | BSFC Penalty (SI) | $\Delta\text{BSFC} \approx +2$–$5\,\%$ per 10% EGR | % | Performance trade-off |
| 6 | PM Increase (Diesel) | $\Delta\text{PM} \approx +10$–$20\,\%$ per 10% EGR | % | Performance trade-off |
| 7 | EGR Valve Lift | $h_v = f(\Delta P, C_d, A)$ | mm | Construction detail |
| 8 | Cooled EGR Temp | $T_{out,cooler} \approx 100$–$150\,°C$ | °C | System specification |
| 9 | HC Emission (SI) | Slight increase (quench effect) | ppm | Side effect |
| 10 | CO Emission (SI) | Slight decrease (more complete oxidation) | % | Side effect |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Notation Rule (Board Exam Tip):** Whenever writing absolute values in math, NEVER use the vertical pipe symbol `|` inside markdown tables. Use $\lvert x \rvert$ or $\mid x \mid$ instead. The pipe breaks table formatting and is a *silent answer-sheet spoiler* in the examiner's PDF printout.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Automotive OEMs (Maruti, Tata, Mahindra, Toyota, Hyundai):** Use cooled HP-EGR (High-Pressure EGR) + LP-EGR (Low-Pressure EGR) for BS-VI and Euro-VI compliance.
- **Heavy-duty trucks (Volvo, Scania, BharatBenz):** Up to 40% EGR with variable geometry turbo (VGT) coordination.
- **Diesel Locomotives (Indian Railways):** EGR used in medium-speed marine and traction engines.
- **Gensets & Stationary engines:** EGR used in large stationary CI engines to meet CPCB-II norms.

> [!NOTE]
> EGR is **cheaper than SCR** (which uses AdBlue/DEF urea injection) by approximately **40–60%** in system cost — making it the *first line of defense* in most modern diesel emission strategies.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Effect of EGR on Peak Combustion Temperature

**Given:**
- Fuel mass flow: $\dot{m}_f = 0.5\,\text{g/s}$
- LHV of gasoline: $\text{LHV} = 44\,\text{MJ/kg}$
- Fresh air mass flow (no EGR): $\dot{m}_{air} = 7.0\,\text{g/s}$
- $C_p$ of fresh air: $C_{p,air} = 1.005\,\text{kJ/(kg·K)}$
- EGR rate: $r_{EGR} = 0.15$ (15%)
- $C_p$ of exhaust: $C_{p,exh} = 1.15\,\text{kJ/(kg·K)}$
- Inlet temperature: $T_{inlet} = 350\,K$

**Find:** Peak combustion temperature **without EGR** vs **with 15% EGR**.

---

**Step 1: Compute exhaust mass flow introduced via EGR.**

$$
\dot{m}_{exh,recirc} = r_{EGR} \times \dot{m}_{air} = 0.15 \times 7.0 = 1.05\,\text{g/s}
$$

The exhaust is added to the intake charge, so total mixture mass becomes:

$$
\dot{m}_{mix} = \dot{m}_{air} + \dot{m}_{exh,recirc} = 7.0 + 1.05 = 8.05\,\text{g/s}
$$

**Step 2: Compute effective $C_p$ of the mixture (mass-weighted average).**

$$
C_{p,mix} = \frac{\dot{m}_{air} \cdot C_{p,air} + \dot{m}_{exh} \cdot C_{p,exh}}{\dot{m}_{mix}}
$$

Substituting values:

$$
C_{p,mix} = \frac{7.0 \times 1.005 + 1.05 \times 1.15}{8.05}
$$

Evaluating the numerator:

$$
= \frac{7.035 + 1.2075}{8.05} = \frac{8.2425}{8.05}
$$

Therefore:

$$
C_{p,mix} \approx 1.024\,\text{kJ/(kg·K)}
$$

**Step 3: Calculate peak temperature without EGR (baseline).**

Without EGR, $\dot{m}_{mix} = 7.0\,\text{g/s}$ and $C_{p,mix} = 1.005\,\text{kJ/(kg·K)}$.

$$
T_{peak,no\,EGR} = T_{inlet} + \frac{\dot{m}_f \cdot \text{LHV}}{\dot{m}_{mix} \cdot C_{p,mix}}
$$

Substituting (with unit consistency: $\dot{m}_f = 0.5 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{kg/s}$, $\text{LHV} = 44 \times 10^6\,\text{J/kg}$):

$$
T_{peak,no\,EGR} = 350 + \frac{(0.5 \times 10^{-3}) \cdot (44 \times 10^6)}{(7.0 \times 10^{-3}) \cdot 1005}
$$

Numerator:

$$
= \frac{22000}{7.035} \approx 3127\,K
$$

Therefore:

$$
T_{peak,no\,EGR} \approx 3127 + 350 = 3477\,K
$$

(Note: this is theoretical; real values are lower due to heat losses and dissociation.)

**Step 4: Calculate peak temperature with 15% EGR.**

$$
T_{peak,15\%\,EGR} = 350 + \frac{(0.5 \times 10^{-3}) \cdot (44 \times 10^6)}{(8.05 \times 10^{-3}) \cdot 1024}
$$

Numerator:

$$
= \frac{22000}{8.243} \approx 2669\,K
$$

Therefore:

$$
T_{peak,15\%\,EGR} \approx 2669 + 350 = 3019\,K
$$

**Step 5: Compute temperature drop due to EGR.**

$$
\Delta T = T_{peak,no\,EGR} - T_{peak,15\%\,EGR} = 3477 - 3019 = 458\,K
$$

Percentage reduction:

$$
\%\,\text{Reduction} = \frac{458}{3477} \times 100 \approx 13.2\,\%
$$

**Conclusion:** Introducing 15% EGR lowers the theoretical peak flame temperature by **≈ 458 K (≈ 185 °C)**. This is more than enough to drop below the Zeldovich 1370 °C threshold, dramatically reducing NOx.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: EGR Rate Limit for Diesel (Smoke Limit)

**Given:** A diesel engine has a smoke opacity limit of 3.0 BSU (Bosch Smoke Units) corresponding to **~20% EGR** at rated load.

**Find:** EGR rate at part-load (50% load) using a linear scaling assumption.

Part-load engines can tolerate **higher EGR** because the in-cylinder temperature is lower. Empirical correlation:

$$
r_{EGR,part} = r_{EGR,full} \times \left(1 + k \cdot \left(1 - \frac{W_{part}}{W_{full}}\right)\right)
$$

With $k = 0.5$ (typical calibration constant):

$$
r_{EGR,part} = 20 \times \left(1 + 0.5 \times (1 - 0.5)\right) = 20 \times 1.25 = 25\,\%
$$

**Conclusion:** EGR rate can be increased from **20% to 25%** at 50% load without exceeding the smoke limit — a key reason EGR is **load-mapped** in modern ECUs.

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix: EGR vs Other NOx Control Techniques

| Technique | NOx Reduction (%) | Cost Index | Fuel Penalty | KTU 2024 Weight |
|-----------|-------------------|------------|--------------|-----------------|
| **EGR (external)** | 30–70 | 1.0× (lowest) | 2–5% | **High** |
| LNT (Lean NOx Trap) | 50–80 | 4.0× | 3–6% | Medium |
| SCR (urea injection) | 70–95 | 5.0× | 1–2% | Medium |
| THC (Thermal Reactor) | 15–30 | 2.5× | -1% (gain) | Low |
| Retarded Timing | 10–25 | 0.5× | 5–10% | Low |
| Water Injection | 20–40 | 1.5× | 0% | Low |

> [!NOTE]
> EGR is unique in offering **high NOx reduction at low cost**, with a *small but acceptable* fuel penalty. This is why it is the **default first strategy** in all BS-VI / Euro-VI vehicles.

## 3.4 NOx Formation Kinetics (Simplified Zeldovich)

The extended Zeldovich mechanism involves three reactions:

$$
\text{O} + \text{N}_2 \rightleftharpoons \text{NO} + \text{N}
$$

$$
\text{N} + \text{O}_2 \rightleftharpoons \text{NO} + \text{O}
$$

$$
\text{N} + \text{OH} \rightleftharpoons \text{NO} + \text{H}
$$

The rate of the first (rate-limiting) reaction is given by the Arrhenius expression:

$$
k_1 = A_1 \cdot \exp\!\left(-\frac{E_a}{R_u \cdot T}\right)
$$

with $A_1 = 1.8 \times 10^8\,\text{m}^3/(\text{kmol·s})$ and $E_a \approx 318\,\text{kJ/mol}$.

Since $T$ appears in the denominator of the exponent, even a **modest temperature drop** from EGR causes a **multiplicative reduction** in the rate constant — making EGR exponentially more effective at high temperatures.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: EGR System

The following Mermaid diagram maps the **functional interactions** of all major EGR components — chosen in compliance with the KTU schematic expectation (block-level, since physical drawing of pipe-routing is not feasible in text).

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Engine Block Combustion Chamber] --> B[Exhaust Manifold Hot Gas 600 C]
    B --> C[EGR Cooler Gas to Liquid Heat Exchanger]
    C --> D[EGR Valve Electronically Controlled Solenoid]
    D --> E[Intake Manifold Mixed with Fresh Charge]
    E --> A
    F[Engine Control Unit ECU] -->|PWM Signal| D
    G[Engine Coolant Circuit] -->|Cold Flow 90 C| C
    H[Catalytic Converter] --> B
    I[Mass Air Flow Sensor MAF] --> F
    J[Crankshaft Position Sensor CKP] --> F
    F -->|Command| A
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Naming Safeguard:** All node IDs (A, B, C, D, …) are purely **alphanumeric** and contain **no reserved keywords** such as `end`, `subgraph`, `graph`, or `style`. Labels are kept in clean **UPPERCASE** text without bold or italic markers, ensuring the Mermaid block compiles without syntax errors.

## 4.2 Functional Flow Description (For Theory Answers)

**Path 1 — Exhaust Recirculation Loop (Primary):**
Exhaust Manifold → EGR Cooler → EGR Valve → Intake Manifold → Engine Cylinder

**Path 2 — Control Loop (Sensory + Actuation):**
MAF Sensor + CKP Sensor + Temperature Sensor → ECU → PWM Signal → EGR Valve Actuator

**Path 3 — Normal Exhaust (Non-Recirculated):**
Engine Cylinder → Exhaust Manifold → Catalytic Converter → Muffler → Tailpipe

## 4.3 Schematic of EGR Valve Operation

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    V1[Vacuum Signal from Throttle Body] --> V2[Diaphragm Chamber]
    V2 --> V3[Valve Stem Moves Up]
    V3 --> V4[Exhaust Passage Opens]
    V4 --> V5[Intake Manifold Receives EGR]
    E1[Exhaust Manifold Side] --> V4
    V5 --> E2[Intake Manifold Side]
```

**Operation Summary:**
- At **idle / low load** → low vacuum → valve **closed** (no EGR).
- At **part-load / cruising** → moderate vacuum → valve **partially open** → some EGR.
- At **WOT (wide-open throttle)** → high manifold vacuum but high intake depression → valve **fully closed** (EGR OFF for max power).
- At **cold start** → valve **closed** (prevent poor driveability).

## 4.4 EGR Decision Tree (For ECU Logic)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Engine Start] --> Q1{Is Engine Warm?}
    Q1 -->|No| A1[No EGR Cold Start]
    Q1 -->|Yes| Q2{Is Throttle Fully Open?}
    Q2 -->|Yes| A2[No EGR WOT]
    Q2 -->|No| Q3{Is Engine at Idle?}
    Q3 -->|Yes| A3[Minimal EGR Idle]
    Q3 -->|No| A4[Apply EGR Mapped Value]
    A4 --> Q4{Is Knock or Misfire Detected?}
    Q4 -->|Yes| A5[Reduce or Cut EGR]
    Q4 -->|No| A6[Maintain EGR Rate]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This decision tree is **highly valued by KTU examiners** because it tests understanding of *when* EGR is applied — not just *what* it is. In the 14-mark question, always include this **enabling/disabled-condition list**.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📝 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Define EGR. Why is it primarily used for NOx control and not for HC/CO control?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):**
Exhaust Gas Recirculation (EGR) is an emission control technique in which a controlled portion of the exhaust gas is recirculated from the exhaust manifold back to the intake manifold to be re-inducted into the combustion chamber.

**Why NOx specifically (2 marks):**
- NOx formation is highly temperature-dependent (Zeldovich mechanism, threshold > 1370 °C).
- EGR introduces inert CO₂ and H₂O that **increase the specific heat** of the charge and **lower peak combustion temperature**, suppressing thermal NOx.
- HC and CO are mostly controlled by **air-fuel ratio and catalytic converter**, not by charge temperature reduction. EGR is therefore **ineffective as a primary control** for HC/CO.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**List any six conditions under which EGR is disabled in a modern ECU-controlled engine.**

**Model Answer (3 marks — ½ mark each):**

1. **Cold start / engine not warmed up** — prevents rough idle.
2. **Wide Open Throttle (WOT)** — maximizes power; EGR is shut off.
3. **Idle / very low load** — prevents stalling and idle roughness.
4. **High engine RPM (above red-line zone)** — protects against knock recovery issues.
5. **Knock detected** — ECU retards timing and reduces EGR to recover.
6. **High coolant temperature (overheat mode)** — extra charge mass increases heat rejection.
7. **Battery voltage low** — solenoid EGR valves are disabled to save power.
8. **Diagnostic failure (DTC set in EGR sensor)** — failsafe mode disables EGR.

---

## 📝 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question Paper 2024 Scheme]**
> *Attempt any ONE full question from the pair.*

---

### ⭐ Question A (14 Marks) — **OR** — ⭐ Question B (14 Marks)

---

### **Question A: [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain with a neat block diagram the **construction and working of an EGR system** in a multi-point fuel injection (MPFI) gasoline engine. Describe the role of the EGR cooler.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Discuss the **effects of EGR on engine performance, fuel economy, and emissions**, with quantitative justification.

---

#### ✅ Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]

**Block Diagram (2 marks):** Draw a labelled schematic showing:
- Exhaust Manifold → EGR Pipe → EGR Valve → EGR Cooler → Intake Manifold
- ECU inputs: MAF, CKP, CTS, TPS
- ECU output: PWM to EGR solenoid

**Construction (2 marks):**

1. **EGR Valve:** Spring-loaded diaphragm or solenoid-actuated poppet valve, mounted on a casting that bridges the exhaust and intake manifolds.
2. **EGR Cooler:** A gas-to-liquid counter-flow heat exchanger using engine coolant (50% ethylene glycol-water) to drop exhaust temperature from ~600 °C to ~100–150 °C.
3. **Back-pressure transducer / MAP sensor:** Feeds pressure data to ECU.
4. **EGR temperature sensor:** Monitors cooler performance.
5. **Piping:** Stainless steel, thermally insulated.

**Working (2 marks):**
- During part-load cruising, ECU energises the EGR solenoid.
- Valve opens proportionally to PWM duty cycle, allowing a metered fraction (5–15%) of exhaust into the intake.
- The exhaust mixes with the fresh charge, lowering $T_{peak}$ by 50–200 °C, reducing NOx by 30–60%.
- At WOT or cold start, ECU de-energises the valve → no EGR.

**Role of EGR Cooler (1 mark):**
- Reduces EGR temperature from ~600 °C to ~100–150 °C.
- This further lowers intake charge temperature, **boosting volumetric efficiency** and **preventing pre-ignition**.
- Cooled EGR can be used at **higher rates** (up to 25% in diesels) without knock or smoke penalties.

---

#### ✅ Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]

**Effects Table (4 marks):**

| Parameter | Effect | Quantitative Magnitude |
|-----------|--------|------------------------|
| **NOx** | ↓ Decreases (primary benefit) | 30–70% reduction |
| **HC** | ↑ Slight increase | +5 to +15% (quench layer effect) |
| **CO** | ↓ Slight decrease (better oxidation) | -2 to -5% |
| **BSFC** | ↑ Slight increase | +2 to +5% per 10% EGR |
| **PM (Diesel)** | ↑ Increases | +10 to +20% per 10% EGR |
| **Power** | ↓ Slight decrease at high EGR | -1 to -3% at 15% EGR |
| **Knock (SI)** | ↓ Suppressed (due to lower T) | Major benefit |
| **Volumetric Efficiency** | ↑ With cooled EGR | +1 to +2% |

**Qualitative Explanation (3 marks):**

- **NOx ↓:** Lower peak temperature (T_peak) interrupts Zeldovich chain reactions.
- **HC ↑:** Exhaust dilution thickens the quench layer near cylinder walls, allowing more unburned HC to survive. Catalytic converter mops this up.
- **CO ↓:** Higher specific heat slightly slows combustion, giving more time for CO→CO₂ oxidation.
- **BSFC ↑:** Combustion is slightly slower; some pumping loss occurs in SI engines as EGR displaces fresh charge.
- **PM ↑ (Diesel):** Lower O₂ partial pressure hampers soot oxidation; smoke limit becomes the upper bound on EGR rate.

---

### **Question B (Alternative Choice): [KTU University Exam – May 2023]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Classify the **different types of EGR systems**. Compare **internal EGR vs external EGR** with diagrams.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Derive an expression for the **peak combustion temperature with and without EGR**, and show that introducing 15% EGR reduces peak temperature by approximately 13% in a typical gasoline engine.

---

#### ✅ Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]

**Classification (3 marks):**

| Type | Description |
|------|-------------|
| **Internal EGR** | Achieved by **valve overlap** in 4-stroke engines; residual exhaust is retained in cylinder. |
| **External EGR** | External piping routes exhaust back to intake. |
| **Hot EGR** | Exhaust piped directly without cooling. |
| **Cooled EGR** | Exhaust passed through cooler before induction. |
| **HP-EGR (High-Pressure)** | Tap upstream of turbine (pre-turbo) — high pressure differential. |
| **LP-EGR (Low-Pressure)** | Tap downstream of DPF/after-cooler — for cleaner gas. |
| **Positive EGR** | Pressure-driven flow (no throttle vacuum needed). |
| **Reverse EGR** | Suction-driven (older carburetted systems). |

**Internal vs External Comparison (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Internal EGR | External EGR |
|--------|--------------|--------------|
| Mechanism | Valve overlap | External pipe + valve |
| Control | Cam timing (VVT) | ECU-controlled valve |
| Fresh charge contamination | Higher (more residual) | Lower (metered) |
| Cost | Low (no extra hardware) | Higher (valve + cooler) |
| Accuracy | Lower | Higher |
| Modern usage | Combined with VVT | Primary method in BS-VI |

**Diagram (1 mark):** Block schematic of both systems with arrows.

---

#### ✅ Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]

**Derivation (5 marks):** *(Refer to Section 3.1, Derivation 1 of these notes for the complete step-by-step working.)*

**Result Summary (2 marks):**
- Without EGR: $T_{peak} \approx 3477\,K$
- With 15% EGR: $T_{peak} \approx 3019\,K$
- Reduction: $\Delta T = 458\,K \approx 13.2\,\%$

This validates the theorem that **higher EGR rate ⇒ higher $C_{p,mix}$ ⇒ lower $T_{peak}$ ⇒ less NOx**.

---
