## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU Students Lose Marks on EGR Questions:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the threshold temperature** (1370 °C / 1643 K) for Zeldovich NOx — losing 1 mark in definitions.
> 2. **Writing EGR affects HC/CO** as if EGR is the *primary* control — wrong framing. Always state *"primary target is NOx; HC/CO are side effects"*.
> 3. **Confusing HP-EGR with LP-EGR** — these are *not* the same; HP-EGR is pre-turbo, LP-EGR is post-DPF. Marks deducted if mixed up.
> 4. **Missing the EGR-disabled conditions** — board examiners *love* asking "when is EGR OFF?" (cold start, WOT, idle, knock). At least 4 must be listed.
> 5. **Forgetting to mention EGR cooler** in MPFI/diesel answers — easy 1-mark loss. Cooler is *mandatory* in modern cooled-EGR systems.
> 6. **Confusing EGR with PCV (Positive Crankcase Ventilation)** — both recirculate gases but from different sources. Don't mix them up!
> 7. **Wrong unit in EGR rate** — always express as **% by mass**, not by volume.
> 8. **Not stating the numerical NOx reduction (30–70%)** — a *must-memorize* value.

---

## 🔁 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — EGR for Exhaust Emission Control**

- ✅ **EGR = Exhaust Gas Recirculation** — recirculates exhaust to intake to reduce NOx.
- ✅ **Primary target = NOx** (thermal, Zeldovich mechanism, threshold 1370 °C).
- ✅ **NOx reduction = 30–70%** depending on engine and EGR rate.
- ✅ **EGR rate:** 5–15% (SI/MPFI), up to 25–40% (CI/Diesel with cooled EGR).
- ✅ **Working:** Exhaust → EGR Cooler → EGR Valve → Intake → Cylinder.
- ✅ **EGR Cooler:** Drops temp from ~600 °C to ~100–150 °C using engine coolant.
- ✅ **EGR OFF conditions:** Cold start, idle, WOT, high RPM, knock, low battery voltage, sensor DTC.
- ✅ **Side effects:** ↑ HC (quench), ↓ CO (better oxidation), ↑ BSFC (+2–5%), ↑ PM in diesel.
- ✅ **Types:** Internal vs External; Hot vs Cooled; HP-EGR vs LP-EGR.
- ✅ **HP-EGR:** Pre-turbo, high pressure differential, modern diesel standard.
- ✅ **LP-EGR:** Post-DPF, cleaner gas, used alongside HP-EGR for BS-VI/Euro-VI.
- ✅ **Formula:** $T_{peak} = T_{inlet} + \dfrac{\dot{m}_f \cdot \text{LHV}}{\dot{m}_{mix} \cdot C_{p,mix}}$.
- ✅ **EGR rate formula:** $\%\text{EGR} = \dfrac{\dot{m}_{exh,recirc}}{\dot{m}_{exh,total}} \times 100$.
- ✅ **Thermodynamic reason:** CO₂ and H₂O have **higher $C_p$** than air → absorb more heat → lower $T_{peak}$.
- ✅ **Cost advantage:** EGR is ~40–60% cheaper than SCR with comparable NOx reduction at low loads.
- ✅ **Modern integration:** Always paired with VGT (Variable Geometry Turbo), DPF, and SCR in BS-VI diesel engines.
- ✅ **Sensors involved:** MAF, MAP, CKP, CTS, EGR temperature sensor.
- ✅ **Actuator:** PWM-controlled solenoid EGR valve.
- ✅ **Difference from PCV:** EGR = exhaust to intake; PCV = crankcase blowby to intake.
- ✅ **Effect on knock:** Reduces knock tendency in SI engines (a hidden benefit).
- ✅ **Volumetric efficiency:** Slightly decreases with hot EGR; increases with cooled EGR.

> **🎯 Final Exam Tip:** Always end an EGR answer with a closing line such as:
> *"EGR is the most cost-effective primary NOx control strategy, forming the cornerstone of modern BS-VI/Euro-VI emission architectures, often integrated with VGT, DPF, and SCR in a layered after-treatment chain."*
> This single sentence can fetch you **1–2 grace marks** in borderline cases!
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Automobile Chassis Electronic Control System: Principles of Electronic Braking
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEICT721 - AUTOMOBILE INSTRUMENTATION
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Automobile Chassis Electronic Control System: Principles of Electronic Braking

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Electronic Braking System (EBS)** is an automobile chassis-mounted electro-hydraulic (or electro-mechanical) closed-loop control system that uses real-time sensor feedback, electronic control unit (ECU) logic, and high-speed solenoid actuators to regulate the braking force applied at each wheel independently, in order to optimize vehicle deceleration, directional stability, and wheel slip within safe physical limits.

According to the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus for **OEICT721 – Automobile Instrumentation**, the **Principles of Electronic Braking** form a critical sub-module under *Automobile Chassis Electronic Control Systems*. The umbrella term encompasses the following sub-systems:

| Sub-System | Full Form | Primary Function |
|---|---|---|
| **ABS** | Anti-lock Braking System | Prevents wheel lock-up under hard braking |
| **EBD** | Electronic Brake-force Distribution | Optimally distributes brake torque between axles |
| **TCS / ASR** | Traction Control / Anti-Slip Regulation | Prevents wheel spin during acceleration |
| **ESC / ESP** | Electronic Stability Control / Program | Corrects vehicle yaw and skidding |
| **BBW / EBB** | Brake-by-Wire / Electronic Brake-by-Wire | Replaces mechanical/hydraulic linkage with electrical signal |
| **RBS** | Regenerative Braking System | Recovers kinetic energy into electrical energy |

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The "Reflexive Hand" on a Skating Rink**
>
> Imagine you are pushing a friend on a skateboard. If you push *too hard* with your palm, the wheels lock, the board spins, and your friend falls. A trained coach, however, applies a **pulsating** grip — squeeze, release, squeeze, release — keeping the wheels at the very edge of grip. The **Anti-lock Braking System (ABS)** is that coach, but it does this **up to 25 times per second** per wheel, using wheel-speed sensors, an ECU, and hydraulic solenoid valves.

A traditional mechanical brake is a *blind* system — the driver pushes the pedal, hydraulic pressure travels through fixed pipes, and equal (or proportionally fixed) force reaches all four wheels regardless of road conditions. An **electronic braking system** introduces **intelligence** between the driver's foot and the wheel cylinder. It continuously asks:

- *How fast is each wheel rotating?*
- *Is the wheel about to lock?*
- *Is the car beginning to spin (yaw)?*
- *What is the brake pedal position?*

It then *individually* and *continuously* modulates hydraulic pressure — **increase, hold, decrease** — at each wheel.

### 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Engineering Metrics (highlighted for KTU exams):**
> - ABS cycle frequency: **15 – 25 Hz** (15–25 pressure corrections per second per wheel)
> - Slip ratio target during ABS: **λ ≈ 0.10 to 0.30** (10%–30%)
> - Maximum friction coefficient road (dry asphalt): **μ ≈ 0.8 – 1.0**
> - Brake fluid (DOT 4) boiling point: **≥ 230 °C**
> - Hydraulic line pressure (passenger car): **8 – 18 MPa**
> - Wheel speed sensor output: **AC sinusoidal, 7 mA per Hz** (typical variable reluctance type)
> - CAN bus message latency for ABS: **< 5 ms**

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Core Control Philosophy — Slip Ratio (λ)

The fundamental variable that *every* electronic braking algorithm tries to control is the **slip ratio (λ)**, defined as the difference between vehicle speed and wheel speed, normalized by the greater of the two.

$$
\lambda \;=\; \frac{V_{\text{vehicle}} - V_{\text{wheel}}}{\max(V_{\text{vehicle}},\, V_{\text{wheel}})}
$$

Where:
- $V_{\text{vehicle}}$ = translational velocity of the vehicle's center of mass (m/s)
- $V_{\text{wheel}} = \omega_{\text{wheel}} \cdot r_{\text{eff}}$ (m/s), with $\omega$ in rad/s and $r_{\text{eff}}$ as effective rolling radius

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Key Insight:** When $\lambda = 0$ → wheel is *rolling free* (no brake). When $\lambda = 1.0$ → wheel is *fully locked*. Peak braking friction typically occurs at $\lambda \approx 0.15$–$0.20$ (the "sweet spot" the ABS ECU chases).

### 2.2 Step-by-Step Operational Logic of the ABS Control Loop

The ABS ECU executes a **closed-loop bang-bang (on-off) controller** in three discrete phases, repeated up to 25 times per second:

**Step 1 — Sensing (Input Phase)**
- **Wheel Speed Sensors (WSS)** — variable reluctance (VR) or Hall-effect sensors — mounted at each wheel hub produce an AC waveform whose **frequency is proportional to wheel speed**.
- **Brake Pedal Position Sensor** (Hall-effect) reports the *driver's demand*.
- **Yaw Rate Sensor** and **Lateral Accelerometer** (only in ESC variants) detect vehicle rotation.

**Step 2 — Decision (ECU Computation Phase)**
- ECU calculates each wheel's deceleration $a_w = d\omega/dt$ and slip ratio $\lambda$.
- If $a_w$ falls below a negative threshold (e.g., $-1.5g$) **OR** $\lambda$ exceeds 0.3, the ECU flags that wheel as **"about to lock"**.

**Step 3 — Actuation (Hydraulic Modulation Phase)**
The hydraulic modulator executes one of three commands via solenoid valves:

| Phase | Solenoid State | Pressure Effect | Physical Action |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Pressure Build** | Inlet valve OPEN, Outlet valve CLOSED | Pressure ↑ | Brake pads clamp harder |
| **Pressure Hold** | Inlet valve CLOSED, Outlet valve CLOSED | Pressure constant | Brake pads hold position |
| **Pressure Release** | Inlet valve CLOSED, Outlet valve OPEN | Pressure ↓ | Brake fluid returns to reservoir |

A **return pump** actively pulls fluid back from the wheel cylinder during release (a critical upgrade over early ABS designs).

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table consolidates **all** examinable mathematical relations for this topic. Memorize the symbols and units — KTU frequently tests unit conversion here.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Wheel linear velocity | $V_w = \omega \cdot r_{\text{eff}}$ | m/s | $\omega$ in rad/s |
| 2 | Slip ratio (braking) | $\lambda = (V_v - V_w)/V_v$ | dimensionless | $V_v \geq V_w$ under braking |
| 3 | Slip ratio (driving) | $\lambda = (V_v - V_w)/V_w$ | dimensionless | For traction control |
| 4 | Longitudinal tire force | $F_x = \mu(\lambda) \cdot F_z$ | N | $F_z$ = vertical load on tire |
| 5 | Braking torque relation | $T_b = F_x \cdot r_{\text{eff}}$ | N·m | Static force balance |
| 6 | Hydraulic pressure (ideal) | $P = F/A$ | Pa | Pascal's principle |
| 7 | Wheel deceleration | $a_w = r_{\text{eff}} \cdot \alpha$ | m/s² | $\alpha = d\omega/dt$ |
| 8 | Vehicle deceleration | $a_v = \mu \cdot g$ | m/s² | $g \approx 9.81$ m/s² |
| 9 | Stopping distance | $d = V_0^2 / (2\mu g)$ | m | Kinematic derivation |
| 10 | WSS frequency | $f = N \cdot \omega / (2\pi)$ | Hz | $N$ = number of tone-wheel teeth |
| 11 | Pedal travel to pressure | $P \approx k \cdot x$ | Pa | Linearized pedal feel |
| 12 | Hydraulic power | $\dot{W} = P \cdot Q$ | W | $Q$ = volumetric flow rate |

> **CRITICAL:** In the table above, vertical bars (e.g., absolute value) are rendered using `\vert` to avoid breaking the markdown table syntax.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The principles discussed above directly influence:
- **Passenger car safety** — Euro NCAP and Bharat NCAP mandate ABS on all four wheels for the highest safety ratings.
- **Heavy commercial vehicles (HCVs)** — Electronic braking replaces pneumatic lines with EBS to reduce stopping distance by up to **15%** at 80 km/h.
- **Electric and hybrid vehicles (EVs/HEVs)** — Brake-by-wire enables seamless blending of *regenerative* (motor as generator) and *friction* braking, recovering **15%–30%** of kinetic energy.
- **Autonomous vehicles (L3+ ADAS)** — AEB (Autonomous Emergency Braking) depends entirely on the same EBS architecture, with the ECU commanded by radar/lidar fusion rather than the driver's foot.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Stopping Distance from First Principles

**Problem Setup (typical KTU 14-mark variant):** *A passenger car traveling at 72 km/h applies maximum braking. The tire-road friction coefficient is 0.8. Calculate the stopping distance and the time taken to stop.*

**Given:**
- Initial velocity: $V_0 = 72 \text{ km/h}$
- Friction coefficient: $\mu = 0.8$
- Gravitational acceleration: $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$

**Step 1 — Convert velocity to SI units**

We must convert kilometers per hour into meters per second. The conversion factor is:

$$
1 \; \text{km/h} \;=\; \frac{1000 \text{ m}}{3600 \text{ s}} \;=\; \frac{5}{18} \text{ m/s}
$$

Applying this to the given velocity:

$$
V_0 \;=\; 72 \times \frac{5}{18} \;\text{m/s} \;=\; 20 \text{ m/s}
$$

The starting velocity is now **20 m/s**.

**Step 2 — Apply Newton's Second Law to find deceleration**

The only horizontal force decelerating the vehicle is kinetic friction. By Newton's second law:

$$
F_{\text{net}} \;=\; m \cdot a
$$

The friction force is:

$$
F_{\text{net}} \;=\; -\mu \cdot m \cdot g
$$

Equating the two expressions:

$$
m \cdot a \;=\; -\mu \cdot m \cdot g
$$

The mass cancels on both sides (a common KTU trick question — "does stopping distance depend on mass?"):

$$
a \;=\; -\mu \cdot g
$$

Substituting numerical values:

$$
a \;=\; -0.8 \times 9.81 \;=\; -7.848 \text{ m/s}^2
$$

The magnitude of deceleration is **7.848 m/s²**.

**Step 3 — Apply the kinematic equation $V^2 = V_0^2 + 2 a d$**

At the stopping point, the final velocity $V = 0$. Substituting:

$$
0 \;=\; V_0^2 + 2 a d
$$

Rearranging to solve for stopping distance $d$:

$$
d \;=\; -\frac{V_0^2}{2 a}
$$

Note: the negative signs cancel because $a$ is already negative. Substituting values:

$$
d \;=\; \frac{(20)^2}{2 \times 7.848} \;=\; \frac{400}{15.696}
$$

$$
\boxed{\,d \;\approx\; 25.48 \text{ m}\,}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute the time to stop using $V = V_0 + a t$**

At rest, $V = 0$:

$$
0 \;=\; 20 + (-7.848) \cdot t
$$

$$
t \;=\; \frac{20}{7.848} \;\approx\; 2.548 \text{ s}
$$

$$
\boxed{\,t \;\approx\; 2.55 \text{ s}\,}
$$

**Verification using energy method (alternative check):** The work-energy theorem gives $F \cdot d = \tfrac{1}{2} m V_0^2$, leading to the same $\mu$-independent-of-mass result, confirming Step 2.

### 3.2 Derivation: Wheel Slip from First Principles

**Given:**
- Vehicle speed: $V_v = 25 \text{ m/s}$
- Wheel rotational speed: $\omega_w = 90 \text{ rad/s}$
- Effective tire radius: $r_{\text{eff}} = 0.32 \text{ m}$

**Step 1 — Compute the wheel's linear velocity**

$$
V_w \;=\; \omega_w \cdot r_{\text{eff}} \;=\; 90 \times 0.32 \;=\; 28.8 \text{ m/s}
$$

**Step 2 — Compare $V_w$ and $V_v$**

Since $V_w > V_v$, the wheel is rotating *faster* than the vehicle translates — this is an **acceleration** slip condition (traction control regime), not a braking slip.

**Step 3 — Apply the driving-slip formula**

$$
\lambda \;=\; \frac{V_w - V_v}{V_w}
$$

Substituting:

$$
\lambda \;=\; \frac{28.8 - 25}{28.8} \;=\; \frac{3.8}{28.8} \;\approx\; 0.1319
$$

$$
\boxed{\,\lambda \;\approx\; 13.2\%\,}
$$

This value lies within the **optimal traction window (10%–15%)**, so the traction control system will not intervene aggressively — only minor torque reduction is required.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Failure Modes vs. Sensor Faults

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Pattern:** "What happens if sensor X fails?" is a recurring 3-mark question.

| Failed Component | ECU Reaction (Fail-Safe) | Driver Noticeable Effect |
|---|---|---|
| One Wheel Speed Sensor | ABS disabled; EBD still active | ABS warning lamp ON; brakes work mechanically |
| All Wheel Speed Sensors | ABS, EBD, ESC disabled | Multiple warning lamps; degraded safety |
| Yaw Rate Sensor | ESC disabled; ABS retained | Reduced cornering intervention |
| Brake Pedal Position Sensor | Brake-by-Wire enters **limp-home** | Increased pedal travel, reduced boost |
| CAN Bus Failure | Master/slave ECU handshake lost | Vehicle may default to hydraulic backup |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Complete Electronic Braking System Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    DRV[Driver Input] -->|Brake Pedal Force| BPS[Brake Pedal Sensor]
    BPS -->|Analog 0 to 5 V| ECU[Brake ECU Master Controller]
    WSS1[Wheel Speed Sensor FL] -->|AC Frequency| ECU
    WSS2[Wheel Speed Sensor FR] -->|AC Frequency| ECU
    WSS3[Wheel Speed Sensor RL] -->|AC Frequency| ECU
    WSS4[Wheel Speed Sensor RR] -->|AC Frequency| ECU
    YRS[Yaw Rate Sensor] -->|CAN Bus| ECU
    LAX[Lateral Accelerometer] -->|CAN Bus| ECU
    ECU -->|PWM Control| HMU[Hydraulic Modulator Unit]
    HMU -->|Pressure Build| WH1[Wheel Cylinder FL]
    HMU -->|Pressure Hold| WH2[Wheel Cylinder FR]
    HMU -->|Pressure Release| WH3[Wheel Cylinder RL]
    HMU -->|Return Pump| WH4[Wheel Cylinder RR]
    ECU -->|Torque Request| VCU[Vehicle Control Unit]
    VCU -->|Regen Blending| INV[Traction Inverter]
    INV -->|Generator Mode| MOT[Drive Motor]
    ECU -->|Diagnostics| DLC[OBD II Diagnostic Link]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid State Diagram — ABS Pressure Modulation Cycle

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> NormalBraking
    NormalBraking --> PressureBuild: Wheel deceleration exceeds threshold
    PressureBuild --> PressureHold: Slip ratio 15 to 20 percent
    PressureHold --> PressureRelease: Slip ratio exceeds 30 percent
    PressureRelease --> PressureBuild: Wheel re-accelerates above threshold
    PressureRelease --> NormalBraking: Vehicle stopped or pedal released
    PressureBuild --> NormalBraking: Driver releases pedal
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Sequence — Sensor-to-Actuator ABS Cycle (One Iteration)

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant Sensor as Wheel Speed Sensor
    participant ECU as ABS ECU
    participant Solenoid as Inlet/Outlet Solenoid
    participant Pump as Return Pump
    participant Wheel as Brake Pad / Wheel
    Sensor->>ECU: AC frequency pulse train
    ECU->>ECU: Calculate slip ratio lambda
    ECU->>ECU: Compare to threshold 0.30
    alt lambda greater than 0.30
        ECU->>Solenoid: CLOSE inlet valve
        ECU->>Solenoid: OPEN outlet valve
        ECU->>Pump: ACTIVATE
        Pump->>Wheel: Release pressure
    else lambda less than 0.30
        ECU->>Solenoid: OPEN inlet valve
        ECU->>Wheel: Build pressure
    end
    ECU->>Sensor: Continue polling at 1 kHz
```

### 4.4 Physical Layout (Textual Schematic Description)

For board-exam diagrammatic questions, draw the following layout and label:

- **Top layer:** Four wheels (circles), each with an associated **Wheel Speed Sensor** mounted on the knuckle/hub.
- **Middle layer:** A central **Brake ECU** block, receiving 4 sensor inputs + 1 pedal input + 2 inertial inputs.
- **Bottom layer:** A single **Hydraulic Modulator Unit (HMU)** containing 3 valves per wheel (inlet, outlet, suction) and a **return pump**.
- **Interconnects:** Three arrows from ECU to HMU: *Build, Hold, Release*. Two arrows from HMU to each wheel cylinder: *Pressure In, Pressure Out*.
- **CAN bus lines:** Drawn as a horizontal backbone connecting ECU to Yaw/Accel sensors and to the rest of the vehicle (powertrain, dashboard).

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Paper Pattern]**

**Q1.** Define the term *Slip Ratio* as applied to an Anti-lock Braking System. What is the typical range of slip ratio maintained by an ABS controller during maximum braking on a dry road?

**Model Answer:**
Slip ratio $\lambda$ is defined as the normalized difference between vehicle translational speed and the linear speed of the wheel contact patch:

$$
\lambda \;=\; \frac{V_v - V_w}{V_v} \quad \text{(braking case)}
$$

It is a dimensionless number ranging from **0** (free rolling) to **1** (fully locked). During ABS intervention on dry asphalt, the ECU regulates $\lambda$ within the **0.10 to 0.30** window, which corresponds to the peak region of the tire-road friction curve. **(3 marks)**

---

**Q2.** List the **three solenoid valve states** in a hydraulic ABS modulator and explain the role of the return pump.

**Model Answer:**
The three states are:
1. **Pressure Build** — Inlet valve open, outlet valve closed → master cylinder pressure admitted to wheel cylinder.
2. **Pressure Hold** — Inlet valve closed, outlet valve closed → pressure locked at current value.
3. **Pressure Release** — Inlet valve closed, outlet valve open → fluid dumped back to reservoir.

The **return pump** actively sucks fluid out of the wheel cylinder during the release phase. Without it, pressure release would rely only on the pedal's return spring, making the cycle too slow (a key design flaw of pre-1978 Bosch ABS prototypes). **(3 marks)**

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> **Choose EITHER Question A OR Question B.**

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Pattern]*

**(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, describe the architecture of an Electronic Braking System (EBS) used in a modern passenger car. Identify and briefly explain the function of **any four** major components. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

The EBS architecture is shown in the Mermaid block diagram in **Section 4.1** of these notes. The four major components are:

1. **Wheel Speed Sensors (WSS):** Variable reluctance (VR) or Hall-effect sensors mounted at each non-driven wheel hub (and on the propeller shaft for driven axles). They generate an AC waveform whose **frequency is proportional to wheel angular velocity**. A 48-tooth tone ring at 1000 rpm yields 800 Hz, allowing the ECU to compute speed with sub-rpm resolution.

2. **Brake Pedal Position Sensor (BPPS):** A dual-redundant Hall-effect sensor providing an analog 0–5 V signal proportional to pedal travel. The ECU uses this as the *driver demand* reference, separate from the *master cylinder pressure*.

3. **Hydraulic Modulator Unit (HMU):** The muscle of the system. Contains **3 solenoid valves per wheel** (inlet, outlet, suction) plus a **DC motor-driven return pump**. The HMU can independently raise, hold, or drop pressure at any of the four wheel cylinders within **5–15 milliseconds**.

4. **ABS/ESC ECU:** A 32-bit automotive-grade microcontroller (e.g., Infineon Aurix) running the slip-control algorithm at a cycle time of **5 ms**. It also handles CAN communication, diagnostics (ISO 14229 UDS), and fail-safe logic. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A four-wheeler moving at 90 km/h is braked to a halt with constant deceleration. The friction coefficient between tire and road is 0.7. Calculate: (i) the stopping distance, (ii) the time taken, and (iii) the wheel slip ratio at the instant the wheel speed has dropped to 22 m/s. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Given:**
- $V_0 = 90 \text{ km/h} = 90 \times \frac{5}{18} = 25 \text{ m/s}$
- $\mu = 0.7$
- $V_w = 22 \text{ m/s}$ at the instant of interest

**(i) Stopping distance:**

$$
d \;=\; \frac{V_0^2}{2 \mu g} \;=\; \frac{(25)^2}{2 \times 0.7 \times 9.81} \;=\; \frac{625}{13.734}
$$

$$
\boxed{\,d \;\approx\; 45.51 \text{ m}\,}
$$

**(ii) Time to stop:**

$$
t \;=\; \frac{V_0}{\mu g} \;=\; \frac{25}{0.7 \times 9.81} \;=\; \frac{25}{6.867}
$$

$$
\boxed{\,t \;\approx\; 3.64 \text{ s}\,}
$$

**(iii) Slip ratio at $V_w = 22$ m/s:**

Since $V_w < V_v$, this is a braking slip:

$$
\lambda \;=\; \frac{V_v - V_w}{V_v} \;=\; \frac{25 - 22}{25} \;=\; \frac{3}{25} \;=\; 0.12
$$

$$
\boxed{\,\lambda \;=\; 12\%\,}
$$

This lies in the optimal ABS band, confirming the system is well-tuned. **(7 marks — 2 + 2 + 3 distribution)**

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – May 2023 Pattern]*

**(a)** Explain the working principle of a **Regenerative Braking System** as used in a Battery Electric Vehicle (BEV). With a block diagram, describe how brake-by-wire coordinates friction and regenerative torque blending. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

In a BEV, the traction motor is electrically reversible — it can act as both a **motor** (driving) and a **generator** (regenerating). When the driver releases the accelerator or presses the brake pedal, the inverter commands the motor to switch into generator mode, converting the vehicle's **kinetic energy into electrical energy** stored in the high-voltage battery.

**Energy recovered (ideal):**

$$
E_{\text{regen}} \;=\; \tfrac{1}{2} m V^2 \cdot \eta_{\text{regen}} \cdot \eta_{\text{mech}}
$$

Where $\eta_{\text{regen}}$ is the generator efficiency (≈ 0.85) and $\eta_{\text{mech}}$ accounts for driveline losses (≈ 0.95).

**Brake-by-Wire Blending Architecture:**

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P[Brake Pedal] -->|Stroke| BPS[Pedal Force Simulator]
    BPS -->|CAN Message| VCU[Vehicle Control Unit]
    VCU -->|Regen Torque Request| INV[Motor Inverter]
    INV -->|Generator Mode| MOT[Traction Motor]
    MOT -->|Charging Current| BAT[HV Battery]
    VCU -->|Friction Supplement| HMU[Hydraulic Modulator]
    HMU -->|Mechanical Pressure| WC[Wheel Cylinders]
```

The VCU first requests **maximum regenerative torque** (up to ≈ 0.3g deceleration). If the driver's demand exceeds this (e.g., emergency stop), the HMU supplements with friction braking. The **pedal force simulator** provides the natural "feel" to the driver even though there is no direct mechanical link to the master cylinder. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare and contrast the **Anti-lock Braking System (ABS)** and **Electronic Stability Control (ESC)** with respect to: (i) primary sensor inputs, (ii) control objective, and (iii) typical actuation. Present the answer in a tabular format. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

| Parameter | **ABS** | **ESC** |
|---|---|---|
| **(i) Primary Sensors** | Wheel Speed Sensors (×4); Brake Pedal Switch | Wheel Speed Sensors (×4); **Yaw Rate Sensor**; **Steering Angle Sensor**; **Lateral Accelerometer** |
| **(ii) Control Objective** | Prevent individual wheel lock-up; maintain steerability during straight-line braking | Prevent vehicle skid/yaw divergence during cornering AND braking; keep vehicle on driver-intended path |
| **(iii) Typical Actuation** | Hydraulic pressure modulation (3-state solenoid valves) at each wheel | Selective **braking** of individual wheels + **engine torque reduction** + (on AWD) torque vectoring |
| **Activation threshold** | Slip ratio > 0.20–0.30 | Yaw rate error > 2–5°/s vs. steering-based reference |
| **Driver override** | None (transparent) | None (transparent) |
| **KTU 2024 weightage** | Often a 3-mark direct question | Often a 7-mark descriptive question |

**(7 marks — 2 + 2 + 3 distribution)**

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Traps (identified from KTU valuation patterns):**
>
> 1. **Unit Conversion Error (-1 mark):** Forgetting to convert km/h → m/s. *Always* multiply by 5/18.
> 2. **Wrong slip-ratio formula (-1 mark):** KTU expects $V_w$ in the denominator for *driving* slip, $V_v$ in the denominator for *braking* slip. Mixing them up is the #1 mistake.
> 3. **Confusing "stopping distance depends on mass" (-1 mark):** On level ground, $d = V_0^2/(2\mu g)$ is *mass-independent*. Many students incorrectly write $d \propto m$.
> 4. **Forgetting the return pump (-0.5 mark):** When describing the HMU, the pump is *not* optional — it is the defining feature of modern ABS vs. early 1970s designs.
> 5. **Incomplete diagram (-1 mark):** Block diagrams must include *all* sensor arrows AND actuator arrows with labels. Unlabelled arrows receive zero credit.
> 6. **Regen vs. friction confusion (-1 mark):** In Part B regenerative braking questions, always state the *energy storage destination* (battery) and the *limitation* (max ≈ 0.3g deceleration).

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist for Last-Minute KTU Prep:**

- ✅ **Slip ratio** $\lambda$ is the single most important variable. Memorize *both* the braking and driving forms.
- ✅ **Optimal λ range for ABS:** 10%–30% (peak friction region).
- ✅ **Three ABS solenoid states:** *Build, Hold, Release* — in that order, repeated 15–25 Hz.
- ✅ **Wheel Speed Sensor (WSS):** VR or Hall-effect; output frequency $f = N \omega / (2\pi)$.
- ✅ **Hydraulic line pressure** (passenger car): 8–18 MPa; pump-driven return is mandatory.
- ✅ **Stopping distance** $d = V_0^2 / (2 \mu g)$ — *mass-independent* on level ground.
- ✅ **Regenerative braking** recovers ≈ 15%–30% of kinetic energy but is capped at ≈ 0.3g; friction braking supplements above that.
- ✅ **Brake-by-Wire** replaces the mechanical pedal-to-master-cylinder link with an electrical signal — but must retain a *pedal force simulator* for driver feel.
- ✅ **ESC** adds yaw-rate and steering-angle sensors to ABS; it can also reduce engine torque.
- ✅ **EBD** (Electronic Brake-force Distribution) is a *software-only* add-on to ABS — no extra hardware.
- ✅ **CAN bus latency** for ABS messages is < 5 ms — this is *why* ABS uses a dedicated ECU and not a general-purpose body controller.
- ✅ **Euro NCAP / Bharat NCAP** mandate ABS for top safety ratings — a frequently tested "industry relevance" point.
- ✅ **DOT 4 brake fluid** boils at ≥ 230 °C; water-contaminated fluid (DOT 3) boils at ≈ 140 °C — *vapor lock* is a real failure mode in Indian driving conditions.
- ✅ **Fail-safe philosophy:** On any single sensor failure, the ECU disables only the *dependent* function; the base hydraulic brake always remains operational.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Automatic Transmission Electronic Control Circuit
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEICT721 - AUTOMOBILE INSTRUMENTATION
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Automatic Transmission Electronic Control Circuit

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Automatic Transmission Electronic Control Circuit (ATECC):** A closed-loop, microprocessor-based electronic control subsystem integrated within a modern automatic transmission assembly that replaces or augments purely hydraulic/mechanical shift logic with real-time sensor telemetry, algorithmic decision-making, and electro-hydraulic actuation to execute gear ratio changes, torque converter lock-up, and line-pressure modulation.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, this is essentially the **Transmission Control Unit (TCU)** architecture — sometimes called an **Electronic Automatic Transmission (EAT)** controller. It is the "brain" that sits between the driver's shift lever input and the actual hydraulic clutches and bands that physically change gears.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **The Elevator Analogy:** Imagine a smart elevator in a 50-story building. The elevator has:
> - **Sensors** (weight, floor buttons, current floor indicator, door status)
> - **Controller** (the small computer that decides which motor to spin, how fast, and when to brake)
> - **Actuators** (motors, brakes, door locks)
>
> The elevator does NOT use a human to manually pull levers for every floor — it **automatically** decides based on inputs. The **Automatic Transmission Electronic Control Circuit works EXACTLY the same way**. Instead of a driver manually pushing a clutch and moving a stick, the car itself decides *when* to shift up or down based on throttle position, vehicle speed, engine load, and brake status, then commands hydraulic solenoids to do the gear change in milliseconds.

## 1.3 The Three Sub-Systems (The Triad)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Every ATECC is built from three functional blocks:
> 1. **INPUT Sub-System** → Sensors (eyes and ears of the system)
> 2. **PROCESSING Sub-System** → ECU/TCU (the brain)
> 3. **OUTPUT Sub-System** → Solenoids & Actuators (the muscles)

## 1.4 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Operating Voltage:** **12 V DC** (nominal automotive bus), with operating range **9 V – 16 V**.
- **Solenoid Coil Resistance:** Typically **2.5 Ω – 5 Ω** (low-side driven).
- **ATF (Automatic Transmission Fluid) Operating Temperature Range:** **−40 °C to +150 °C**; optimal viscosity band is **80 °C – 100 °C**.
- **Shift Execution Time:** **100 ms – 400 ms** for a single up-shift under normal load.
- **Processor Clock Speed:** Modern TCUs use **16-bit / 32-bit microcontrollers** at **40 MHz – 200 MHz**.
- **CAN Bus Data Rate:** **500 kbps** (standard CAN 2.0B for powertrain domain).
- **Pressure Modulation Frequency:** PWM signal at **30 Hz – 300 Hz** for line-pressure and torque-clutch solenoids.
- **Lock-up Clutch Engagement Slip Target:** **20 rpm – 50 rpm** (slip speed before full lock).

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Breakdown — The "Why" and "How"

### 2.1.1 Input Stage — The Sensor Array

The TCU continuously polls a set of sensors. Each sensor transduces a physical quantity into an analog voltage (or digital pulse train) that the ECU's **Analog-to-Digital Converter (ADC)** can read.

| Sensor | Physical Quantity Measured | Output Signal Type | Purpose in ATECC |
|---|---|---|---|
| **VSS (Vehicle Speed Sensor)** | Output shaft rotational speed | Hall-effect square wave, 0–5 V | Primary shift-decision parameter |
| **TSS (Turbine Speed Sensor)** | Torque converter turbine rpm | Hall-effect or magnetic pickup | Detects input clutch slip |
| **TPS (Throttle Position Sensor)** | Accelerator pedal angle (0–100 %) | Variable resistor (potentiometer) → 0–5 V | Determines load → influences shift map |
| **ATF Temp Sensor** | Transmission fluid temperature | NTC thermistor (resistance drops with heat) | Adjusts line pressure & shift timing for viscosity |
| **Brake Light Switch** | Brake pedal state (ON/OFF) | 12 V digital | Cancels overdrive; enables torque converter lock |
| **Shift Lever Position Sensor (PRNDL)** | Driver-selected gear range | Multi-position switch / rotary encoder | Defines operating mode (P, R, N, D, 3, 2, 1) |
| **Manual Downshift Switch (Kick-down)** | Wide-open throttle (WOT) signal | 0 V or 12 V digital | Forces a downshift for passing |

### 2.1.2 Processing Stage — The ECU/TCU Logic

> [!NOTE]
> The TCU executes a **shift map** (also called a **shift schedule** or **shift logic map**) stored in non-volatile memory (Flash/EEPROM). This map is a 2-D lookup table indexed by `Vehicle_Speed × Throttle_Position`.

The TCU's real-time algorithm works as follows:

1. **Read all sensor inputs** via ADC channels and digital I/O pins.
2. **Filter** the inputs (e.g., moving average on VSS to reject noise from wheel slip).
3. **Index the shift map** using current `(VSS, TPS)` coordinate to retrieve the target gear.
4. **Compare** target gear vs current gear.
5. **If change is needed → Command the appropriate shift solenoids** (Solenoid A and Solenoid B in a typical 4-speed automatic) to reposition hydraulic valves.
6. **Modulate line pressure** using a PWM-driven pressure control solenoid, ensuring firm but smooth clutch engagement.
7. **Engage lock-up clutch** (if conditions are met) by energizing the lock-up solenoid.
8. **Loop** at a typical cycle time of **10 ms – 20 ms**.

### 2.1.3 Output Stage — The Actuator Network

| Actuator | Type | Control Signal | Physical Effect |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Shift Solenoid A & B** | On/Off (N.C. or N.O.) hydraulic solenoid | 12 V switched via low-side driver | Routes line pressure to specific clutch packs |
| **Line Pressure Solenoid** | Proportional (current-controlled) | PWM, 0–100 % duty cycle | Modulates main line pressure (3–15 bar) |
| **Torque Converter Lock-Up Solenoid** | Proportional or on/off | PWM | Engages clutch in torque converter for 1:1 coupling |
| **Accumulator Backpressure Solenoid** | Proportional | PWM | Controls shift quality (firmness/softness) |
| **Inhibitor Relay** | Electromechanical relay | 12 V coil | Prevents starter engagement unless in P or N |

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following equations form the analytical core of ATECC operation.

| # | Formula / Relation | Description | Typical Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $v = \frac{\pi \cdot D \cdot N}{60}$ | Vehicle speed from wheel rpm (D = tire diameter, N = wheel rpm) | v in m/s |
| 2 | $f_{VSS} = \frac{N_{output} \cdot P}{60}$ | VSS pulse frequency (P = teeth on tone ring) | Hz |
| 3 | $\tau_{eng} \cdot i_g = \tau_{out}$ | Torque at output = engine torque × gear ratio | N·m |
| 4 | $i_g = \frac{N_{input}}{N_{output}}$ | Gear ratio definition | dimensionless |
| 5 | $P_{line} = k \cdot i_{sol} + P_{min}$ | Line pressure as linear function of solenoid current | bar, A |
| 6 | $\eta_{lock} = 1 - \frac{\Delta N_{slip}}{N_{turbine}}$ | Torque converter lock-up efficiency (slip-based) | dimensionless |
| 7 | $R_{ATF}(T) = R_0 \cdot e^{B \left(\frac{1}{T} - \frac{1}{T_0}\right)}$ | NTC thermistor resistance vs temperature (Steinhart-Hart, simplified) | Ω, K |
| 8 | $D_{PWM} = \frac{T_{on}}{T_{period}} \times 100\%$ | PWM duty cycle (controls solenoid average voltage) | % |
| 9 | $V_{avg} = D_{PWM} \cdot V_{batt}$ | Average voltage seen by proportional solenoid | V |
| 10 | $t_{shift} \approx \frac{\Delta \omega_{input} \cdot J_{driveline}}{\tau_{clutch\_net}}$ | Approximate shift execution time | s, N·m, kg·m² |
| 11 | $P_{hyd} = Q \cdot \Delta P$ | Hydraulic power (Q = flow, ΔP = pressure drop) | W |

> [!NOTE]
> **Use `\vert` or `\mid` in exam scripts for absolute value notation** (e.g., $\vert v \vert$) to avoid markdown table syntax breaking in KTU digital answer sheets.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Fuel Economy:** TCU-optimized shift points and lock-up timing improve highway fuel economy by **5 % – 15 %** compared to non-electronic automatics.
- **Emissions Control:** Earlier lock-up and adaptive shift maps reduce CO₂ and NOx.
- **Drivability:** Adaptive learning algorithms personalize shift firmness to driver style.
- **Diagnostics:** OBD-II (On-Board Diagnostics, **SAE J1962** standard) integration enables real-time fault codes (P07xx series for transmission).
- **Integration with ADAS:** Modern TCUs share data with Engine ECU, ABS/ESC, and traction control over **CAN bus** for coordinated vehicle dynamics.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The two derivations below are the most frequently asked in KTU ESE for this topic. Memorize the logic flow, not just the result.

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Vehicle Speed from VSS Pulse Frequency

**Problem Statement:** A vehicle's VSS tone ring has $P = 48$ teeth. The output shaft rotates at $N_{out} = 1500\ rpm$. The tire diameter (rolling diameter) is $D = 0.65\ m$. Find (a) the VSS pulse frequency and (b) the vehicle speed in km/h.

### Step 1: Relate shaft rotation to pulses

Each tooth of the tone ring produces **one pulse** per revolution of the output shaft. Therefore the number of pulses per second equals teeth per revolution times revolutions per second.

$$f_{VSS} = \frac{N_{out} \cdot P}{60}$$

### Step 2: Substitute numerical values

$$
\begin{aligned}
f_{VSS} &= \frac{1500\ \text{rpm} \times 48\ \text{teeth}}{60\ \text{s/min}} \\
&= \frac{72000}{60} \\
&= 1200\ \text{Hz} = 1.2\ \text{kHz}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Logic:** $1500$ revolutions per minute means $25$ revolutions per second, and $25$ rev/s $\times 48$ teeth = $1200$ pulses per second.

### Step 3: Convert shaft rpm to linear vehicle speed

The vehicle's linear speed equals the circumferential speed of the tire.

$$v = \pi \cdot D \cdot N_{out} \quad \text{(units: m/rev } \times \text{ rev/min → m/min)}$$

To get SI units, convert rpm to rev/s first:

$$
\begin{aligned}
v &= \pi \cdot D \cdot \frac{N_{out}}{60} \\
&= \pi \times 0.65\ \text{m} \times \frac{1500}{60}\ \text{rev/s} \\
&= 3.1416 \times 0.65 \times 25 \\
&= 51.05\ \text{m/s}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4: Convert m/s to km/h

$$
\begin{aligned}
v_{km/h} &= 51.05\ \text{m/s} \times \frac{3600\ \text{s/h}}{1000\ \text{m/km}} \\
&= 51.05 \times 3.6 \\
&\approx 183.8\ \text{km/h}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Answer:** $f_{VSS} = 1200\ \text{Hz}$ and $v \approx 184\ \text{km/h}$.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Line Pressure Solenoid — PWM to Pressure

**Problem Statement:** A line-pressure proportional solenoid has the following characteristics:
- Minimum pressure (D=0 %): $P_{min} = 3.0\ \text{bar}$
- Maximum pressure (D=100 %): $P_{max} = 12.0\ \text{bar}$
- Battery voltage: $V_{batt} = 14.0\ \text{V}$ (alternator charging)
- TCU outputs a PWM at $f = 100\ \text{Hz}$ with duty cycle $D = 45\ \%$.

Find (a) the average solenoid voltage and (b) the commanded line pressure.

### Step 1: Average voltage equation

A PWM signal with duty cycle $D$ delivers an average DC voltage to the load equal to:

$$V_{avg} = D_{PWM} \cdot V_{batt}$$

### Step 2: Substitute values

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{avg} &= 0.45 \times 14.0\ \text{V} \\
&= 6.30\ \text{V}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3: Linear pressure model

For a proportional solenoid, the output pressure varies **linearly** between $P_{min}$ and $P_{max}$ as duty cycle sweeps $0$ to $100\ \%$:

$$P_{line} = P_{min} + D_{PWM} \cdot (P_{max} - P_{min})$$

### Step 4: Substitute values

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{line} &= 3.0\ \text{bar} + 0.45 \times (12.0\ \text{bar} - 3.0\ \text{bar}) \\
&= 3.0 + 0.45 \times 9.0 \\
&= 3.0 + 4.05 \\
&= 7.05\ \text{bar}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Answer:** $V_{avg} = 6.30\ \text{V}$ and $P_{line} = 7.05\ \text{bar}$.

> **Logic Note:** Higher line pressure (closer to $12\ \text{bar}$) is used during **WOT / kick-down** to prevent clutch slip under high torque. Lower pressure (near $3\ \text{bar}$) is used during light load cruising to improve shift smoothness and reduce pump parasitic losses.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Decision Flow for a Typical 4-Speed Automatic

The following table shows how a TCU uses **two shift solenoids (A and B)** to select among four forward gears. Note: `1` = solenoid energized (ON), `0` = de-energized (OFF). The arrangement below corresponds to a representative unit (e.g., Aisin Warner / Ford CD4E-style logic).

| Gear | Solenoid A | Solenoid B | Engaged Clutch(es) | Typical Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **1st** | 0 | 0 | C1 + F (one-way clutch) | Launch from standstill |
| **2nd** | 1 | 0 | C2 + F | Hills, slow speed |
| **3rd** | 0 | 1 | C3 + F | Cruise, mild acceleration |
| **4th (OD)** | 1 | 1 | C4 + F (overdrive) | Highway cruising, best economy |
| **Reverse** | Special case | Special case | Reverse clutch | Reverse motion |

> **Engineering Insight:** The combination pattern `00, 10, 01, 11` is a classic **binary solenoid logic** mapping. This is why the TCU can address 4 gears using only 2 solenoids — a hallmark of optimal actuator design.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — ATECC Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Driver Input - Shift Lever PRNDL] --> B[Shift Lever Position Sensor]
    C[Accelerator Pedal] --> D[Throttle Position Sensor TPS]
    E[Transmission Output Shaft] --> F[Vehicle Speed Sensor VSS]
    G[Torque Converter Turbine] --> H[Turbine Speed Sensor TSS]
    I[ATF Temperature] --> J[ATF Temp Sensor NTC]
    K[Brake Pedal] --> L[Brake Light Switch]
    M[Battery 12V] --> N[Power Supply and 5V Regulator]
    N --> O[TCU Microcontroller 32-bit]
    B --> O
    D --> O
    F --> O
    H --> O
    J --> O
    L --> O
    O --> P[Low-Side Driver Stage]
    P --> Q[Shift Solenoid A]
    P --> R[Shift Solenoid B]
    P --> S[Line Pressure Solenoid PWM]
    P --> T[Lock-Up Clutch Solenoid PWM]
    O --> U[CAN Transceiver TJA1050]
    U --> V[CAN Bus 500 kbps]
    V --> W[Engine ECU]
    V --> X[ABS ESC Module]
    V --> Y[Instrument Cluster]
    O --> Z[Fault Memory EEPROM OBD-II]
    Q --> AA[Hydraulic Valve Body]
    R --> AA
    S --> AA
    T --> AB[Torque Converter Clutch]
    AA --> AC[Planetary Gear Set]
    AC --> AD[Final Output to Driveshaft]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Shift-Map Lookup Concept

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0([Start Loop every 10 ms]) --> A1[Read VSS and TPS]
    A1 --> A2[Read ATF Temp and Brake State]
    A2 --> A3[Apply Input Filters - Moving Average]
    A3 --> A4{Lookup Shift Map VSS x TPS}
    A4 -->|Target Gear equals Current| A5[No Action - Hold]
    A4 -->|Target Gear greater than Current| A6[Command UP-Shift]
    A4 -->|Target Gear less than Current| A7[Command DOWN-Shift]
    A6 --> A8[Energize Solenoid A or B per Binary Map]
    A7 --> A8
    A8 --> A9[Modulate Line Pressure via PWM]
    A9 --> A10{Lock-up Conditions Met?}
    A10 -->|Yes| A11[Engage Lock-Up Solenoid]
    A10 -->|No| A12[Keep Converter Unlocked]
    A11 --> A13[Store DTC if any Fault Detected]
    A12 --> A13
    A5 --> A13
    A13 --> A0
```

## 4.3 Signal Conditioning Path for a Hall-Effect VSS

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Tone Ring Teeth P=48] --> B[Hall-Effect IC]
    B --> C[Open-Collector Output]
    C --> D[Pull-Up Resistor 4.7k to 5V]
    D --> E[Schmitt Trigger Hysteresis]
    E --> F[TCU Input Capture Pin]
    F --> G[Timer Counter Module]
    G --> H[Calculated Frequency and RPM]
    H --> I[Speed Value in Shift Map Index]
```

## 4.4 Solenoid Driver Circuit (Low-Side PWM)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[TCU PWM Output 5V Logic] --> B[Gate Driver IC TLE7201 or similar]
    B --> C[Power MOSFET N-channel]
    C --> D[Solenoid Coil 3 to 5 ohm]
    D --> E[12V Battery Rail]
    C --> F[Flyback Diode 1N5408]
    F --> E
    C --> G[Current Sense Resistor 0.1 ohm]
    G --> H[Differential Amplifier]
    H --> I[TCU ADC Channel - Closed-Loop Current Control]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Design Note on Section 4.4:** The current-sense resistor enables **closed-loop current control**. The TCU reads back the actual solenoid current via ADC and adjusts the PWM duty cycle to maintain the commanded current (and therefore the commanded pressure). This is what makes the line-pressure regulation accurate to within **±0.1 bar** in premium systems.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> - Students **commonly lose 2–3 marks** by forgetting to convert rpm → rev/s (or minutes → seconds) in speed derivations. Always write the unit conversions explicitly.
> - Another frequent error: **confusing VSS (output shaft speed) with TSS (turbine input speed)**. VSS measures output, TSS measures pre-gearbox input. State clearly which shaft you are computing for.
> - In shift-map questions, students often **omit the binary solenoid logic** (which solenoid is ON for which gear). Examiners explicitly allocate **3 marks** for this in 14-mark questions.
> - Always mention the **closed-loop feedback** aspect in TCU answers — saying "TCU commands solenoid" without "and reads back current/pressure" loses 1–2 marks.

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`** Define the function of a Transmission Control Unit (TCU) in an automatic transmission. List any four sensors whose signals it processes.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

A **Transmission Control Unit (TCU)** is a microprocessor-based electronic controller that manages all electronically controlled functions of an automatic transmission, including gear shifting, line-pressure modulation, and torque-converter lock-up. It receives real-time inputs from various sensors, executes a pre-programmed shift logic algorithm, and commands electro-hydraulic solenoids to actuate gear changes.

**Four key sensors (½ mark each):**
1. **Vehicle Speed Sensor (VSS)** — output shaft speed.
2. **Throttle Position Sensor (TPS)** — accelerator pedal angle.
3. **Turbine Speed Sensor (TSS)** — torque converter output speed.
4. **ATF Temperature Sensor** — fluid temperature.

(Alternatives: Brake light switch, Shift lever position sensor, Kick-down switch — any four accepted.)

---

### **Q2. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`** What is a torque converter lock-up clutch? Why is it controlled electronically rather than purely mechanically?

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

A **torque converter lock-up clutch** is a friction clutch integrated inside the torque converter that mechanically couples the engine crankshaft directly to the transmission input shaft, eliminating the **hydraulic slip** of the torque converter and improving fuel economy by **5–10 %** at steady highway speeds.

**Why electronic control (1 mark):**
- Mechanical lock-up engages at a fixed speed, causing **shock and judder**.
- Electronic control allows **gradual, PWM-modulated engagement** at the optimal speed, load, and temperature conditions.
- It can be **disengaged instantly** during tip-out, downshift, or braking for smoother drivability.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module-Internal Choice)

> *Per KTU 2024 ESE pattern, you must answer ONE of the two alternatives.*

---

### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)** — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024]`

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the functional architecture of an **Automatic Transmission Electronic Control Circuit (ATECC)**. Label and describe the input, processing, and output sub-systems. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **binary shift-solenoid logic** used to select four forward gears in a typical electronic automatic transmission. Show how solenoids A and B are energized for each gear. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]

**1. Block Diagram Description (3 marks):**
The ATECC consists of three blocks:
- **Input block** — Sensors: VSS, TPS, TSS, ATF Temp sensor, Brake switch, Shift-lever position sensor.
- **Processing block** — TCU (microcontroller with ADC, timers, CAN, Flash memory holding the shift map).
- **Output block** — Low-side driver MOSFETs driving shift solenoids A & B, line-pressure proportional solenoid, and lock-up clutch solenoid, all interfaced to the hydraulic valve body.

**2. Function of each block (2 marks):**
- **Inputs** convert physical quantities (speed, temperature, pedal position) into electrical signals.
- **Processor** runs the shift algorithm in real time, typically with a **10–20 ms** cycle.
- **Outputs** convert electrical commands into hydraulic motion that repositions clutches and bands in the planetary gear set.

**3. Closed-loop feedback (2 marks):**
The TCU reads back the turbine and output speeds (via TSS and VSS) and confirms whether the commanded gear is actually achieved. If slip exceeds threshold, the TCU modulates line pressure upward and stores a **Diagnostic Trouble Code (DTC)** in EEPROM. Communication with the engine ECU and ABS over **CAN bus** (500 kbps) ensures coordinated torque reduction during shifts (so-called **torque management**).

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]

**1. Concept (2 marks):**
A 4-speed automatic uses **two on/off shift solenoids** to direct pressurized ATF to one of four possible combinations of clutch packs. The solenoids act as a **2-bit binary selector**.

**2. Truth Table (3 marks):**

| Gear | Solenoid A | Solenoid B | Engaged Clutch |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1st | 0 (OFF) | 0 (OFF) | C1 |
| 2nd | 1 (ON) | 0 (OFF) | C2 |
| 3rd | 0 (OFF) | 1 (ON) | C3 |
| 4th (OD) | 1 (ON) | 1 (ON) | C4 |

**3. Working (2 marks):**
When the TCU commands a gear change, it de-energizes the current solenoid combination and energizes the new combination within **100–400 ms**. The hydraulic valve body routes line pressure to the corresponding clutch pack, which locks planetary gear members to produce the desired ratio. The line-pressure solenoid simultaneously adjusts clamping force to prevent slip and ensure smooth engagement.

---

### **QUESTION B (14 Marks)** — `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**(a)** Explain the operation of a **PWM-controlled proportional solenoid** used for line-pressure regulation in an automatic transmission. Derive the relationship between duty cycle and commanded pressure. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With a suitable sketch, describe the **torque-converter lock-up control strategy** in an electronic automatic transmission. State any four conditions under which the TCU will disengage the lock-up clutch. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]

**1. Construction (2 marks):**
A **proportional solenoid** is an electro-hydraulic actuator consisting of a coil, a movable armature, and an orifice. The magnetic force on the armature (proportional to current²) positions a spool that controls the size of a hydraulic bleed orifice. The result: **current in → pressure out**.

**2. PWM Driving (2 marks):**
The TCU drives the solenoid coil with a **PWM signal** at a fixed frequency (typically 100–300 Hz). The average coil current — and therefore the average magnetic force and average output pressure — is proportional to the duty cycle:

$$V_{avg} = D_{PWM} \cdot V_{batt}$$

**3. Pressure vs Duty Cycle Relation (3 marks):**
For a linear proportional solenoid, the output pressure varies linearly between the minimum and maximum design values:

$$P_{line} = P_{min} + D_{PWM} \cdot (P_{max} - P_{min})$$

- At $D = 0\ \%$: $P_{line} = P_{min}$ (typically **3 bar** for light load).
- At $D = 100\ \%$: $P_{line} = P_{max}$ (typically **12–15 bar** for WOT).
- Example: At $D = 50\ \%$ → $P_{line} = 7.5\ \text{bar}$.

The TCU uses **closed-loop current control** via a current-sense resistor to maintain the commanded pressure accurately.

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]

**1. Lock-up operation (3 marks):**
Inside the torque converter, a hydraulically-actuated **lock-up clutch plate** is splined to the engine side of the converter. When the lock-up solenoid is energized, pressurized ATF is routed to the **apply side** of the clutch piston, pressing the friction plate against the converter cover. This creates a **mechanical 1:1 coupling** between engine and input shaft, eliminating converter slip. A typical slip threshold for lock-up is **20–50 rpm**.

**2. Engagement conditions (2 marks):**
The TCU engages the lock-up clutch only when **all** of the following are met:
- Vehicle speed above a calibrated threshold (e.g., $> 50\ \text{km/h}$).
- Throttle position is steady (no rapid pedal movement).
- ATF temperature is in the normal operating range (typically $> 40\ °\text{C}$).
- Transmission is in 3rd or 4th gear (top two ratios).
- Brake pedal is released.
- Engine is not in fuel-cut mode.

**3. Disengagement conditions (2 marks):**
The TCU **disengages** the lock-up clutch under any of:
- **Brake pedal pressed** (to prevent stall on downshift).
- **Throttle tip-out** (sudden accelerator release).
- **Kick-down (WOT) request** for a downshift.
- **ATF temperature out of range** (cold or overheating).
- **Engine braking detected** (off-throttle, low speed).

The use of **PWM ramp-up** during engagement and **PWM ramp-down** during disengagement prevents the **torque-hole** shock (judder) that was common in older non-electronic lock-up systems.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU Exam Day:**

- ☐ ATECC = **TCU + Sensors + Solenoids + Hydraulic valve body**. Three-block architecture: **Input → Process → Output**.
- ☐ The **shift map** is a 2-D lookup table indexed by **Vehicle Speed × Throttle Position** (with ATF temperature and brake state as secondary modifiers).
- ☐ **VSS** measures **output** shaft speed; **TSS** measures **turbine (input)** shaft speed. Never confuse them.
- ☐ Two **on/off shift solenoids** (A, B) address **4 forward gears** via binary combinations `00, 10, 01, 11`.
- ☐ **Line-pressure solenoid** is **proportional** (PWM), not on/off. Pressure equation: $P_{line} = P_{min} + D_{PWM} \cdot (P_{max} - P_{min})$.
- ☐ **Lock-up clutch** engages at steady highway cruise to eliminate torque-converter slip → improves fuel economy **5–10 %**.
- ☐ Lock-up is **disengaged** during braking, kick-down, tip-out, cold ATF, or low gear.
- ☐ TCU communicates with **Engine ECU** and **ABS/ESC** over **CAN bus at 500 kbps** for torque management during shifts.
- ☐ **Closed-loop current control** on solenoids uses a **current-sense resistor** + ADC feedback for accurate pressure regulation.
- ☐ Standard **OBD-II transmission DTCs** are in the **P07xx** range.
- ☐ Key derived formulas to memorize: $v = \pi D N/60$, $f_{VSS} = NP/60$, $P_{line} = P_{min} + D \cdot (P_{max} - P_{min})$, $V_{avg} = D \cdot V_{batt}$.
- ☐ Always state **units** explicitly in derivations (rpm → rev/s, m/s → km/h). Examiners deduct for missing unit conversions.
- ☐ Modern TCUs use **32-bit microcontrollers** at **40–200 MHz** with **10–20 ms** control loop cycle.
- ☐ PWM frequency for proportional solenoids: **30 Hz – 300 Hz**.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Cruise Control Circuit

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OEICT721 - AUTOMOBILE INSTRUMENTATION > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Cruise Control Circuit # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Cruise Control Circuit (CCC):** A closed-loop, negative-feedback electronic control system integrated within a vehicle's powertrain and instrumentation network that automatically regulates the throttle valve opening to maintain a driver-set road speed, compensating for varying tractive resistances (gradient, wind drag, rolling resistance) without continuous driver pedal intervention. In strict KTU 2024 Scheme instrumentation terminology, the Cruise Control Circuit is classified under **Driver Assistance Subsystems (DAS)** and is treated as a classical example of an **Electro-Mechanical Servo-Control Loop** with the following formal architecture: $$\text{Cruise Control} \equiv \text{Set-Point Tracking System with Integral Action}$$ The reference input is the *desired velocity* ($v_{set}$), the controlled output is the *actual road velocity* ($v_{actual}$), and the actuator is the *throttle body servo motor* (or vacuum-operated diaphragm in legacy systems). | Aspect | KTU 2024 Specification | |---|---| | **System Class** | Negative-feedback closed-loop | | **Controller Type** | PI / PID (Proportional-Integral-Derivative) | | **Actuator** | Stepper motor / DC servo / Vacuum actuator | | **Feedback Sensor** | Vehicle Speed Sensor (VSS) — Hall-effect / Magneto-resistive | | **Set-Point Memory** | Non-volatile ECU RAM / EEPROM | --- ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy & Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Plain-English Analogy — "The Smart Thermostat in Your Car"** Imagine you are walking on a treadmill. You want to walk at **exactly 5 km/h**. Now, the treadmill occasionally speeds up (downhill) or slows down (uphill). If you keep adjusting your leg speed manually, you get tired. A **cruise control circuit** is like giving yourself a **smart exoskeleton** that *senses* your current speed (via a speedometer sensor) and *automatically nudges* your legs faster or slower to keep you at the target speed. You simply set "5 km/h" once, and the exoskeleton does the rest. **Mapping the analogy to engineering terms:** | Human / Treadmill Element | Engineering Equivalent | |---|---| | Your target walking speed (5 km/h) | Set-point velocity $v_{set}$ | | Your actual leg speed | Feedback velocity $v_{actual}$ from VSS | | Exoskeleton nudging your legs | Throttle actuator (servo motor) | | The brain deciding "faster/slower" | ECU microcontroller running PID algorithm | | Treadmill disturbances (hills) | Plant disturbances (gradient, drag) | **Key Intuitive Truth:** The circuit never "remembers" a fixed throttle position; it continuously *compares* two speeds and *corrects* the throttle 25–50 times per second. This is why KTU examiners often frame the topic as "a practical implementation of a **PI controller**." --- ## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Physical Constants > [!IMPORTANT] > **Critical Numerical Benchmarks (must memorize for KTU exams):** > - **Speed measurement range:** 0 – 200 km/h (typical passenger car) > - **VSS pulse frequency:** $f_{VSS} = 0.01 \text{ Hz/(km/h)}$ to $0.05 \text{ Hz/(km/h)}$ > - **Sampling rate of ECU:** **40 – 100 Hz** (loop execution) > - **Throttle actuation time:** **< 50 ms** (stepper) or **< 30 ms** (DC servo) > - **Vehicle mass $m$ (mid-size sedan):** $\approx 1500$ kg > - **Aerodynamic drag coefficient $C_d$:** 0.25 – 0.40 > - **Air density $\rho$:** **1.225 kg/m³** (sea level, ISA standard) > - **Gravitational acceleration $g$:** **9.81 m/s²** --- # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Operational Philosophy — The "Why" The cruise control circuit exists because of a fundamental **longitudinal dynamics problem** on a road vehicle. Newton's second law applied to a car moving on a slope is: $$m \frac{dv}{dt} = F_{tractive} - F_{drag} - F_{rolling} - F_{gradient}$$ The driver wishes to maintain $v = v_{set} = \text{constant}$, which implies $\frac{dv}{dt} = 0$. Therefore, the **throttle must be continuously modulated** to balance the resisting forces, which change with speed, gradient, and wind. ## 2.2 Block-Level Architecture (Six Functional Stages) The KTU 2024 syllabus breaks the cruise control circuit into **six canonical stages**. Every examiner's answer should mention these: 1. **Driver Input Stage** — SET, RESUME, ACCEL, DECEL, CANCEL switches (momentary-contact, multiplexed onto a single ECU pin via a resistor ladder). 2. **Reference Velocity Memory (Set-Point Latch)** — A non-volatile memory cell in ECU that stores $v_{set}$ even after engine OFF. 3. **Feedback Acquisition Stage (VSS)** — Hall-effect sensor on transmission output shaft producing a **square-wave pulse train** with frequency proportional to wheel/vehicle speed. 4. **Error Detector / Comparator** — Subtraction block: $e(t) = v_{set} - v_{actual}(t)$. 5. **Compensator (PI / PID Algorithm)** — ECU software implementing discrete-time control law. 6. **Actuator Drive Stage** — H-bridge driving a DC servo motor (modern) or vacuum solenoid (legacy), coupled to the throttle cable or electronic throttle body (drive-by-wire). ## 2.3 Signal Conditioning of the VSS Pulse Train The VSS emits a digital pulse train. To obtain a continuous velocity signal, the ECU's software applies one of the following: - **Frequency-to-Voltage Conversion (FVC)** using a charge pump. - **Pulse Counting over a fixed time window** $T_w$ (typically **100 ms**) to compute average speed. - **Period Measurement** for low-speed accuracy. The conversion formula (count method) used by the ECU is: $$v_{actual} = \frac{N_{pulses} \cdot C_{wheel}}{T_w \cdot N_{teeth}}$$ where $N_{pulses}$ is the count, $C_{wheel}$ is the wheel circumference, and $N_{teeth}$ is the number of teeth on the tone ring. ## 2.4 PID Control Law (KTU High-Yield) The compensator output $u(t)$ applied to the throttle actuator is: $$u(t) = K_p \cdot e(t) + K_i \int_0^t e(\tau)\, d\tau + K_d \cdot \frac{de(t)}{dt}$$ For KTU exam purposes, most automotive cruise systems use **PI only** (the derivative term is noisy on speed signals): $$u(t) = K_p \cdot e(t) + K_i \int_0^t e(\tau)\, d\tau$$ > [!NOTE] > **Why PI and not P alone?** A pure proportional controller would always have a steady-state error on a slope. The Integral term drives the long-term average error to zero, ensuring $v_{actual} \to v_{set}$ exactly. ## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet) | # | Formula / Equation | Physical Meaning | Typical Units | Boundary / Limit | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | $m \dfrac{dv}{dt} = F_t - F_d - F_r - F_g$ | Longitudinal force balance | N (newtons) | Valid for $v > 0$ | | 2 | $F_d = \tfrac{1}{2} \rho C_d A v^2$ | Aerodynamic drag | N | $v$ in m/s | | 3 | $F_r = \mu_r m g \cos\theta$ | Rolling resistance | N | $\mu_r \approx 0.012$ to $0.015$ | | 4 | $F_g = m g \sin\theta$ | Gradient resistance | N | $\theta$ = road slope angle | | 5 | $F_t = \dfrac{\eta \cdot T_{eng} \cdot i_g \cdot i_0}{r_{wheel}}$ | Tractive force at wheel | N | $\eta \approx 0.85$ to $0.92$ | | 6 | $e(t) = v_{set} - v_{actual}(t)$ | Velocity error signal | m/s or km/h | — | | 7 | $u(t) = K_p e + K_i \int e\, dt$ | PI compensator output | % throttle (0–100) | Output clamped to [0, 100] | | 8 | $v_{actual} = \dfrac{N_p \cdot C_{wheel}}{T_w \cdot N_{teeth}}$ | VSS speed estimation | m/s | $T_w \geq 100$ ms | | 9 | $f_{VSS} = k_v \cdot v$ | VSS pulse frequency | Hz | $k_v$ is sensor constant | | 10 | $\tau_{throttle} \leq 50$ ms | Actuator response constraint | ms | Hard real-time limit | | 11 | $G_{plant}(s) = \dfrac{K_v}{(\tau_1 s + 1)(\tau_2 s + 1)}$ | Vehicle longitudinal plant TF | — | 2nd order approximation | | 12 | $\text{Duty Cycle } D = \dfrac{u(t)}{100} \times 100\%$ | PWM command to throttle | % | 100 Hz – 1 kHz PWM | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Units Discipline:** All velocities used inside the PI loop must be in **m/s**; the dashboard display converts to km/h. Mixing units is the **#1 cause of errors** in KTU numerical answers. --- # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: Steady-State Velocity Error on a Slope A classic KTU numerical problem states: *"A 1500 kg vehicle on a 4% gradient with cruise control engaged at 90 km/h — find the steady-state throttle opening if $K_p$ alone is used."* We derive the condition below. ### Step 1 — Plant Equation at Steady State At steady state, $\frac{dv}{dt} = 0$, so the force balance collapses to: $$F_t = F_d + F_r + F_g$$ Substituting the standard resistive force terms (assuming $\cos\theta \approx 1$ for small angles, as $\theta \approx 2.29^\circ$ for a 4% grade): $$\begin{aligned} F_t &= \tfrac{1}{2}\rho C_d A v^2 + \mu_r m g + m g \sin\theta \end{aligned}$$ ### Step 2 — Convert Gradient Percentage to Angle For a 4% grade, the rise-to-run ratio is 0.04: $$\begin{aligned} \theta &= \arctan(0.04) \\ \theta &\approx 0.04 \text{ rad} \quad (\text{small-angle approximation}) \\ \sin\theta &\approx 0.04 \end{aligned}$$ ### Step 3 — Convert Cruise Speed to SI Units $$\begin{aligned} v &= 90 \text{ km/h} \\ v &= \dfrac{90 \times 1000}{3600} \text{ m/s} \\ v &= 25 \text{ m/s} \end{aligned}$$ ### Step 4 — Numerical Substitution (with $C_d = 0.32$, $A = 2.2$ m², $\mu_r = 0.013$) $$\begin{aligned} F_d &= \tfrac{1}{2} \times 1.225 \times 0.32 \times 2.2 \times (25)^2 \\ F_d &= 0.5 \times 1.225 \times 0.32 \times 2.2 \times 625 \\ F_d &= 269.5 \text{ N} \\ F_r &= 0.013 \times 1500 \times 9.81 \times 1 \\ F_r &= 191.3 \text{ N} \\ F_g &= 1500 \times 9.81 \times 0.04 \\ F_g &= 588.6 \text{ N} \end{aligned}$$ ### Step 5 — Total Resistive Force $$\begin{aligned} F_{total} &= 269.5 + 191.3 + 588.6 \\ F_{total} &= 1049.4 \text{ N} \end{aligned}$$ ### Step 6 — Required Throttle (Tractive Force) at Steady State For a proportional-only cruise controller, the steady-state throttle opening is: $$\begin{aligned} u_{ss} &= K_p \cdot e_{ss} \\ \end{aligned}$$ where $e_{ss} = v_{set} - v_{actual}$. The required tractive force $F_{total}$ corresponds (via an engine map) to a throttle percentage. For our example, the required throttle equates to approximately **38% opening**. ### Step 7 — Observation: The Proportional-Only Error If the road is **flat** and the vehicle already travels at exactly 90 km/h, then $F_{total} \approx 460$ N, requiring only ~18% throttle. But on a 4% grade, the same throttle opening produces insufficient force, so $v$ drops. The new steady state is **less than 90 km/h** — this non-zero $e_{ss}$ is the **steady-state error of P-control**, justifying the **Integral term** in real cruise systems. --- ## 3.2 Derivation: VSS Frequency for a Given Vehicle Speed Given a tone ring with $N = 48$ teeth and a wheel of effective radius $r = 0.32$ m: ### Step 1 — Wheel Circumference $$\begin{aligned} C_{wheel} &= 2 \pi r \\ C_{wheel} &= 2 \pi \times 0.32 \\ C_{wheel} &= 2.0106 \text{ m} \end{aligned}$$ ### Step 2 — Wheel Revolutions per Second at v = 25 m/s $$\begin{aligned} f_{wheel} &= \dfrac{v}{C_{wheel}} \\ f_{wheel} &= \dfrac{25}{2.0106} \\ f_{wheel} &= 12.434 \text{ rev/s} \end{aligned}$$ ### Step 3 — VSS Pulse Frequency (48 pulses per revolution) $$\begin{aligned} f_{VSS} &= N_{teeth} \times f_{wheel} \\ f_{VSS} &= 48 \times 12.434 \\ f_{VSS} &= 596.8 \text{ Hz} \end{aligned}$$ ### Step 4 — ECU Sampling Time for 1% Resolution To achieve 1 km/h resolution, count pulses over a window such that **at least 1 pulse** corresponds to **1 km/h = 0.2778 m/s** traveled: $$\begin{aligned} \text{Pulses per km/h} &= \dfrac{1000 \text{ m}}{C_{wheel}} \times N_{teeth} \\ &= \dfrac{1000}{2.0106} \times 48 \\ &= 23873 \text{ pulses per (km/h)} \\ \end{aligned}$$ So a 100 ms window captures about 663 pulses at 90 km/h — well above 1, giving excellent resolution. --- ## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix (Modern vs Legacy Cruise Control) | Parameter | Legacy (1990s) — Vacuum Actuator | Modern (2020s) — Drive-by-Wire | |---|---|---| | **Actuator** | Engine vacuum diaphragm + solenoid | BLDC / Stepper motor on e-throttle | | **Control signal** | Vacuum pressure (kPa) | PWM / CAN message | | **Integration** | Standalone ECU | Integrated into VECU / PCM | | **Speed range** | 40 – 200 km/h | 30 – 180 km/h (adaptive) | | **Latency** | ~150 ms | ~25 ms | | **Adaptive feature** | None | Radar-based ACC (stop-and-go) | | **Failure mode** | Vacuum leak → disengage | Plausibility check → DTC logged | | **KTU relevance** | Block diagram only | Full PID + sensor fusion | --- # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Cruise Control Circuit — Functional Block Architecture The diagram below shows the complete closed-loop signal flow of a cruise control circuit. Each node represents a physical or logical block; arrows show signal direction (driver inputs, feedback, and control outputs). ```mermaid flowchart LR driverInput[Driver Switch Bank
SET RESUME ACCEL DECEL CANCEL] ecuCore[ECU Microcontroller
Reference Latch and PI Algorithm] vssSensor[Vehicle Speed Sensor
Hall Effect Tone Ring] fvcBlock[FVC or Pulse Counter
Frequency to Voltage] errorDet[Error Detector
e t equals vset minus vactual] piBlock[PI Compensator
u of t equals Kp e plus Ki integral] hbridge[H Bridge Driver
PWM at 1 kHz] throttleActuator[Throttle Actuator
DC Servo or Stepper Motor] throttleBody[Throttle Body
Air Intake Regulation] vehiclePlant[Vehicle Plant
Longitudinal Dynamics] dashboard[Instrument Cluster
Speed Display and CRUISE Lamp] driverInput --> ecuCore vssSensor --> fvcBlock fvcBlock --> errorDet ecuCore --> errorDet errorDet --> piBlock piBlock --> hbridge hbridge --> throttleActuator throttleActuator --> throttleBody throttleBody --> vehiclePlant vehiclePlant --> vssSensor vehiclePlant --> dashboard ecuCore --> dashboard classDef sensorNode fill:#cfe2ff,stroke:#0d6efd,color:#000 classDef controlNode fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#fd7e14,color:#000 classDef actuatorNode fill:#d1e7dd,stroke:#198754,color:#000 classDef plantNode fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#dc3545,color:#000 class vssSensor,fvcBlock sensorNode class ecuCore,errorDet,piBlock,driverInput controlNode class hbridge,throttleActuator,throttleBody actuatorNode class vehiclePlant,dashboard plantNode ``` **How to read this diagram for KTU answers:** - The **outer loop** runs from VSS back to the Error Detector — this is the **feedback path**. - The **inner path** from PI Compensator to Throttle Actuator is the **forward path**. - The plant is the **vehicle itself** (mechanical load), not an electronic block. - The driver input enters the ECU, *not* the throttle directly — a common student mistake. ## 4.2 State Transition Diagram — Cruise Control Switch Logic The cruise control state machine has 5 stable states. The ECU transitions between them based on driver inputs and vehicle conditions. KTU examiners frequently ask students to draw or describe these states. ```mermaid stateDiagram-v2 [*] --> stateOff stateOff --> stateArmed: Driver presses ON switch stateArmed --> stateEngaged: Vehicle speed above 40 km/h
and driver presses SET stateEngaged --> stateOverspeed: vactual exceeds vset by 8 km/h stateEngaged --> stateSuspended: Driver presses CANCEL
or applies brake stateOverspeed --> stateSuspended: Automatic disengage
latched stateSuspended --> stateEngaged: Driver presses RESUME
and conditions valid stateEngaged --> stateOff: Driver presses OFF
or ignition OFF stateSuspended --> stateOff: Driver presses OFF stateOverspeed --> stateOff: Ignition OFF stateOff: OFF
Circuit de energized
No throttle control stateArmed: ARMED
ECU monitoring VSS
Awaiting SET stateEngaged: ENGAGED
PI loop active
Throttle servo driven stateOverspeed: OVERSPEED
Latched fault
Throttle closed stateSuspended: SUSPENDED
Last speed stored
PI loop paused ``` **KTU Pitfall:** Students often forget the **40 km/h minimum** and the **brake-pedal disengagement** path. Both are safety-mandated by ECE R89 and are **high-yield exam points**. ## 4.3 Typical Switch Interface Circuit (Resistor Ladder Multiplexing) A KTU-favorite sub-topic is how a single ECU pin reads **5 different switches** using a resistor network: ```mermaid flowchart TB vref[5V ECU Reference] --> r1[Resistor R1 1000 ohm] r1 --> r2[Resistor R2 1000 ohm] r2 --> r3[Resistor R3 1000 ohm] r3 --> r4[Resistor R4 1000 ohm] r4 --> gnd[Ground 0V] tap1[Tap Point 1
OFF switch]:::tapNode tap2[Tap Point 2
SET switch]:::tapNode tap3[Tap Point 3
RESUME switch]:::tapNode tap4[Tap Point 4
CANCEL switch]:::tapNode sw1[OFF NO] -.->|parallel| tap1 sw2[SET NO] -.->|parallel| tap2 sw3[RESUME NO] -.->|parallel| tap3 sw4[CANCEL NO] -.->|parallel| tap4 tap1 --> adcPin[ECU ADC Pin AN0] tap2 --> adcPin tap3 --> adcPin tap4 --> adcPin classDef tapNode fill:#e2d6f3,stroke:#6f42c1,color:#000 ``` **Operation principle:** - The ECU applies 5 V at the top of the ladder and reads the divided voltage at the ADC pin. - Pressing **SET** (a normally-open switch) shorts out R2 + R3 + R4, raising the ADC voltage to a specific level. - The ECU uses **voltage-window decoding** to identify which switch is pressed. | Switch Pressed | Resistors Shorted | ADC Voltage (V) | Recognized Code | |---|---|---|---| | None (open) | None | 1.00 | 0x00 (idle) | | OFF | R1+R2+R3+R4 | 5.00 | 0x04 | | SET | R2+R3+R4 | 2.50 | 0x03 | | RESUME | R3+R4 | 1.67 | 0x02 | | CANCEL | R4 | 1.25 | 0x01 | > [!IMPORTANT] > This resistor-ladder trick is **exam-favorite** because it tests (a) circuit analysis, (b) ADC interfacing, and (c) automotive wiring-harness minimization — three KTU syllabus outcomes in one question. --- # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) > **[KTU University Exam — May 2024]** **Q1. Define a cruise control circuit and identify its principal input and feedback sensors.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** A cruise control circuit is a closed-loop electronic system that automatically regulates a vehicle's throttle opening to maintain a driver-set road speed, eliminating the need for continuous accelerator-pedal input. The **principal input sensor** is the *driver switch bank* (SET, RESUME, ACCEL, DECEL, CANCEL) which feeds the set-point velocity to the ECU. The **principal feedback sensor** is the *Vehicle Speed Sensor (VSS)* — typically a Hall-effect device mounted on the transmission output shaft — which provides a pulse-train whose frequency is proportional to the actual vehicle speed. The ECU computes the error $e(t) = v_{set} - v_{actual}(t)$ and drives a throttle actuator via a PI control law to drive this error to zero. *Marking key: 1 mark for definition, 1 mark for input sensor, 1 mark for feedback sensor.* --- > **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** **Q2. Why is a PI (Proportional-Integral) compensator preferred over a pure P compensator in cruise control applications? Justify with one quantitative reason.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** A pure proportional compensator produces a non-zero **steady-state error** when subjected to a constant disturbance, such as a road gradient. On a constant slope, the resisting force $F_g = mg \sin\theta$ is constant, but the proportional output $u = K_p \cdot e$ falls to zero only when $e = 0$. However, a non-zero $u$ is required to overcome the slope, so the controller settles at a non-zero $e_{ss}$, meaning the actual speed is *less* than the set speed. The **Integral** term $K_i \int e\, dt$ accumulates any persistent error over time and continues to increase the throttle command until the error is driven to exactly zero. Hence, the steady-state error of a PI controller on a constant slope is mathematically zero — a property essential for accurate cruise control. Quantitatively, for a ramp-like disturbance, PI reduces $e_{ss}$ from $\tfrac{1}{K_p}$ (P-only) to zero. --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) > **[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, Module 3, Question A]** ### Question A (14 Marks) **Q. (a)** With the help of a labelled block diagram, explain the complete architecture of an electronic cruise control circuit. Identify and justify the role of each block. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A passenger car of mass 1500 kg is travelling on a level road at a cruise-set speed of 100 km/h. The vehicle is governed by a proportional-integral controller with $K_p = 0.8$ and $K_i = 0.05$ per second. The aerodynamic drag coefficient $C_d = 0.30$, frontal area $A = 2.2$ m², air density $\rho = 1.225$ kg/m³, and rolling resistance coefficient $\mu_r = 0.013$. Determine: (i) the total resistive force at the set speed, (ii) the steady-state throttle opening assuming a linear engine map where 1% throttle ≈ 25 N of tractive force, and (iii) whether the steady-state speed error is zero. Justify using the PI law. **(7 marks)** --- #### Model Solution **Part (a) — Block Diagram Explanation (7 marks)** The cruise control circuit is best described using a forward-path / feedback-path block diagram (see Section 4.1). The six canonical blocks are: 1. **Driver Switch Bank (1 mark):** Provides the human-machine interface. Includes SET, RESUME, ACCEL, DECEL, CANCEL momentary-contact switches. These are multiplexed onto a single ECU pin using a resistor-ladder network to minimize wiring harness weight — a key automotive design principle. 2. **ECU with Reference Latch and PI Algorithm (2 marks):** The heart of the system. A microcontroller (typically Infineon TC27x or NXP S32K series in modern vehicles) executes a discrete-time PI algorithm at 50–100 Hz. The set-point velocity is latched in non-volatile memory so that RESUME restores the previous setting. 3. **Vehicle Speed Sensor (VSS) and FVC (1 mark):** The feedback sensor. A Hall-effect device reading a 48-tooth tone ring produces a frequency $f_{VSS}$ proportional to wheel speed. A frequency-to-voltage converter or a pulse counter provides a digital speed value $v_{actual}$. 4. **Error Detector (1 mark):** Computes $e(t) = v_{set} - v_{actual}(t)$. In software, this is a single subtraction instruction executed each loop cycle. 5. **H-Bridge Driver and Throttle Actuator (1 mark):** The forward-path power stage. A PWM-driven H-bridge supplies bidirectional current to a DC servo motor or stepper motor coupled to the throttle body, modulating air inflow into the engine. 6. **Vehicle Plant and Dashboard Feedback (1 mark):** The mechanical system (vehicle longitudinal dynamics) closes the loop, while the instrument cluster provides visual feedback via the speedometer and a "CRUISE" tell-tale lamp. --- **Part
