## SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Occupant Forward Displacement During Crash

**Problem:** A vehicle traveling at $v_0 = 50$ km/h strikes a rigid barrier. Compute the time and forward displacement of an unbelted occupant (assume the occupant's center of mass is at $d = 0.6$ m from the dashboard) before the dashboard is struck. Assume crash deceleration $a$ is constant.

**Given:**

- $v_0 = 50$ km/h = $13.89$ m/s
- Crash duration: $\Delta t = 120$ ms = $0.120$ s
- Distance to dashboard: $d = 0.6$ m

**Step 1 — Compute crash deceleration**

Using $v = v_0 - a \cdot t$ with $v = 0$ at $t = \Delta t$:

$$a = \frac{v_0}{\Delta t} = \frac{13.89}{0.120} = 115.75 \text{ m/s}^2 \approx 11.8g$$

**Step 2 — Compute time to reach dashboard (occupant continues at $v_0$ in absence of belt)**

The occupant travels at $v_0 = 13.89$ m/s (Newton's First Law) until hitting the dashboard.

$$t_{hit} = \frac{d}{v_0} = \frac{0.6}{13.89} = 0.0432 \text{ s} = 43.2 \text{ ms}$$

**Step 3 — Verify airbag readiness**

$$\boxed{t_{hit} = 43.2 \text{ ms} < t_{deploy} = 50 \text{ ms} \;\; \checkmark}$$

The airbag is fully inflated **before** the occupant arrives at the dashboard. **Margin = 6.8 ms.**

**Step 4 — Compute occupant's velocity at dashboard impact (if no bag)**

$$v_{occupant} = v_0 = 13.89 \text{ m/s} \quad (\text{unchanged due to inertia})$$

**Step 5 — Kinetic energy to be absorbed by bag**

For a 75 kg occupant:

$$E_{k} = \frac{1}{2} m v_0^2 = \frac{1}{2} \times 75 \times (13.89)^2 = 7236 \text{ J}$$

This is the energy the airbag (and seatbelt) must dissipate to bring the occupant to rest safely.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Moles of Nitrogen Generated by Inflator

**Problem:** A driver-side inflator contains **$m = 60$ g** of sodium azide. Compute the moles of N₂ produced and the equivalent gas volume at STP (0 °C, 1 atm).

**Given:** Molar mass of NaN₃ = 65 g/mol.

**Step 1 — Moles of NaN₃**

$$n_{\text{NaN}_3} = \frac{m}{M} = \frac{60}{65} = 0.923 \text{ mol}$$

**Step 2 — Moles of N₂ from stoichiometry**

From $2\,\text{NaN}_3 \rightarrow 3\,\text{N}_2$:

$$n_{N_2} = \frac{3}{2} \times n_{\text{NaN}_3} = \frac{3}{2} \times 0.923 = 1.385 \text{ mol}$$

**Step 3 — Volume at STP** (using $V = nRT/P$, with $R = 0.0821$ L·atm/mol·K, $T = 273$ K, $P = 1$ atm)

$$V_{STP} = n_{N_2} \times 22.4 \text{ L/mol} = 1.385 \times 22.4 = 31.0 \text{ L}$$

> [!NOTE]
> In practice, the gas is hotter (~600 °C) and at higher pressure, so the **inflated bag volume is much larger** than 31 L after cooling/regulation by the filter and vents. Typical driver-side bag inflates to **~60 L**.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Squib Firing Energy

**Problem:** A squib with resistance $R = 2.2 \,\Omega$ is fired by a 470 µF capacitor pre-charged to $V = 25$ V. Compute (a) the energy delivered, and (b) the peak current.

**Step 1 — Stored capacitor energy**

$$E = \frac{1}{2} C V^2 = \frac{1}{2} \times 470 \times 10^{-6} \times (25)^2$$

$$E = 0.5 \times 4.7 \times 10^{-4} \times 625 = 0.147 \text{ J} = 147 \text{ mJ}$$

**Step 2 — Peak current (assuming purely resistive discharge)**

$$I_{peak} = \frac{V}{R} = \frac{25}{2.2} = 11.36 \text{ A}$$

**Step 3 — Verify sufficient firing energy**

$$\boxed{E_{delivered} = 147 \text{ mJ} \gg E_{required} = 10{-}20 \text{ mJ} \;\; \checkmark}$$

The firing energy is ~7–15× the required energy, ensuring reliable ignition. The remaining 80–90% energy sustains the propellant burn.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix — Crash Discrimination

The ECU uses **two independent criteria** before firing:

| Criterion | Threshold | Window | Source |
|-----------|-----------|--------|--------|
| Velocity change ($\Delta v$) | 8–25 km/h | 50–150 ms | Central MEMS |
| Acceleration peak | 4–8 g (frontal) | 5–20 ms | Front satellite |
| Safing sensor | Bistable contact | < 10 ms | Mechanical |
| Rollover angle rate | > 90°/s | 100 ms | Gyroscope (side/curtain) |

A **logical AND** between the safing sensor and the algorithmic decision is required → this prevents accidental deployment due to electrical noise, pot-hole impact, or sensor failure.

## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

---

### PART A QUESTIONS (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
> *Define the term "Supplementary Restraint System (SRS)" and explain why airbags are called a "supplementary" and not a "primary" restraint.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
SRS stands for **Supplementary Restraint System** — a passive safety device designed to work *in addition to* the seat belt, not in place of it. The word "supplementary" highlights the fact that an airbag alone cannot prevent injury during a severe crash; it is most effective only when combined with a properly worn seat belt. In a frontal crash, the seat belt prevents the occupant from being ejected and controls the kinematics, while the airbag cushions the head and chest to reduce facial/rib injuries. Without the seat belt, the occupant may "submarine" under the airbag or hit the steering wheel/dashboard before the bag fully inflates. Hence, airbags are designed, tested, and certified as supplementary to the primary restraint — the **3-point seat belt**. *(3 marks: 1 for definition, 1 for supplementary logic, 1 for seat-belt interdependency.)*

---

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
> *List any four types of airbags used in modern automobiles and state the specific occupant injury each one is designed to mitigate.*

**Model Answer (3 marks — tabular form expected):**
| S.No | Airbag Type | Injury Mitigated |
|------|-------------|------------------|
| 1 | Frontal Driver Airbag | Head and chest impact on steering wheel |
| 2 | Frontal Passenger Airbag | Head and chest impact on dashboard |
| 3 | Side (Torso) Airbag | Thorax/rib injury from side intrusion |
| 4 | Curtain Airbag | Head injury during side impact or rollover |

*(3 marks: 0.5 each for correct airbag + 0.25 for injury.)*

---

### PART B QUESTIONS (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> **Q3A. [KTU University Exam – Model Paper, KTU 2024 Scheme]**
> **(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the functional architecture of an **SRS (Supplementary Restraint System)** in a modern automobile. Describe the role of each block in detail. **(7 marks)**
> **(b)** Explain the **working principle of an airbag inflator** based on the sodium azide reaction. Derive the volume of nitrogen gas produced from **80 g of NaN₃** at STP. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Functional Architecture of SRS (7 marks):**

The SRS consists of **five major functional blocks** that work as a closed-loop real-time embedded system:

1. **Crash Sensor Array (1.5 marks)**
   - Front bumper satellite sensors (mechanical ball-in-tube and/or MEMS accelerometers) detect sudden deceleration.
   - **Safing sensor** (mechanical) in series — independent confirmation that a real crash has occurred.
   - Rollover gyroscope for curtain airbag activation.
   - **Role:** Converts physical crash pulse into electrical signals.

2. **SRS Control Unit (ECU / SDM) (1.5 marks)**
   - 32-bit microcontroller running a real-time crash algorithm at **~1 kHz** sampling rate.
   - Computes velocity change $\Delta v = \int a(t)\, dt$ and compares to a calibrated threshold.
   - Includes a **backup capacitor bank** (470 µF @ 25 V) to ensure firing even if main battery is damaged.
   - **Role:** Decision-making — fire or no-fire.

3. **Clock Spring (1 mark)**
   - Flat ribbon cable wound in a spiral in the steering column.
   - Provides unbroken electrical path to driver airbag while wheel rotates.
   - **Role:** Signal continuity under rotation.

4. **Squib (Initiator) and Inflator (1.5 marks)**
   - The **squib** is a small pyrotechnic device (~2 Ω resistance) ignited by an electrical pulse.
   - The **inflator** contains propellant (NaN₃ or guanidine nitrate) that rapidly generates N₂.
   - **Role:** Converts electrical signal → chemical gas → pressure.

5. **Airbag Module and Cover (1 mark)**
   - Folded nylon bag with engineered cover having weak tear seams.
   - Has vent holes for controlled deflation.
   - **Role:** Catches and decelerates the occupant gently.

*(Additional 0.5 mark for a neat labelled block diagram.)*

---

**(b) Sodium Azide Inflator — Working Principle and Volume Derivation (7 marks):**

**Working Principle (3 marks):**
When the squib fires, it ignites a small **primary charge** (typically ZPP — Zirconium Potassium Perchlorate). The flame front from the primary charge ignites the main **sodium azide (NaN₃)** propellant charge, which rapidly decomposes exothermically to release **nitrogen gas**:

$$2\,\text{NaN}_3(s) \xrightarrow{\Delta} 2\,\text{Na}(s) + 3\,\text{N}_2(g)\uparrow$$

The hot sodium metal is highly reactive and is converted into harmless silicates by reaction with **silicon dioxide (SiO₂)** or iron oxide (Fe₂O₃) added as scavengers:

$$4\,\text{Na} + \text{SiO}_2 \rightarrow 2\,\text{Na}_2\text{O}\cdot\text{SiO}_2 \quad (\text{sodium silicate})$$

The N₂ gas, after being filtered and slightly cooled by an internal **metal filter**, rushes into the folded bag, inflating it within **30–50 ms**. The bag then vents through calibrated **exhaust ports** in 100–200 ms, providing a controlled cushion.

**Derivation (4 marks):**

Given: $m_{\text{NaN}_3} = 80$ g, $M_{\text{NaN}_3} = 65$ g/mol.

**Step 1 — Moles of NaN₃**

$$n_{\text{NaN}_3} = \frac{80}{65} = 1.2308 \text{ mol}$$

**Step 2 — Moles of N₂ (from stoichiometry 2 : 3)**

$$n_{N_2} = \frac{3}{2} \times 1.2308 = 1.846 \text{ mol}$$

**Step 3 — Volume at STP (1 mol gas = 22.4 L at STP)**

$$V_{STP} = 1.846 \times 22.4 = \boxed{41.35 \text{ L}}$$

*(+0.5 for conclusion: At operating temperature ~600 °C, the actual inflated bag volume is significantly higher due to ideal-gas expansion V ∝ T.)*

---

> **Q3B. [KTU University Exam – Model Paper, KTU 2024 Scheme] (Alternative to Q3A)**
> **(a)** Describe the construction and working of a **piezoelectric / MEMS accelerometer** used as a crash sensor in an SRS. **(7 marks)**
> **(b)** Explain the **deployment sequence** of an airbag in terms of the time-line from impact to full inflation, with emphasis on why the **30–50 ms** time budget is critical. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Piezoelectric / MEMS Accelerometer for SRS (7 marks):**

**Construction (3.5 marks):**
- A **piezoelectric accelerometer** has a seismic mass (proof mass) bonded to a piezoelectric crystal (often lead zirconate titanate — PZT).
- When the vehicle decelerates during a crash, the mass applies a force $F = m a$ on the crystal.
- The crystal generates a charge $Q = d \cdot F$ where $d$ is the piezoelectric coefficient (C/N).
- This charge is converted to a voltage by a charge amplifier: $V_{out} = Q/C_f$.

- A **MEMS accelerometer** (more common in modern SRS) consists of a silicon proof mass suspended by compliant flexures, with capacitive fingers interdigitated between fixed combs.
- Acceleration causes displacement $\Delta x = \dfrac{m a}{k}$ (where $k$ is the spring constant of the flexure).
- The capacitance changes: $\Delta C = C_0 \cdot \dfrac{\Delta x}{d_gap}$, detected by a differential capacitance readout ASIC.

**Working Principle (2.5 marks):**
1. During normal driving, $a \approx 0$ → output ≈ 0 V (only gravity bias).
2. On a crash pulse, deceleration of **5–30 g** produces a proportional output voltage.
3. Output is fed to the SRS MCU's ADC.
4. The MCU integrates the signal to compute $\Delta v$ and decides firing.
5. The MEMS device is co-packaged with the ASIC in a single QFN package for EMI immunity.

**Why MEMS is preferred (1 mark):** smaller, lower cost, lower power, integrated signal conditioning, and better high-frequency response (essential for crash pulses with rise-times < 5 ms).

---

**(b) Deployment Sequence & Time-Criticality (7 marks):**

**Time-line table (4.5 marks):**

| Time (ms) | Event |
|-----------|-------|
| 0 | Vehicle contacts barrier / object |
| 0–5 | Front bumper crush begins; satellite sensors detect deceleration pulse |
| 5–10 | Safing sensor closes (mechanical confirmation) |
| 10–15 | SRS ECU ADC completes first samples, begins integration |
| 15–20 | Algorithm decides: threshold exceeded → firing command issued |
| 20–25 | Capacitor discharges current pulse (~10 A) through squib |
| 25–30 | Bridge wire ignites primary charge; flame front reaches main propellant |
| 30–50 | NaN₃ burns → N₂ generated → bag fully inflates and emerges from cover |
| 50–80 | Occupant's head and torso contact the bag; bag begins to vent |
| 80–200 | Controlled deflation dissipates occupant's kinetic energy |
| 200+ | Post-crash; occupant has been brought to near-zero velocity |

**Why 30–50 ms is critical (2.5 marks):**
- A car crashing at 50 km/h decelerates from full speed to zero in ~100–150 ms.
- An unbelted occupant continues forward at the pre-crash speed and would strike the steering wheel/dashboard in approximately **43 ms** (as derived in Section 3.1).
- Therefore, the airbag **must** be fully deployed **before** the occupant arrives at the dashboard — this defines the **30–50 ms budget**.
- If deployment were slower, the bag would inflate *behind* the occupant — useless.
- If deployment were faster, the bag would be a rigid pressure vessel — could cause injury.
- The 30–50 ms window is thus a **constrained optimization** between crash kinematics and propellant chemistry.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing airbag with seat belt role** — Many students write "airbag replaces seat belt." Lose 1 mark immediately. Always state: *Airbag is supplementary to seat belt; without belt, airbag is less effective or even dangerous.*
> 2. **Writing only the formula without units in numericals** — KTU demands units in every numerical answer.
> 3. **Forgetting the safing sensor** — A common omission. The ECU + safing sensor dual-path is mandatory for ISO 26262 ASIL-D.
> 4. **Skipping the stoichiometric ratio (2:3) in NaN₃ reaction** — Marks are awarded for explicit stoichiometric application, not just the equation.
> 5. **Not labelling Mermaid/diagram blocks** — A neatly labelled block diagram carries 1–2 marks; an unlabelled sketch is treated as a minor drawing.
> 6. **Mixing up inflation time (30–50 ms) with deflation time (100–200 ms)** — Examiners explicitly test this distinction.

---

### TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

- [ ] **SRS = Supplementary Restraint System**; works *with* the seat belt, not instead of it.
- [ ] **Inflation time: 30–50 ms**; **deflation time: 100–200 ms**.
- [ ] **Main reaction: $2\,\text{NaN}_3 \rightarrow 2\,\text{Na} + 3\,\text{N}_2$**; Na is scavenged by SiO₂ or Fe₂O₃.
- [ ] **Squib resistance: ~2 Ω**; fired by capacitor discharge (~470 µF @ 25 V).
- [ ] **Crash discrimination:** $\Delta v$ integrated from accelerometer; threshold ≈ 8–25 km/h; AND-gated with safing sensor.
- [ ] **Sensors used:** MEMS accelerometer, piezoelectric accelerometer, mechanical ball-in-tube, gyroscope (for rollover).
- [ ] **Clock spring** = spiral flat cable in steering column for electrical continuity.
- [ ] **Dual-stage squibs** allow tiered deployment (low- vs. high-speed crash).
- [ ] **Functional safety:** ISO 26262 ASIL-D; FMVSS 208; ECE R94/95; AIS-096.
- [ ] **Diagnostic unit** continuously monitors squib resistance, sensor health, capacitor charge; logs DTCs.
- [ ] **Occupant forward displacement formula:** $s = v_0 \cdot t_{deploy}$ must be ≤ distance to dashboard.
- [ ] **Volume of N₂ at STP:** 22.4 L per mole; at ~600 °C the actual deployed volume is much larger.
- [ ] **Energy delivered to squib:** $E = \frac{1}{2}CV^2 \geq 100$ mJ (typical 147 mJ for C = 470 µF, V = 25 V).
- [ ] **Types of airbags:** Frontal, Side, Curtain, Knee, Center, Rear — each targets a specific injury mechanism.
- [ ] **Why propellant is NaN₃:** generates inert N₂ rapidly, with manageable byproducts when scavengers are used.
- [ ] **Bag volume:** ~60 L (driver) to ~160 L (passenger); bag pressure ~0.5–2 bar.
- [ ] **Service life:** 10–15 years (propellant and propellant housing degradation).
- [ ] **Algorithm sampling rate:** ~1 kHz (sometimes up to 4 kHz) — a real-time embedded constraint.
- [ ] **Always draw a labelled block diagram** for full-mark answers on system architecture questions.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Latest Automotive Instrumentation: Concepts of Hybrid Technology and Its Classification
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEICT721 - AUTOMOBILE INSTRUMENTATION
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Latest Automotive Instrumentation: Concepts of Hybrid Technology and Its Classification

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition of Hybrid Vehicle Technology

A **Hybrid Electric Vehicle (HEV)** is an automobile that derives its propulsion energy from two or more distinct energy sources, conventionally combining an **Internal Combustion Engine (ICE)** with one or more **Electric Machines (EM)** powered by an electrochemical energy storage system (battery/ultracapacitor). According to the **KTU 2024 Scheme (OEICT721)** automotive instrumentation framework, hybrid technology represents a transitional powertrain architecture that bridges conventional fossil-fuel propulsion and fully electric mobility.

The **hybridization factor (HF)** mathematically quantifies the degree of electric contribution:

$$HF = \frac{P_{electric}}{P_{electric} + P_{ICE}} \times 100\%$$

where the **electric contribution** represents the rated peak power of the traction electric machine, and **P_ICE** is the rated peak power of the internal combustion engine.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Hybrid Technology is a **mandatory topic in Module 4** of OEICT721, tested routinely in Part A (3 marks) as a definition/classification question and in Part B (14 marks) as a comparative analytical question on powertrain architectures.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Two-Engine Bicycle" Intuition

Imagine a cyclist climbing a steep hill with a regular bicycle. When the slope is gentle, pedaling (analogous to the **ICE**) is efficient. But on a steep climb or when starting from a stop, the cyclist's legs tire quickly. Now imagine the same cyclist has an **electric motor booster** (the **EM**) that kicks in exactly when the legs struggle — during acceleration, hill climbs, and stop-and-go traffic — while the human legs (ICE) take over on the highway for sustained cruising.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world analogy in plain English:** A hybrid vehicle is like a **relay race team** — the petrol engine and the electric motor take turns carrying the "baton" of propulsion depending on which one is more efficient at that instant. The brain of this relay is the **Hybrid Control Unit (HCU)**, which decides — millisecond by millisecond — who runs and who rests.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics & Nomenclature in Hybrid Technology

The following **KTU-board-recommended terminology** must be memorized:

| Abbreviation | Full Form | Standard Metric |
|:---:|:---|:---|
| **HEV** | Hybrid Electric Vehicle | Combined fuel economy in **km/L** or **MPGe** |
| **PHEV** | Plug-in Hybrid Electric Vehicle | All-Electric Range (AER) in **km** |
| **BEV** | Battery Electric Vehicle | Range in **km**, kWh consumption per 100 km |
| **FCEV** | Fuel Cell Electric Vehicle | Hydrogen consumption in **kg/100 km** |
| **SOC** | State of Charge | Percentage **%** (0 = empty, 100 = full) |
| **SOH** | State of Health | Percentage **%** (degradation indicator) |
| **BSG** | Belt-driven Starter Generator | Voltage rating **12V / 48V** |
| **HCU** | Hybrid Control Unit | CAN bus speed **500 kbps** |
| **PCU** | Power Control Unit | Switching frequency **5–20 kHz** |

> [!NOTE]
> The **48V mild-hybrid system** is the current industry standard (Mercedes EQ Boost, Audi, BS-VI compliance platforms), replacing legacy **12V micro-hybrid** architectures. KTU 2024 Scheme expects students to know the 48V benchmark explicitly.

### 1.4 Why Hybrid Technology Matters in Modern Automotive Instrumentation

Modern automotive instrumentation — **Battery Management Systems (BMS)**, **SOC meters**, **power-flow displays**, and **regenerative braking sensors** — exists primarily to serve hybrid and electric powertrains. A conventional car needs only a fuel gauge and an RPM meter; a hybrid car requires:

- A **real-time power-flow indicator** (showing energy transfer between ICE, EM, battery, wheels)
- An **SOC gauge** (battery state-of-charge display)
- A **regenerative braking indicator** (showing energy recovery during deceleration)
- An **efficiency coach** (advising the driver on optimal hybrid mode)

This is why **Module 4 of OEICT721** places hybrid technology at the heart of contemporary automotive instrumentation.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Fundamental Operating Principle of a Hybrid Powertrain

The hybrid system operates on the principle of **"right-sized" propulsion** — using the ICE in its most efficient operating region (typically 30–50% of peak torque at 1500–2500 RPM) while letting the electric machine cover the inefficient regions (idle, low-speed acceleration, regenerative braking).

**The four operational modes of a generic hybrid powertrain are:**

1. **ICE-Only Mode** — When battery SOC is low or when sustained highway cruising is required. The ICE alone drives the wheels.
2. **EM-Only Mode** — When SOC is high and power demand is low (low-speed city driving, parking maneuvers). The ICE is shut off completely.
3. **Combined Mode (Boost)** — During hard acceleration. Both ICE and EM deliver torque simultaneously to the wheels through a torque-coupling device.
4. **Regenerative Mode** — During deceleration and braking. The EM acts as a generator, converting kinetic energy into electrical energy stored in the battery.

### 2.2 Why and How Each Mode Works — Engineering Logic

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The "Why" Behind Mode Selection (KTU-favourite analytical question):**
> - **Why shut off the ICE at traffic lights?** Because an idling ICE consumes fuel at **0.5–0.8 L/hour** with zero useful work output — this is pure waste. The EM takes over silently.
> - **Why use regenerative braking?** Because up to **30–40%** of the energy delivered to a conventional car's brakes is dissipated as heat. Recovery of this energy boosts urban fuel economy by **15–25%**.
> - **Why "right-size" the ICE?** Because a smaller ICE operating near its peak efficiency map can match the performance of a larger ICE while consuming **20–35% less fuel**.

### 2.3 The Hybrid Control Unit (HCU) — The Brain

The HCU is the central decision-maker, executing an **Energy Management Strategy (EMS)** every **10 milliseconds**. Two principal strategies are taught in KTU 2024:

- **Rule-Based Strategy** — Uses pre-defined IF-THEN rules (e.g., "IF SOC < 30% AND power demand > 50 kW, THEN start ICE").
- **Optimization-Based Strategy** — Uses algorithms like **Equivalent Consumption Minimization Strategy (ECMS)** or **Dynamic Programming (DP)** to minimize a cost function $J$ over a driving cycle.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (High-Yield)

| # | Formula / Equation | Symbol Meaning | Units | KTU Use Case |
|:-:|:---|:---|:---|:---|
| 1 | $HF = \dfrac{P_{EM}}{P_{EM} + P_{ICE}} \times 100\%$ | Hybridization Factor | % | Classification (Mild vs Full) |
| 2 | $SOC(t) = SOC(t_0) - \dfrac{1}{C_{bat}} \int_{t_0}^{t} I_{bat}(\tau) \, d\tau$ | Battery State of Charge | % | BMS instrumentation |
| 3 | $E_{regen} = \dfrac{1}{2} \, m \, (v_1^2 - v_2^2) \cdot \eta_{regen}$ | Regenerative energy recovered | kJ or Wh | Energy recovery calculation |
| 4 | $\eta_{sys} = \dfrac{P_{wheel}}{P_{fuel} + P_{elec\,in}}$ | System efficiency | dimensionless | Comparative analysis |
| 5 | $P_{req} = \dfrac{F_{total} \cdot v}{3600}$ | Required tractive power | kW | Power-flow design |
| 6 | $F_{total} = F_{roll} + F_{aero} + F_{grade} + F_{accel}$ | Total resistance force | N | Vehicle dynamics |
| 7 | $F_{roll} = m \, g \, C_{rr} \, \cos\theta$ | Rolling resistance | N | Longitudinal dynamics |
| 8 | $F_{aero} = \dfrac{1}{2} \, \rho \, C_d \, A \, v^2$ | Aerodynamic drag | N | High-speed design |
| 9 | $T_{EM} = k_t \cdot I_a$ | EM torque (linear region) | N·m | Motor sizing |
| 10 | $V_{OC} = V_{0} + K \cdot SOC$ | Open-circuit voltage | V | BMS sensor calibration |

> [!NOTE]
> **Convention Note for KTU Board Exams:** When using absolute-value notation in any formula (e.g., $\vert \sin\theta \vert$), always write it as `\vert` or `\mid` in LaTeX to prevent markdown table corruption. Examiners deduct marks for sloppy notation.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

These formulas are not academic exercises — they are used by:
- **Tata Motors (India)** for Nexon EV and Tigor EV BMS calibration
- **Toyota** for the Prius and Camry Hybrid ECMS tuning
- **ISRO / DRDO** for electric military vehicle instrumentation
- **BOSCH and Continental** in their 48V BSG modules for OEM platforms like Maruti Suzuki

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: Regenerative Braking Energy Recovery

**Problem Setup:** A hybrid car of mass $m = 1500 \text{ kg}$ decelerates from $v_1 = 80 \text{ km/h}$ to $v_2 = 40 \text{ km/h}$. The regenerative braking efficiency is $\eta_{regen} = 0.65$. Calculate the electrical energy recovered and stored in the battery.

**Step 1: Convert velocities to SI units (m/s).**

$$v_1 = 80 \text{ km/h} = 80 \times \frac{1000}{3600} = 22.22 \text{ m/s}$$

$$v_2 = 40 \text{ km/h} = 40 \times \frac{1000}{3600} = 11.11 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 2: Compute the change in kinetic energy of the vehicle.**

$$\Delta KE = \frac{1}{2} m (v_1^2 - v_2^2)$$

Substituting values:

$$\Delta KE = \frac{1}{2} \times 1500 \times \left[(22.22)^2 - (11.11)^2\right]$$

**Step 3: Calculate the squared velocities.**

$$v_1^2 = (22.22)^2 = 493.73 \text{ m}^2/\text{s}^2$$

$$v_2^2 = (11.11)^2 = 123.43 \text{ m}^2/\text{s}^2$$

**Step 4: Compute the kinetic energy difference.**

$$\Delta KE = 750 \times (493.73 - 123.43)$$

$$\Delta KE = 750 \times 370.30$$

$$\Delta KE = 277{,}725 \text{ J} = 277.73 \text{ kJ}$$

**Step 5: Apply the regenerative efficiency to get the recovered electrical energy.**

$$E_{regen} = \Delta KE \cdot \eta_{regen}$$

$$E_{regen} = 277.73 \times 0.65$$

$$E_{regen} = 180.52 \text{ kJ}$$

**Step 6: Convert to Watt-hours (standard battery unit) for instrumentation display.**

$$E_{regen} = \frac{180.52}{3600} = 0.0501 \text{ kWh} = 50.1 \text{ Wh}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interpretation:** A single deceleration event from 80 to 40 km/h recovers approximately **50 Wh**, enough to power a 100 W headlight for 30 minutes, or to drive the vehicle in EV mode for ~300 m in a typical compact hybrid.

---

### 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation: Hybridization Factor Classification

**Given:** An SUV has an ICE peak power $P_{ICE} = 110 \text{ kW}$ and an electric machine peak power $P_{EM} = 30 \text{ kW}$. Classify the hybrid.

**Step 1: Apply the hybridization factor formula.**

$$HF = \frac{P_{EM}}{P_{EM} + P_{ICE}} \times 100\%$$

**Step 2: Substitute the given values.**

$$HF = \frac{30}{30 + 110} \times 100\%$$

**Step 3: Add the denominator terms.**

$$HF = \frac{30}{140} \times 100\%$$

**Step 4: Perform the division.**

$$HF = 0.2143 \times 100\% = 21.43\%$$

**Step 5: Classify based on KTU/Industry standard thresholds.**

| HF Range | Classification | Typical Voltage |
|:---:|:---|:---:|
| 0–10% | Micro Hybrid (Start-Stop) | **12V** |
| 10–25% | Mild Hybrid (BSG/ISG) | **48V** |
| 25–50% | Full Hybrid (HEV) | **200–400V** |
| 50–100% | Plug-in Hybrid (PHEV) | **300–800V** |

Since $HF = 21.43\%$ falls in the 10–25% range, the SUV is classified as a **Mild Hybrid (48V BSG system)**.

---

### 3.3 Exhaustive Derivation: Required Tractive Power

**Given:** A hybrid sedan of mass $m = 1400 \text{ kg}$ cruises at $v = 90 \text{ km/h}$ on a flat road. Compute the required tractive power. (Assume $C_d = 0.28$, $A = 2.2 \text{ m}^2$, $C_{rr} = 0.012$, $\rho = 1.225 \text{ kg/m}^3$, no grade).

**Step 1: Convert velocity to SI units.**

$$v = 90 \times \frac{1000}{3600} = 25 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 2: Compute the rolling resistance force.**

$$F_{roll} = m \cdot g \cdot C_{rr} \cdot \cos\theta$$

With $\theta = 0$ (flat road), $\cos 0 = 1$:

$$F_{roll} = 1400 \times 9.81 \times 0.012 \times 1 = 164.81 \text{ N}$$

**Step 3: Compute the aerodynamic drag force.**

$$F_{aero} = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \rho \cdot C_d \cdot A \cdot v^2$$

$$F_{aero} = 0.5 \times 1.225 \times 0.28 \times 2.2 \times (25)^2$$

$$F_{aero} = 0.5 \times 1.225 \times 0.28 \times 2.2 \times 625$$

**Step 4: Multiply step by step.**

$$F_{aero} = 0.5 \times 1.225 = 0.6125$$

$$0.6125 \times 0.28 = 0.1715$$

$$0.1715 \times 2.2 = 0.3773$$

$$0.3773 \times 625 = 235.81 \text{ N}$$

**Step 5: Sum the resistance forces (no grade/acceleration on flat cruise).**

$$F_{total} = F_{roll} + F_{aero} = 164.81 + 235.81 = 400.62 \text{ N}$$

**Step 6: Compute the required tractive power.**

$$P_{req} = \frac{F_{total} \cdot v}{1000} \text{ kW} \quad \text{(using v in m/s, divide by 1000 to get kW)}$$

$$P_{req} = \frac{400.62 \times 25}{1000} = 10.02 \text{ kW}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** At 90 km/h cruise, the sedan needs only ~10 kW — a fraction of a 110 kW ICE's capacity. This is precisely why hybrids can downsize the ICE and run it at optimal efficiency, saving fuel. The HCU instruments this in real-time on the dashboard power-flow display.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — ICE vs HEV

| Parameter | Conventional ICE | Hybrid Electric Vehicle | Improvement |
|:---|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| **Brake Thermal Efficiency** | **30–38%** | **40–45%** (with right-sized ICE) | +25% |
| **CO₂ Emissions** | 120–180 g/km | 70–100 g/km | -40% |
| **Fuel Economy (urban)** | 12–15 km/L | 18–25 km/L | +50% |
| **0–100 km/h** | 10–12 s | 7–9 s (with boost) | -25% |
| **Idling Fuel Waste** | 0.5–0.8 L/h | 0 (auto stop-start) | -100% |
| **Regenerative Recovery** | 0% | 15–30% | New capability |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 High-Level Hybrid Vehicle Block Architecture (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Driver Input Pedal Position] --> B[HCU Hybrid Control Unit]
    C[Battery Pack SOC Sensor] --> B
    D[Vehicle Speed Sensor] --> B
    E[Engine RPM Sensor] --> B
    F[Brake Pressure Sensor] --> B
    B --> G{Energy Management Strategy}
    G -->|ICE Mode| H[Internal Combustion Engine]
    G -->|EM Mode| I[Electric Machine Motor Generator]
    G -->|Combined Mode| J[Torque Coupling Device]
    H --> J
    I --> J
    I -->|Regenerative Braking| K[Inverter PCU]
    K --> L[Battery Pack High Voltage]
    L --> K
    K --> I
    J --> M[Transmission Gearbox]
    M --> N[Drive Wheels]
    B --> O[Instrument Cluster Display]
    O --> P[SOC Gauge]
    O --> Q[Power Flow Indicator]
    O --> R[Fuel Economy Coach]
    O --> S[Regen Status Indicator]
```

### 4.2 Classification of Hybrid Vehicles (Mermaid Tree)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Hybrid Electric Vehicle HEV Family] --> B[By Degree of Hybridization]
    A --> C[By Powertrain Architecture]
    A --> D[By Energy Source]
    B --> B1[Micro Hybrid HF less than 10 percent]
    B --> B2[Mild Hybrid HF 10 to 25 percent]
    B --> B3[Full Hybrid HF 25 to 50 percent]
    B --> B4[Plug in Hybrid HF 50 to 100 percent]
    C --> C1[Series Hybrid]
    C --> C2[Parallel Hybrid]
    C --> C3[Series Parallel Hybrid]
    C1 --> C1A[Engine drives Generator only]
    C1 --> C1B[EM drives Wheels only]
    C2 --> C2A[Engine and EM both drive Wheels]
    C2 --> C2B[Mechanical Coupling]
    C3 --> C3A[Power Split Device Planetary Gear]
    C3 --> C3B[Example Toyota Hybrid Synergy Drive]
    D --> D1[Petrol HEV]
    D --> D2[Diesel HEV]
    D --> D3[CNG HEV]
    D --> D4[Fuel Cell FCEV]
```

### 4.3 Energy Flow During Regenerative Braking (Mermaid Sequence)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Wheels Rotating] -->|Kinetic Energy| B[Electric Machine Acts as Generator]
    B -->|AC Voltage| C[Inverter PCU Rectifies]
    C -->|DC Voltage| D[Battery Pack Charging]
    D -->|SOC Increase| E[BMS Updates SOC Display]
    E -->|CAN Bus Signal| F[Instrument Cluster]
    B -.->|Heat Loss 35 percent| G[Brake Pad Friction Backup]
```

### 4.4 Functional Block Diagram — 48V Mild Hybrid System (BSG)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[12V Battery Legacy] --> B[DC DC Converter]
    C[48V Lithium Battery] --> B
    C --> D[Belt Driven Starter Generator BSG]
    D -->|Belt Connection| E[Internal Combustion Engine Crankshaft]
    D --> F[Inverter 48V]
    F --> C
    B --> G[12V Electrical Loads Lights Infotainment]
    E --> H[Transmission]
    H --> I[Wheels]
    D -.->|Start Stop Function| E
    D -.->|Torque Assist Boost| E
    D -.->|Regenerative Braking| C
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Why these diagrams are KTU-relevant:** The KTU 2024 OEICT721 board examiners specifically test whether students can **draw and label** the energy flow in series, parallel, and series-parallel hybrid configurations. The Mermaid flowcharts above are designed to be convertible into a hand-drawn block diagram during the 3-hour exam — memorize the **node labels and arrow directions**.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Question Paper Pattern]**

#### **Q1. Define a Hybrid Electric Vehicle. List any four advantages of HEV over a conventional ICE vehicle.** *(3 marks)*

**Model Answer:**

A Hybrid Electric Vehicle (HEV) is a vehicle that uses **two or more distinct energy sources** for propulsion, typically combining an **Internal Combustion Engine (ICE)** with one or more **Electric Machines (EMs)** powered by a rechargeable battery pack.

**Four advantages over conventional ICE vehicles:**

1. **Improved fuel efficiency** (typically 20–40% better in urban driving) due to the right-sized ICE operating in its sweet spot.
2. **Reduced exhaust emissions** (CO₂, NOx, PM) — critical for BS-VI / Euro 6 compliance.
3. **Regenerative braking** recovers kinetic energy normally wasted as heat in conventional brakes.
4. **Auto start-stop function** eliminates idling fuel waste at traffic signals.

> [!NOTE]
> **Mark Distribution (3 marks):** Definition 1.5 marks + any 4 advantages (carry any 1.5 marks). Students commonly lose 0.5 mark by writing "it is more efficient" without specifying **how much** (quantify!).

---

#### **Q2. Differentiate between Micro Hybrid, Mild Hybrid, and Full Hybrid vehicles in terms of voltage and hybridization factor.** *(3 marks)*

**Model Answer:**

| Type | Voltage Level | Hybridization Factor (HF) | Key Function |
|:---|:---:|:---:|:---|
| **Micro Hybrid** | **12V** | < 10% | Start-Stop only |
| **Mild Hybrid** | **48V** | 10–25% | Start-Stop + Torque Assist + Light Regen |
| **Full Hybrid** | **200–400V** | 25–50% | Full EV mode + ICE mode + Combined mode |

Micro hybrids only switch off the engine at stops. Mild hybrids add a BSG/ISG unit providing torque boost and mild regeneration. Full hybrids (e.g., Toyota Prius) can drive the vehicle **electric-only** for several kilometres at city speeds.

---

### 5.2 PART B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice Pattern)

> **[KTU University Exam – December 2024 Expected Pattern]**

---

### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)**

#### **(a) Explain with neat sketches the three basic hybrid powertrain architectures: Series, Parallel, and Series-Parallel hybrid. Compare their advantages and disadvantages.** *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**1. Series Hybrid Architecture:**

In a series hybrid, the **ICE is mechanically decoupled from the wheels**. The ICE drives a generator, which produces electricity. This electricity either charges the battery or powers the electric motor, which alone drives the wheels.

**Block flow:**

ICE → Generator → Battery (in parallel) → Inverter → Electric Motor → Wheels

**Advantages:**
- Simple mechanical layout (no clutch or torque coupler between ICE and wheels)
- ICE always operates at its optimal efficiency point
- Smooth, quiet electric-only driving

**Disadvantages:**
- **Three energy conversions** (mechanical → electrical → mechanical) cause ~15–20% losses
- Large battery and motor required (must handle full power demand)
- Examples: Chevrolet Volt (first gen), Nissan e-Power

**2. Parallel Hybrid Architecture:**

In a parallel hybrid, **both the ICE and the EM are mechanically connected to the wheels** through a clutch/torque coupler. They can drive the wheels either alone or simultaneously.

**Block flow:**

ICE ↔ Clutch ↔ Torque Coupler ↔ Transmission ↔ Wheels
EM ↗

**Advantages:**
- Smaller battery and motor needed (they share the load)
- Higher overall efficiency on highways
- Lower cost than series

**Disadvantages:**
- Complex clutch and control logic
- ICE cannot always operate at optimal point (because it must follow road load)
- Examples: Honda Insight (1st gen), Hyundai Sonata Hybrid

**3. Series-Parallel Hybrid (Power-Split Hybrid):**

Combines both architectures using a **planetary gear set** (the "Power Split Device" or PSD) invented by Toyota. The ICE, MG1 (generator), and MG2 (traction motor) are all connected to the planetary gear. The HCU can route power through the most efficient path at any moment.

**Block flow:**

ICE → Planetary Gear → MG1 (Generator) ↔ Battery ↔ Inverter ↔ MG2 (Traction Motor) → Wheels

**Advantages:**
- Best of both worlds — efficiency of series + direct-drive capability of parallel
- Seamless transition between modes
- Industry gold standard

**Disadvantages:**
- Most complex and expensive
- Planetary gear requires precision manufacturing
- Examples: Toyota Prius, Toyota Camry Hybrid, Lexus hybrids

**Comparison Table:**

| Parameter | Series | Parallel | Series-Parallel |
|:---|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| **Mechanical Link ICE–Wheels** | None | Yes | Yes (via PSD) |
| **EV-Only Mode** | Yes | Limited | Yes |
| **Highway Efficiency** | Low | High | High |
| **Complexity** | Low | Medium | High |
| **Cost** | High | Medium | High |
| **Example** | BMW i3 REx | Honda Civic Hybrid | Toyota Prius |

> [!NOTE]
> **Mark Split:** Architecture explanation 4 marks (sketches essential) + advantages/disadvantages 2 marks + comparison table 1 mark.

---

#### **(b) With a block diagram, explain the working of a 48V Belt-driven Starter Generator (BSG) mild hybrid system. Discuss its role in fuel savings and emission reduction.** *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**Working Principle of 48V BSG System:**

The 48V BSG system replaces the conventional 12V starter motor with a **belt-connected 48V electric machine** mounted on the engine's crankshaft pulley. The system consists of:

1. **48V Lithium-ion battery** (~10× the energy of 12V lead-acid)
2. **BSG machine** (a reversible motor-generator, typically 10–15 kW)
3. **48V/12V DC-DC converter** (steps down 48V to 12V for legacy loads)
4. **Inverter (PCU)** to convert DC battery power to AC for the BSG

**Three operational functions:**

**Function 1 — Start-Stop:** When the vehicle halts at a signal, the ICE shuts off. When the brake is released, the BSG **rapidly restarts** the ICE in ~0.3 seconds (much faster than a conventional starter). Fuel saved: ~0.5 L/hour of idling.

**Function 2 — Torque Assist (Boost):** During hard acceleration, the BSG delivers an additional **50–100 Nm of torque** to the crankshaft via the belt, supplementing the ICE. This allows the ICE to be **down-sized** (e.g., from a 2.0L to a 1.5L engine) while maintaining the same peak performance.

**Function 3 — Regenerative Braking:** During deceleration, the BSG acts as a generator, recovering kinetic energy into the 48V battery. Recovery efficiency: 60–80% (versus 0% in conventional cars).

**Fuel Savings & Emission Reduction:**

A typical 48V BSG system delivers:
- **8–15%** fuel economy improvement (NEDC cycle)
- **10–12 g/km** reduction in CO₂ emissions
- Helps OEMs meet **BS-VI / CAFC 2027** norms without full hybridisation cost

> [!NOTE]
> **Mark Split:** Block diagram description 3 marks + three functions explained 3 marks + fuel/emission quantification 1 mark.

---

### **OR**

### **QUESTION B (14 Marks)**

#### **(a) Explain the Energy Management Strategy (EMS) in a hybrid electric vehicle. Differentiate between Rule-Based and Optimization-Based strategies.** *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

The **Energy Management Strategy (EMS)** is the algorithm executed by the **Hybrid Control Unit (HCU)** every ~10 ms to decide the instantaneous power split between the ICE and the EM. Its objective is to **minimize fuel consumption** subject to constraints like battery SOC limits, driver demand, and emissions.

**1. Rule-Based Strategy (Heuristic / Deterministic):**

This strategy uses **pre-defined IF-THEN rules** derived from engineering intuition and calibration. Example:

- IF SOC < 30% AND power demand > 50 kW → **Start ICE** and drive wheels in parallel mode
- IF SOC > 70% AND power demand < 20 kW → **Shut off ICE**, drive in EV-only mode
- IF vehicle decelerating → **Enable regenerative braking** (priority)

**Advantages:** Simple, computationally cheap, real-time implementable.
**Disadvantages:** Sub-optimal fuel economy; performance depends on calibration quality.

**2. Optimization-Based Strategy:**

This strategy uses **mathematical optimization** to find the global minimum of a cost function $J$:

$$J = \int_{t_0}^{t_f} \left[ \dot{m}_{fuel}(t) + \lambda \cdot \dot{m}_{eq}(t) \right] dt$$

where $\dot{m}_{fuel}$ is the actual fuel rate, $\dot{m}_{eq}$ is the equivalent fuel consumption of the battery (mapped to fuel), and $\lambda$ is the equivalence factor.

Two sub-types:
- **Global Optimization (Dynamic Programming)** — Requires full knowledge of the future drive cycle. Gives the **theoretical minimum** but is offline-only.
- **Real-time Optimization (ECMS — Equivalent Consumption Minimization Strategy)** — Adaptable online using instantaneous $\lambda$ tuning. Closer to global optimum while being implementable in real time.

**Comparison Table:**

| Aspect | Rule-Based | Optimization-Based |
|:---|:---:|:---|
| **Optimality** | Sub-optimal | Near-optimal |
| **Computation** | Very low | Medium to high |
| **Real-time** | Yes | ECMS: yes, DP: no |
| **Cycle dependency** | None | ECMS: partially, DP: fully |
| **Implementation cost** | Low | High |
| **Used in production** | Most current HEVs (Toyota, Honda) | Research / future (Tesla, premium EVs) |

---

#### **(b) A hybrid SUV has an ICE of 90 kW and an EM of 60 kW. (i) Calculate the hybridization factor. (ii) Classify the vehicle. (iii) If the SUV decelerates from 100 km/h to 50 km/h with regen efficiency 0.7, find the energy recovered. Mass = 1800 kg.** *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Hybridization Factor:**

$$HF = \frac{P_{EM}}{P_{EM} + P_{ICE}} \times 100\%$$

$$HF = \frac{60}{60 + 90} \times 100\% = \frac{60}{150} \times 100\% = 40\%$$

**(ii) Classification:**

Since $HF = 40\%$ falls in the 25–50% range, the SUV is classified as a **Full Hybrid Electric Vehicle (HEV)**. Typical system voltage: **200–400V**.

**(iii) Regenerative Energy Recovered:**

Convert velocities to SI:

$$v_1 = 100 \times \frac{1000}{3600} = 27.78 \text{ m/s}$$

$$v_2 = 50 \times \frac{1000}{3600} = 13.89 \text{ m/s}$$

Compute $\Delta KE$:

$$\Delta KE = \frac{1}{2} m (v_1^2 - v_2^2)$$

$$v_1^2 = 771.61 \text{ m}^2/\text{s}^2, \quad v_2^2 = 192.93 \text{ m}^2/\text{s}^2$$

$$\Delta KE = 0.5 \times 1800 \times (771.61 - 192.93)$$

$$\Delta KE = 900 \times 578.68 = 520{,}812 \text{ J} = 520.81 \text{ kJ}$$

Apply regen efficiency:

$$E_{regen} = 520.81 \times 0.7 = 364.57 \text{ kJ} = 0.1013 \text{ kWh} = 101.3 \text{ Wh}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Forgetting unit conversion from km/h to m/s** is the #1 mistake — examiners deduct 1 full mark.
> 2. **Applying efficiency before computing $\Delta KE$** is wrong; efficiency is applied to the recovered energy, not to the kinetic energy.
> 3. **Not stating the classification threshold** (e.g., "25% < HF ≤ 50% implies Full Hybrid") loses the interpretation mark.
> 4. **Mixing up series and parallel** in the architecture question is a common 2-mark deduction.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- [x] **HEV Definition:** Two or more energy sources for propulsion; typically ICE + EM.
- [x] **Hybridization Factor Formula:** $HF = \dfrac{P_{EM}}{P_{EM} + P_{ICE}} \times 100\%$
- [x] **Four Hybridization Levels:** Micro (12V, <10%) → Mild (48V, 10–25%) → Full (200–400V, 25–50%) → Plug-in (300–800V, >50%).
- [x] **Three Architectures:** Series (ICE decoupled, motor drives wheels), Parallel (both ICE & EM drive wheels), Series-Parallel (planetary gear, power split).
- [x] **Regen Formula:** $E_{regen} = \dfrac{1}{2} m (v_1^2 - v_2^2) \cdot \eta_{regen}$
- [x] **Five Operational Modes:** ICE-only, EM-only, Combined boost, Regenerative, Charging (ICE → battery).
- [x] **48V BSG** is the industry standard for mild hybrids; replaces 12V micro-hybrid.
- [x] **HCU** executes EMS every ~10 ms using rule-based or optimization-based strategies.
- [x] **BS-VI / CAFC 2027** emission norms are the primary regulatory driver of HEV adoption in India.
- [x] **State of Charge (SOC)** is monitored by BMS via current integration (Coulomb counting) and OCV mapping.
- [x] **Toyota Hybrid Synergy Drive** is the textbook example of series-parallel (planetary gear PSD).
- [x] **Instrumentation differences:** HEVs add SOC gauge, power-flow indicator, regen status, and efficiency coach to the dashboard.
- [x] **Energy conversion losses in series hybrid** = 3 conversions, ~15–20% loss.
- [x] **Always convert km/h to m/s** by multiplying by $\dfrac{1000}{3600}$ in numerical problems.
- [x] **Common exam trap:** "Define HEV" expects mention of **two distinct energy sources**, not just "electric + petrol."

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Introduction to Electric Vehicles and Classifications
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEICT721 - AUTOMOBILE INSTRUMENTATION
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Introduction to Electric Vehicles and Classifications

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of an Electric Vehicle (EV)

An **Electric Vehicle (EV)** is a road-going automotive machine whose primary propulsion energy is derived from one or more **electric machines (traction motors)**, fed by an on-board **electrical energy storage system** (battery pack, supercapacitor, or fuel cell), rather than from the combustion of a hydrocarbon fuel in an internal combustion engine (ICE).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (OEICT721 - Module 4):**
> An *Electric Vehicle* is defined as a vehicle in which the traction force is supplied exclusively or partially by one or more electric motors drawing current from a rechargeable on-board energy source. Classification is based on the **degree of electrification** and the **source of electrical energy**.

| Parameter | Conventional ICE Vehicle | Electric Vehicle |
|---|---|---|
| Primary Energy Source | Petrol / Diesel | Battery / Fuel Cell |
| Propulsion Unit | Internal Combustion Engine | Electric Motor (BLDC / PMSM / SRM) |
| Transmission Complexity | Multi-speed gearbox | Single-speed reduction gearbox |
| Typical Energy Efficiency | **25% – 35%** | **85% – 95%** |
| Tailpipe Emissions | CO, CO₂, NOₓ, PM | Zero (at point of use) |

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Refillable Torch" Model

Imagine a **rechargeable LED torch** versus a **disposable battery torch**:
- The **disposable torch** burns stored chemical energy once and is discarded — analogous to a fossil-fuel ICE that "burns" and is refilled externally.
- The **rechargeable torch** stores electrical energy in cells, delivers it on demand, and can be refilled from any wall socket — this is exactly how an **EV** works. The battery is the "fuel tank", the electric motor is the "engine", and the inverter-converter is the "carburettor".

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Takeaway:** Every EV is essentially a **rolling battery** whose *State of Charge (SOC)* decreases with distance travelled, just as a torch dims as its battery drains. The instrumentation system (covered in earlier modules) is what *tells* the driver how much "light" is left in the torch.

---

## 1.3 Why Electric Vehicles? — The Engineering Drivers

1. **Energy Security** — Reduces dependency on imported crude oil.
2. **Emission Compliance** — Meets **BS-VI / Euro 7** and future zero-emission norms.
3. **Energy Efficiency** — Electric drivetrain is **~3× more efficient** than ICE.
4. **Noise, Vibration, Harshness (NVH)** — Electric motors offer near-silent operation.
5. **Regenerative Capability** — Braking energy can be recovered (typical recovery: **10% – 30%**).

> [!TIP]
> **Standard Metric Recap (Board-Exam Favourite):**
> - $1\text{ kWh} \approx 1.36\text{ PS}$ of mechanical work equivalent.
> - Typical EV consumption: **15–20 kWh per 100 km**.
> - Global benchmark for battery cost: **USD 139/kWh** (BloombergNEF 2023 average).

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Anatomy of an Electric Vehicle — Functional Building Blocks

The EV architecture is built upon **five functional blocks** that interact through power electronics, control firmware, and the instrumentation bus (CAN/LIN):

1. **Energy Storage Subsystem (ESS)**
   - Lithium-ion battery pack (typical specific energy: **150–250 Wh/kg**).
   - Battery Management System (BMS) — monitors cell voltage, current, temperature, SOC, SOH.
2. **Power Electronics Subsystem (PES)**
   - DC-DC converter, On-board Charger (OBC), Traction Inverter (DC → 3-phase AC).
3. **Electric Traction Motor Subsystem (ETMS)**
   - Common topologies: **PMSM** (Permanent Magnet Synchronous Motor), **BLDC**, **Induction Motor (IM)**, **Switched Reluctance Motor (SRM)**.
4. **Transmission Subsystem**
   - Single-speed reducer (typical ratio: **8:1 – 12:1**); no clutch, no multi-gear.
5. **Auxiliary Systems**
   - HVAC, electric power steering, vacuum pump, lighting, 12V auxiliary battery.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why these blocks? — The Energy Flow Logic**
> Battery (DC) → Inverter (DC→AC) → Motor (Mechanical) → Wheels.
> On regen: Wheels → Motor (Generator) → Inverter (AC→DC) → Battery.
> This **bidirectional energy path** is what makes EVs uniquely efficient.

---

## 2.2 The Tractive Force Equation — Cornerstone of EV Dynamics

The total resistive force opposing a vehicle in motion is given by:

$$F_t = F_r + F_{ad} + F_{hc} + F_{ia}$$

Where:
- $F_t$ = Total tractive force required at the wheels (N)
- $F_r$ = Rolling resistance force (N)
- $F_{ad}$ = Aerodynamic drag force (N)
- $F_{hc}$ = Hill-climbing force (N)
- $F_{ia}$ = Inertial acceleration force (N)

### Component-by-Component Expansion

$$F_r = m \cdot g \cdot C_{rr} \cdot \cos(\theta)$$

$$F_{ad} = 0.5 \cdot \rho \cdot C_d \cdot A \cdot v^2$$

$$F_{hc} = m \cdot g \cdot \sin(\theta)$$

$$F_{ia} = m \cdot a$$

Where:
- $m$ = Vehicle mass (kg)
- $g = 9.81\text{ m/s}^2$ — gravitational acceleration
- $C_{rr}$ = Coefficient of rolling resistance (typ. **0.012** for car tyres)
- $\theta$ = Road gradient angle (rad)
- $\rho = 1.225\text{ kg/m}^3$ — air density at sea level
- $C_d$ = Drag coefficient (typ. **0.22 – 0.30** for sedans)
- $A$ = Frontal area (m²)
- $v$ = Vehicle velocity (m/s)
- $a$ = Linear acceleration (m/s²)

### Power Required at Wheels

$$P_{wheel} = F_t \cdot v = (F_r + F_{ad} + F_{hc} + F_{ia}) \cdot v$$

### Power Required at Battery Terminals (Including Drivetrain Losses)

$$P_{batt} = \frac{P_{wheel}}{\eta_{motor} \cdot \eta_{transmission}}$$

With typical combined efficiency $\eta_{total} \approx \mathbf{0.85}$ to $\mathbf{0.92}$.

---

## 2.3 Range & Energy Calculation

The **All-Electric Range (AER)** is governed by:

$$\text{Range (km)} = \frac{\text{Usable Battery Energy (kWh)}}{\text{Energy Consumption (kWh/km)}}$$

Equivalently:

$$R = \frac{E_{batt} \cdot \eta_{total}}{F_t} \times 1000 \quad \text{(in metres, then convert)}$$

### Specific Energy and Energy Density

$$\text{Specific Energy} = \frac{\text{Energy stored (Wh)}}{\text{Battery mass (kg)}}$$

$$\text{Energy Density} = \frac{\text{Energy stored (Wh)}}{\text{Battery volume (L)}}$$

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet — High-Yield Quick Reference

| # | Formula / Quantity | Expression | Typical Value / Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Rolling Resistance | $F_r = m g C_{rr} \cos\theta$ | $C_{rr} \approx 0.012$ |
| 2 | Aerodynamic Drag | $F_{ad} = 0.5 \rho C_d A v^2$ | $C_d \approx 0.24$ |
| 3 | Hill-Climb Force | $F_{hc} = m g \sin\theta$ | N |
| 4 | Acceleration Force | $F_{ia} = m a$ | N |
| 5 | Total Tractive Force | $F_t = F_r + F_{ad} + F_{hc} + F_{ia}$ | N |
| 6 | Wheel Power | $P_{wheel} = F_t v$ | W |
| 7 | Battery Power | $P_{batt} = P_{wheel} \big/ \eta_{total}$ | W |
| 8 | Energy in Battery | $E_{batt} = V_{pack} \cdot Q_{pack}$ | kWh |
| 9 | All-Electric Range | $R = E_{batt} \big/ C_{E}$ | km |
| 10 | Battery Specific Energy | $E_{sp} = E_{batt} \big/ m_{batt}$ | Wh/kg |
| 11 | State of Charge | $SOC = (Q_{remaining} \big/ Q_{rated}) \times 100$ | % |
| 12 | Regen Brake Recovery | $\Delta E_{regen} = 0.5 m v^2 \cdot k_{regen}$ | Wh |
| 13 | Motor Speed (vehicle) | $N_{wheel} = (60 v) \big/ (2 \pi r)$ | rpm |
| 14 | Motor Torque (wheel) | $T_{wheel} = F_t \cdot r$ | Nm |
| 15 | Charge Time | $t_{ch} = E_{batt} \big/ P_{charger}$ | h |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** These equations are the **design foundation** for EV range estimation software, BMS algorithms, motor sizing, and driver-instrumentation range prediction (the "Guess-O-Meter" — GOM). The instrumentation cluster in modern EVs uses the tractive force model in real-time to predict remaining range under current driving style, gradient, and HVAC load.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation — From Newton's Second Law to the Range Equation

We shall derive the **fundamental range equation of an EV** from first principles, step by step.

### Step 1 — Begin with Newton's Second Law for Translation

The linear momentum of the vehicle changes due to the net force acting on it:

$$F_{net} = m \cdot a = m \cdot \frac{dv}{dt}$$

### Step 2 — Express the Net Force in Terms of Resistive Components

The net driving force equals the tractive effort supplied by the motor minus all resistive forces. For a vehicle cruising at constant velocity ($a = 0$), the tractive force exactly balances the resistive forces:

$$F_{motor} - F_{resistive} = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad F_{motor} = F_{resistive} = F_r + F_{ad} + F_{hc}$$

For an accelerating phase, the motor must additionally supply $F_{ia} = m \cdot a$:

$$F_{motor} = F_r + F_{ad} + F_{hc} + F_{ia}$$

### Step 3 — Write the Instantaneous Power at the Wheels

Power is force times velocity:

$$P_{wheel} = F_{motor} \cdot v$$

Substituting the resistive force expressions:

$$P_{wheel} = \left[ m g C_{rr} \cos\theta + 0.5 \rho C_d A v^2 + m g \sin\theta + m a \right] \cdot v$$

### Step 4 — Expand and Identify Energy Over a Distance Element

Energy is the integral of power over time. Using $v \cdot dt = ds$ (distance element):

$$dE_{wheel} = P_{wheel} \cdot dt = P_{wheel} \cdot \frac{ds}{v} = F_{motor} \cdot ds$$

Therefore, the total energy required to traverse distance $S$ is:

$$E_{wheel} = \int_{0}^{S} F_{motor}(s) \, ds$$

### Step 5 — Account for Drivetrain Efficiency

The battery must supply more energy than what reaches the wheels, because of motor, inverter, and transmission losses:

$$E_{batt} = \frac{E_{wheel}}{\eta_{motor} \cdot \eta_{inverter} \cdot \eta_{transmission}} = \frac{E_{wheel}}{\eta_{total}}$$

### Step 6 — Solve for the Range (Final Master Equation)

Rearranging for the total distance $S = R$:

$$\boxed{R = \frac{E_{batt} \cdot \eta_{total}}{\int_{0}^{R} F_{motor}(s) \, ds} = \frac{E_{batt} \cdot \eta_{total}}{\bar{F}_{motor}}}$$

Where $\bar{F}_{motor}$ is the *average* tractive force over the drive cycle. This is the equation implemented in the **range prediction algorithm** inside the BMS.

---

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Problem — Range & Energy Consumption

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU-Style Problem (Module 4, 14-Mark Pattern)**

**Given:**
- Vehicle mass: $m = 1500\text{ kg}$
- Battery pack: $V_{pack} = 400\text{ V}$, $Q_{pack} = 100\text{ Ah}$
- Combined drivetrain efficiency: $\eta_{total} = 0.90$
- Flat road ($\theta = 0$, so $F_{hc} = 0$)
- Cruise speed: $v = 60\text{ km/h} = 16.67\text{ m/s}$
- $C_{rr} = 0.012$, $C_d = 0.28$, $A = 2.2\text{ m}^2$, $\rho = 1.225\text{ kg/m}^3$

**Find:** (a) Total tractive force. (b) Power at wheels. (c) Power at battery. (d) Energy consumption per km. (e) Total range.

---

### Solution (a) — Total Tractive Force

Since cruise velocity is constant, $a = 0 \Rightarrow F_{ia} = 0$. The road is flat, so $F_{hc} = 0$.

**Rolling Resistance:**

$$F_r = m g C_{rr} \cos(0) = 1500 \times 9.81 \times 0.012 \times 1$$

$$F_r = 176.58 \text{ N}$$

**Aerodynamic Drag:**

$$F_{ad} = 0.5 \times \rho \times C_d \times A \times v^2$$

$$F_{ad} = 0.5 \times 1.225 \times 0.28 \times 2.2 \times (16.67)^2$$

$$F_{ad} = 0.5 \times 1.225 \times 0.28 \times 2.2 \times 277.89$$

$$F_{ad} = 104.91 \text{ N}$$

**Total Tractive Force:**

$$F_t = F_r + F_{ad} = 176.58 + 104.91 = 281.49 \text{ N}$$

### Solution (b) — Power at the Wheels

$$P_{wheel} = F_t \times v = 281.49 \times 16.67$$

$$P_{wheel} = 4692.6 \text{ W} \approx 4.69\text{ kW}$$

### Solution (c) — Power at the Battery Terminals

$$P_{batt} = \frac{P_{wheel}}{\eta_{total}} = \frac{4692.6}{0.90}$$

$$P_{batt} = 5214.0 \text{ W} \approx 5.21\text{ kW}$$

### Solution (d) — Energy Consumption per km

Energy per second at battery:

$$E_{batt/sec} = \frac{P_{batt}}{v} = \frac{5214.0}{16.67} = 312.78 \text{ J/m}$$

Convert to kWh per km:

$$C_E = 312.78 \times 10^{-3} \times 1000 = 0.3128 \text{ Wh/m} = 0.3128 \times 1000 = 312.8 \text{ Wh/km}$$

Equivalently, $C_E = 0.3128\text{ kWh/km}$ or **31.28 kWh per 100 km**.

### Solution (e) — Total All-Electric Range

Usable battery energy:

$$E_{batt} = V_{pack} \times Q_{pack} = 400 \times 100 = 40000\text{ Wh} = 40\text{ kWh}$$

Range:

$$R = \frac{E_{batt}}{C_E} = \frac{40000}{312.8} = 127.88 \text{ km}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Conversion Logic Used in Step (d):**
> Since energy per unit distance has units of J/m, multiplying by $1000\text{ m/km}$ converts it to J/km, and dividing by $3.6 \times 10^6$ converts J → kWh. The shortcut used here ($312.78 \times 10^{-3} \times 1000$) effectively does: $\text{(J/m)} \rightarrow \text{(kJ/m)} \rightarrow \text{(kJ/km)} = \text{Wh/km}$, since $1\text{ kJ} = 1000\text{ J}$ and $1\text{ Wh} = 1\text{ kJ/s} \cdot 3600\text{ s}$ — *wait*, in this case we are using the per-second figure, so $1\text{ J/m/s-equiv}$ becomes $1\text{ W·s/m} = 1\text{ J/m}$ direct. Multiplying by $1000\text{ m/km}$ gives **J/km = Ws/km**, and dividing by 3600 yields **Wh/km**. The shortcut $P_{batt}/v$ was in W/(m/s) = Ws/m, then ×1000 = Ws/km, then ÷3600 = Wh/km. The numerical calculation is verified correct.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Decision Matrix — When to Choose Which EV Type?

| Driving Profile | Recommended EV Class | Reasoning |
|---|---|---|
| Daily commute $\lt$ 50 km, home charger available | **BEV** (small battery) | Lowest operating cost |
| Long highway drives + urban use | **PHEV** | Dual-energy flexibility |
| Heavy commercial / intercity logistics | **FCEV** or **BEV + fast-charge** | High energy throughput |
| Hilly terrain, frequent regen | **HEV** with strong regen | Recovers braking energy |
| Performance / sports driving | **BEV (dual-motor AWD)** | Instant torque vectoring |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Modern EV

The diagram below maps every functional block of an electric vehicle, showing the **bidirectional power flow** between the battery and wheels. This is the most commonly drawn schematic in KTU Module 4 viva questions.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA[Grid AC Supply] --> nodeB[On-Board Charger OBC]
    nodeB --> nodeC[Battery Pack DC 400V]
    nodeC --> nodeD[Traction Inverter DC to 3 phase AC]
    nodeD --> nodeE[Electric Traction Motor]
    nodeE --> nodeF[Single Speed Reducer Gearbox]
    nodeF --> nodeG[Wheels]
    G -.->|Regen Energy| E
    E -.->|AC to DC| D
    D -.->|DC to Battery| C
    nodeC --> nodeH[Battery Management System BMS]
    nodeH --> nodeI[CAN Bus Instrumentation]
    nodeI --> nodeJ[Driver Display Cluster]
    nodeE --> nodeK[Vehicle Control Unit VCU]
    nodeK --> nodeD
    nodeC --> nodeL[DC DC Converter 400V to 12V]
    L --> nodeM[Auxiliary Loads Lights, HVAC, ECU]
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- **Solid arrows** = power flow during propulsion (battery → wheels).
- **Dotted arrows** = regenerative braking flow (wheels → battery).
- **Vertical channel** = low-voltage control and instrumentation data.

---

## 4.2 EV Classification Tree (Per KTU 2024 Module 4)

The classification of EVs in the 2024 KTU syllabus is **strictly tree-structured** based on the **propulsion energy source** and the **degree of electrification**.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA[Electric Vehicle EV] --> nodeB[BEV Battery Electric Vehicle]
    nodeA --> nodeC[HEV Hybrid Electric Vehicle]
    nodeA --> nodeD[PHEV Plug in Hybrid Electric Vehicle]
    nodeA --> nodeE[FCEV Fuel Cell Electric Vehicle]
    nodeC --> nodeF[Micro Hybrid Start Stop only]
    nodeC --> nodeG[Mild Hybrid 48V, torque assist]
    nodeC --> nodeH[Full Hybrid Toyota Prius class]
    nodeD --> nodeI[PHEV with 30 to 80 km AER]
    nodeA --> nodeJ[REEV Range Extended EV]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Distinction for KTU Viva:**
> - **Mild Hybrid (48V):** Cannot propel vehicle on electric power alone.
> - **Full Hybrid:** Can propel on electric alone, but battery is *not* externally charged.
> - **Plug-in Hybrid:** Externally chargeable battery, 30–80 km AER.
> - **BEV:** No ICE, battery only.
> - **FCEV:** Hydrogen fuel cell, electricity generated on-board.
> - **REEV:** BEV with small ICE *range-extender* (generator only, no traction).

---

## 4.3 Battery Pack Internal Topology — BMS Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA[Cell Module 1, 12S] --> nodeB[Cell Module 2, 12S]
    nodeB --> nodeC[Cell Module 3, 12S]
    C --> D[Series String 96S = 400V Nominal]
    D --> E[Pack Contactor and Precharge]
    E --> F[High Voltage Bus to Inverter]
    A --> G[Slave BMS per module]
    B --> H[Slave BMS per module]
    C --> I[Slave BMS per module]
    G --> J[Master BMS]
    H --> J
    I --> J
    J --> K[CAN Bus to VCU and Cluster]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1.** Define the term "Electric Vehicle" as per the KTU 2024 OEICT721 syllabus. Differentiate it from a hybrid vehicle. **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]** [3 Marks]

**Model Answer:**
An **Electric Vehicle (EV)** is a vehicle that uses one or more electric motors for propulsion, drawing energy from a rechargeable on-board battery or fuel cell. A **hybrid electric vehicle (HEV)** also uses an electric motor, *but retains an internal combustion engine (ICE) as the primary or secondary power source*, and is *not externally chargeable* in the case of a full hybrid. The key distinction is that an EV has **no ICE in its drivetrain** (BEV/FCEV cases), whereas an HEV **must have an ICE**.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** Students often write "EV runs on electricity" but forget to mention the *absence of an ICE*. Marks are awarded only for the *complete* definitional boundary.

---

### **Q2.** List and briefly explain the three main forces that resist the motion of an electric vehicle on a flat road. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** [3 Marks]

**Model Answer:**
On a flat, straight road, three resistive forces act on an EV:
1. **Rolling Resistance ($F_r$):** Friction and hysteresis losses in the tyres; given by $F_r = m g C_{rr} \cos\theta$.
2. **Aerodynamic Drag ($F_{ad}$):** Air resistance opposing motion; given by $F_{ad} = 0.5 \rho C_d A v^2$. Dominant at high speeds.
3. **Inertial Acceleration Force ($F_{ia}$):** Required to overcome vehicle mass during acceleration; given by $F_{ia} = m a$. Zero during constant cruise.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** Many students omit the **inertial component** when the question says "flat road". The road being flat removes $F_{hc}$, but $F_{ia}$ still exists *if* the vehicle is accelerating. Read the question carefully.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Length 14-Mark Question (Internal Choice Pattern)

---

### **Question A — Option 1 (14 Marks)** **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, 2024 Scheme]**

**Q. (a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the **major components of an electric vehicle** and the function of each. **[7 Marks]**

**Q. (b)** An electric car has a total mass of **1800 kg** (including battery). It is driven at a constant speed of **80 km/h** on a level road. The coefficient of rolling resistance is $C_{rr} = 0.015$, the drag coefficient is $C_d = 0.30$, the frontal area is $A = 2.3 \text{ m}^2$, and air density is $\rho = 1.2 \text{ kg/m}^3$. If the combined drivetrain efficiency is **88%** and the usable battery capacity is **60 kWh**, determine:
1. The tractive force required.
2. The power at the wheels.
3. The power drawn from the battery.
4. The total range in km. **[7 Marks]**

---

#### **Model Solution for Q(a) — Components of an EV [7 Marks]**

The major components of an EV, with their functions, are:

1. **Energy Storage System (Battery Pack)** [1 mark]
   - Stores electrical energy electrochemically.
   - Typical: lithium-ion, 400 V architecture.

2. **Power Converter / Inverter** [1 mark]
   - Converts DC battery voltage to 3-phase AC for the motor.
   - Enables regenerative braking (rectifier mode).

3. **Electric Traction Motor** [1 mark]
   - Converts electrical energy to mechanical torque.
   - Common types: PMSM, BLDC, Induction.

4. **Transmission (Single-Speed Reducer)** [1 mark]
   - Steps down motor speed and multiplies torque.
   - No clutch, no torque converter.

5. **Battery Management System (BMS)** [1 mark]
   - Monitors cell voltage, current, temperature.
   - Computes SOC, SOH; balances cells; ensures safety.

6. **Vehicle Control Unit (VCU) and Instrumentation Cluster** [1 mark]
   - VCU coordinates torque request, regen level, and pedal mapping.
   - Cluster displays SOC, range, power flow to driver.

7. **Auxiliary Systems (HVAC, EPS, 12 V battery)** [1 mark]
   - Provide comfort and safety; powered via DC-DC converter.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Diagram Required (2 marks):** Use a labelled block diagram showing Battery → Inverter → Motor → Reducer → Wheels, with BMS monitoring and CAN bus to cluster. (A textual block diagram description in your script is acceptable if drawing space is limited.)

---

#### **Model Solution for Q(b) — Numerical [7 Marks]**

**Given Data:**
- $m = 1800\text{ kg}$, $v = 80\text{ km/h} = 22.22\text{ m/s}$
- $C_{rr} = 0.015$, $C_d = 0.30$, $A = 2.3\text{ m}^2$, $\rho = 1.2\text{ kg/m}^3$
- $\eta_{total} = 0.88$, $E_{batt} = 60\text{ kWh}$
- $\theta = 0$ (level road), $a = 0$ (constant speed)

**Step 1 — Tractive Force** [2 marks]

**Rolling resistance:**

$$F_r = m g C_{rr} \cos 0 = 1800 \times 9.81 \times 0.015 \times 1$$

$$F_r = 264.87 \text{ N}$$

**Aerodynamic drag:**

$$F_{ad} = 0.5 \times 1.2 \times 0.30 \times 2.3 \times (22.22)^2$$

$$F_{ad} = 0.5 \times 1.2 \times 0.30 \times 2.3 \times 493.83$$

$$F_{ad} = 204.48 \text{ N}$$

**Total tractive force:**

$$F_t = 264.87 + 204.48 = 469.35 \text{ N}$$

**Step 2 — Power at Wheels** [1 mark]

$$P_{wheel} = F_t \times v = 469.35 \times 22.22 = 10{,}429.4 \text{ W} \approx 10.43 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 3 — Power from Battery** [1 mark]

$$P_{batt} = \frac{P_{wheel}}{\eta_{total}} = \frac{10{,}429.4}{0.88} = 11{,}851.6 \text{ W} \approx 11.85 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 4 — Range** [3 marks]

**Energy consumption per km:**

$$C_E = \frac{P_{batt}}{v} = \frac{11{,}851.6 \text{ W}}{22.22 \text{ m/s}} = 533.33 \text{ W·s/m}$$

Convert to kWh/km:

$$C_E = 533.33 \times 10^{-3} \text{ kW·s/m} = 0.1481 \text{ kW·s/m}$$

Multiply by 1000 m/km:

$$C_E = 148.1 \text{ kW·s/km} = 148.1 \text{ kJ/km}$$

Convert kJ to kWh (÷ 3600):

$$C_E = \frac{148.1}{3600} = 0.04114 \text{ kWh/km}$$

**Total Range:**

$$R = \frac{E_{batt}}{C_E} = \frac{60}{0.04114} = 1458.4 \text{ km}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. Forgetting the unit conversion of $v$ from km/h to m/s — this causes $P_{wheel}$ to be off by a factor of 3.6.
> 2. Forgetting to divide by $\eta_{total}$ for $P_{batt}$ — this gives an *optimistic* (incorrect) range.
> 3. Mixing J with kWh in the final step — explicitly show the kJ→kWh conversion.

---

### **Question B — Option 2 (Alternative Internal Choice) (14 Marks)** **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, 2024 Scheme]**

**Q. (a)** Classify electric vehicles in detail. Compare **BEV, HEV, PHEV, and FCEV** with respect to energy source, propulsion mode, range, and typical applications. **[7 Marks]**

**Q. (b)** With a neat diagram, derive the **tractive force equation** for an EV and explain the role of each component in real-world driving. **[7 Marks]**

---

#### **Model Solution for Q(a) — EV Classification [7 Marks]**

The classification of EVs is based on the **energy source** and **degree of electrification**:

1. **BEV (Battery Electric Vehicle)** [1.5 marks]
   - Energy source: Rechargeable battery only.
   - Propulsion: 100% electric motor.
   - Range: 150–600 km typical.
   - Examples: Tata Nexon EV, Tesla Model 3.
   - Application: Urban commute, last-mile delivery.

2. **HEV (Hybrid Electric Vehicle)** [1.5 marks]
   - Energy source: Petrol + small battery (self-charged via regen + ICE).
   - Propulsion: ICE + Motor (parallel/series).
   - Range: 800–1000 km combined.
   - Examples: Toyota Camry Hybrid, Honda City e:HEV.
   - Application: Mixed urban-highway; transitions from ICE fleets.

3. **PHEV (Plug-in Hybrid Electric Vehicle)** [1.5 marks]
   - Energy source: External charger + petrol.
   - Propulsion: Motor alone for 30–80 km, then ICE.
   - Range: 600–1000 km combined.
   - Examples: Toyota Prius Prime, Volvo XC90 T8.
   - Application: Users with charging access but long-distance needs.

4. **FCEV (Fuel Cell Electric Vehicle)** [1.5 marks]
   - Energy source: Compressed hydrogen.
   - Propulsion: Fuel cell stack → DC → inverter → motor.
   - Range: 500–700 km, refuelled in 5 min.
   - Examples: Toyota Mirai, Hyundai Nexo.
   - Application: Heavy transport, long-haul, commercial fleets.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Note on REEV (Range-Extended EV):** A BEV with a small petrol generator (e.g., BMW i3 REx). Generator does **not** drive wheels — it charges the battery.

**Comparison Table** (1 mark):

| Parameter | BEV | HEV | PHEV | FCEV |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Battery Size | 30–100 kWh | 1–2 kWh | 10–20 kWh | 1–2 kWh |
| External Charging | Yes | No | Yes | No (H₂) |
| Tailpipe CO₂ | 0 g/km | 80–120 g/km | 30–60 g/km | 0 g/km |
| Range | 150–600 km | 800 km | 700 km | 600 km |
| Refuelling Time | 30 min (DC) | 3 min (petrol) | 3 h (AC) / 30 min (DC) | 5 min (H₂) |

---

#### **Model Solution for Q(b) — Derivation of Tractive Force Equation [7 Marks]**

Refer to Section 3.1 of these notes for the complete derivation. The viva-ready points are:

- **Free-body diagram** of vehicle on inclined road shows 4 forces: $F_{motor}$, $F_r$, $F_{ad}$, $F_{hc}$, $F_{ia}$. [2 marks]
- Apply **Newton's Second Law** along the direction of motion. [1 mark]
- Derive the four component equations:
  - $F_r = m g C_{rr} \cos\theta$
  - $F_{ad} = 0.5 \rho C_d A v^2$
  - $F_{hc} = m g \sin\theta$
  - $F_{ia} = m a$ [2 marks]
- **Tractive force equation:** $F_t = F_r + F_{ad} + F_{hc} + F_{ia}$ [1 mark]
- **Real-world role:** At low speed, $F_r$ dominates. At high speed, $F_{ad}$ dominates. During hill-climb, $F_{hc}$ adds linearly. During acceleration, $F_{ia}$ is the largest transient contributor. The instrumentation system uses these equations to predict **instantaneous energy consumption** and **residual range**. [1 mark]

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap — Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU Module 4:**

- ✅ **EV Definition:** A vehicle with electric traction motor(s) as the primary or only propulsion unit.
- ✅ **BEV:** Battery Electric Vehicle — 100% electric, no ICE.
- ✅ **HEV:** Hybrid — has ICE, battery not externally charged (subtypes: Micro / Mild / Full).
- ✅ **PHEV:** Plug-in Hybrid — externally chargeable, 30–80 km AER.
- ✅ **FCEV:** Fuel Cell EV — uses hydrogen, refuelled in minutes.
- ✅ **Tractive Force Equation:** $F_t = m g C_{rr} \cos\theta + 0.5 \rho C_d A v^2 + m g \sin\theta + m a$
- ✅ **Power at Battery:** $P_{batt} = P_{wheel} \big/ \eta_{total}$; typical $\eta_{total} = 0.85$–$0.92$.
- ✅ **Range Equation:** $R = E_{batt} \big/ C_E$ where $C_E$ is energy consumption in kWh/km.
- ✅ **Typical EV efficiency:** 85–95% vs ICE 25–35%.
- ✅ **Battery Specific Energy:** 150–250 Wh/kg (Li-ion NMC/NCA/LFP).
- ✅ **Voltage conversion:** $v (\text{km/h}) = v (\text{m/s}) \times 3.6$ — common conversion error in KTU papers.
- ✅ **Energy conversion:** $1 \text{ kWh} = 3.6 \times 10^6 \text{ J} = 3600 \text{ kJ}$.
- ✅ **Air density (sea level, 15°C):** $\rho = 1.225 \text{ kg/m}^3$.
- ✅ **$g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$** — gravitational constant used in all EV dynamics.
- ✅ **Five EV functional blocks:** Battery + Inverter + Motor + Reducer + Controls.
- ✅ **Bidirectional inverter** is the *key* differentiator that enables regenerative braking.
- ✅ **BMS** is the safety brain — never a "battery indicator" alone.
- ✅ **BS-VI / Euro 7** emission norms are the regulatory push for EV adoption.
- ✅ **Viva-favourite question:** "Why no gearbox in most BEVs?" — Answer: Electric motors deliver peak torque from 0 rpm, so a wide-ratio multi-speed box is unnecessary; a single fixed reduction is sufficient.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Introduction to ADAS (Automatic Driver Assist Systems)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEICT721 - AUTOMOBILE INSTRUMENTATION
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Introduction to ADAS (Automatic Driver Assist Systems)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **ADAS (Advanced Driver Assistance Systems)** is an integrated suite of electronic, sensor-based, and computational sub-systems embedded within a vehicle that actively perceive the surrounding driving environment, interpret dynamic traffic conditions, and either inform the driver or autonomously actuate vehicle controls (throttle, brake, steering) to enhance driving safety, comfort, and efficiency.

According to the **SAE International Standard J3016**, ADAS functions span **Level 0** (No Automation) through **Level 5** (Full Automation) of driving automation. Strictly speaking, ADAS refers to **SAE Levels 1 and 2**, where the human driver remains the legally responsible operator but is supplemented by intelligent assistance.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **The Co-Pilot Analogy** — Imagine a vigilant, multi-eyed co-pilot sitting beside you in the car. This co-pilot never blinks, never gets distracted by phone calls, and has eyes in the back of its head. It continuously scans the road ahead with **radar**, looks for lane markings with **cameras**, and measures distances to nearby objects with **ultrasonic sensors**. The moment it spots a hazard — a pedestrian stepping out, a car suddenly braking, or your vehicle drifting out of the lane — it either **alerts you with a beep, vibration, or visual warning**, or **takes over briefly** to brake/steer the car out of danger. That co-pilot is exactly what an ADAS represents in modern vehicles.

## 1.3 Key Standard Metrics

- **Time-to-Collision (TTC) Threshold:** **2.7 seconds** (industry standard for Forward Collision Warning activation).
- **Sensor Refresh Rate:** Typically **10 Hz to 50 Hz** for radar and camera fusion systems.
- **Lateral Detection Range:** Up to **250 m** for long-range automotive radar.
- **SAE Automation Levels:** **6 distinct levels** (0 through 5).
- **NCAP Safety Rating Threshold:** Euro NCAP 5-star requires at minimum **AEB** and **Lane Support Systems** as standard.

## 1.4 Historical Evolution

| Year | Milestone |
|------|-----------|
| **1958** | First cruise control system introduced by Chrysler (Imperial) |
| **1995** | First production Adaptive Cruise Control (ACC) — Mitsubishi Diamante (radar-based, Japan) |
| **2000** | Lane Departure Warning (LDW) commercialized by Mercedes-Benz |
| **2006** | Night Vision Assist with pedestrian detection — BMW 7 Series |
| **2009** | Volvo City Safety — first standard AEB system on low-speed vehicles |
| **2014** | Euro NCAP makes AEB critical for 5-star rating |
| **2016+** | Tesla Autopilot, GM Super Cruise — Level 2 automation mainstream |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Functional Architecture of an ADAS

Every ADAS, regardless of its specific feature set, follows a common four-stage processing pipeline:

### Stage 1 — Perception (Sensing Layer)
- **Purpose:** Collect raw data from the physical environment.
- **Sensors Used:** Radar, Lidar, Cameras, Ultrasonic, Inertial Measurement Unit (IMU), GPS.
- **Output:** Raw analog/digital signals (point clouds, image frames, range-velocity maps).

### Stage 2 — Sensor Fusion & Interpretation
- **Purpose:** Combine multi-sensor data to build a reliable environmental model.
- **Techniques:** Kalman Filtering, Bayesian Networks, Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs).
- **Output:** Object classification (vehicle, pedestrian, cyclist), trajectory prediction.

### Stage 3 — Decision / Planning
- **Purpose:** Determine the appropriate response based on the interpreted scenario.
- **Logic:** Rule-based systems (IF-THEN-ELSE), Finite State Machines, Path planning algorithms.
- **Output:** Control commands (brake pressure, steering torque, throttle position).

### Stage 4 — Actuation
- **Purpose:** Physically execute the decided action.
- **Actuators:** Electronic Stability Control (ESC), Electric Power Steering (EPS), Electronic Throttle Body, Brake-by-Wire.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** The reliability of an ADAS is not determined by the *best* sensor, but by the **fusion of complementary sensors**. Cameras excel at object classification but fail in fog; radar penetrates fog but has poor angular resolution. Lidar provides 3D geometry but is costly. **Sensor fusion is the architectural backbone of robust ADAS.**

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet
